entries.title: Developer Guide
entries.content:
Developer Guide

Welcome to the CrowdVision Developer Guide. Before diving into the codebase, it is crucial to understand what we are building and why the architecture is structured the way it is.

The primary goal of CrowdVision is to provide a robust, real-time crowd monitoring platform built on a digital twin paradigm. It serves facility managers by streaming live IoT sensor data directly into a 3D visualization of their buildings.

-----

🗺️ System Capabilities Overview

Below is a high-level mindmap of CrowdVision’s current and planned functional domains:

entries.title: User Stories
entries.content:
User Stories

CrowdVision was designed outside-in. Before any service or schema existed, we collected user stories and used them as the raw material of the DDD process described in Strategic Design: the Ubiquitous Language was mined from the nouns and verbs the stories kept repeating, and the way stories clustered around different vocabularies became the first sketch of the bounded contexts.

This page catalogues that backlog in its DDD shape. Stories are grouped by bounded context, written in each context’s dialect, and annotated with the model element that answers them. Stories that refused to fit inside a single context are collected at the end — they are the ones that shaped the context map. Where the backlog and the realized design pull in different directions, the difference is stated and justified rather than hidden.

How to read a story
-----
Stories follow the classic shape — As a persona, I want a capability, so that some value arrives — and each carries a note on where the domain model answers it. The vocabulary inside the stories is deliberately the Ubiquitous Language of the owning context: if a word in a story does not appear in that context’s language table, either the story or the language was considered wrong and one of them was reworked.

-----

From Stories to the Model

The path from backlog to strategic design was mechanical on purpose, so that the model could always be traced back to someone who wanted something:



The table below shows the trace for the load-bearing vocabulary. When a story says the words on the left, the model answers with the element on the right.

“my organization”“join with the right role”“the building”, “a room”“a reading”“capacity”, “maximum temperature”“a column on my dashboard”“my devices”“what I want to hear about”When a story says…The Domain aggregate, referenced everywhere by its name — the shared identifier.The Role invitation domain service, producing a Membership with a ranked Role.The Building aggregate root, Room entities, geometry value objects.The per-metric Signal aggregates — immutable, append-only.Bounds inside the BuildingThreshold aggregate, with per-room overrides.An allowed metric inside the BuildingPreference aggregate.DeliveryChannel aggregates, one per device.The AlertPreference aggregate, scoped per principal per domain.The model answers withIdentity & AccessIdentity & AccessBuilding ManagementTelemetry IngestionTelemetry IngestionTelemetry DistributionAlertingAlertingContext

-----

Personas

The personas mirror the ranked Role policy of the Identity & Access Context, plus one system actor: the sensor itself, which authenticates like any principal but whose stories live in Telemetry Ingestion.

Standard UserStaff MemberDomain AdministratorPlatform AdministratorSensorPersonastandard_customerbusiness_staffbusiness_adminadmindevice principalRole in the modelA visitor or student who checks the spaces they use — is the study room free, is the hall crowded.Front-line operations — security or facilities staff who watch the live view, respond to alerts, and keep room data current.A facility manager who owns one or more Domains: buildings, member onboarding, alerting policy.The operator of the whole multi-tenant installation.An IoT device or simulator that submits readings under long-lived credentials.Who they are

-----

Identity & Access

Model: Identity & Access Context — realised by auth-service.

IA-1IA-2IA-3IA-4IA-5IA-6IDAs a standard user, I want to create an account with my email and a credential and sign in, so that the platform can recognize me across organizations.As a domain administrator, I want to onboard a new member directly with the role I choose — including one more powerful than an ordinary member — so that the right people arrive with the right authority from their first sign-in.As an enterprise staff member, I want to sign in through my company’s identity provider, so that joining or leaving the company automatically governs my access.As a domain administrator, I want to split my organization into subdomains, so that campus and department structure is reflected in access control.As a member of several organizations, I want one sign-in to carry all my memberships, so that I can switch organizations without re-authenticating.As a domain administrator, I want my domain to be invisible to public discovery, so that only people I invite can find it and request access.StoryThe Account aggregate is the consistency boundary around a principal; credential material lives on the aggregate but is never part of what the context hands out.The Role invitation domain service grants a Membership carrying a ranked Role; because Role is ranked, authorization can later be expressed as “at least this powerful” rather than an enumeration of cases.The authStrategy policy switch on the Domain aggregate delegates verification to an external provider via SSOConfig; the invariant that strategy and configuration agree keeps a federated domain from also accepting self-service joins.Domain.subdomains models the organizational hierarchy; its depth was deliberately left open.The identity token issuance service embeds every Membership in the signed token — the Published Language all downstream contexts conform to.The visibleFromOutside flag on the Domain aggregate gates discovery; invitations remain the only door into a private domain.Where the model answers it

-----

Building Management

Model: Building Management Context — realised by twin-service.

BM-1BM-2BM-3BM-4BM-5IDAs a domain administrator, I want to upload a description of my building and have a digital twin built from it, so that the twin mirrors the real structure without developer intervention or database migrations.As a domain administrator of a shared structure, I want one building to be visible to several organizations, so that a co-managed structure is not duplicated per tenant.As a staff member, I want to rename a room and make certain rooms stand out from the rest, so that the twin reflects how the space is actually used today.As a standard user, I want to see only the buildings of organizations I belong to, so that tenants stay isolated from each other.As a standard user, I want rooms drawn at their true position and size in the 3D view, so that I recognize the space I am standing in.StoryA Building aggregate is created as a coherent whole: Rooms live inside its boundary, positioned by Coordinates and sized by Dimensions value objects; the system assigns the identity.Tenancy is a many-to-many association: a Building records the domain names (the shared identifier) it is scoped by.Rooms are entities reachable only through their Building: the edit flows through the aggregate root and the aggregate is saved as one coherent whole.The tenancy rule is evaluated entirely inside the context: memberships arrive in the identity token and the Building already records its domains — no call to Identity & Access at request time.Geometry is pure value-object data: position and dimensions are defined entirely by their values, and the twin renders straight from the model.Where the model answers it

Why no story here mentions temperature or occupancy
-----
Stories about live numbers are deliberately absent from this context. The Room model carries only structural data — readings belong to the Telemetry contexts. The one story that wants both at once is epic X-1 below, and it is cross-context on purpose.

-----

Telemetry Ingestion

Model: Telemetry Ingestion Context — realised by sensor-service.

TI-1TI-2TI-3TI-4TI-5TI-6IDAs a sensor, I want to submit a time-stamped temperature signal for a room without waiting for a reply, so that a constrained device can report and move on.As a staff member, I want a particular room’s capacity or temperature limit to differ from the building default, so that a server room or a lecture hall is judged by its own rules.As a domain administrator, I want exactly one authoritative set of bounds per building, so that there is never ambiguity about which limit applied when an alert fired.As a domain administrator, I want recorded readings to be incorruptible — a correction is a new reading, never an edit — so that a post-incident review can be trusted.As a platform developer, I want to introduce a new kind of reading (say, air quality) without coordinating a release of every consumer, so that the platform can grow sensor by sensor.As a domain administrator, I want occupancy and temperature aggregated over a chosen reading window — day, week or month, averaged, summed, min or max — so that I can plan capacity instead of only reacting to it.StoryThe submission is fire-and-forget. An accepted reading is validated against the model, normalized into a canonical Signal and published into the platform; an invalid reading is rejected at the door and never enters the system — the verdict guards the platform, not the device.BuildingThreshold owns the building-level Bounds; a RoomThreshold value object overrides them, and resolving the effective bound for a reading is domain logic.At most one threshold configuration exists per Building: the aggregate owns the complete picture of that building’s bounds.Signals are conceptually immutable, append-only aggregates; the model has no operation that rewrites history.Each metric is a small aggregate of its own, and the context describes its metrics — key, label, unit, fields — as a Published Language downstream contexts discover instead of hard-coding.The reading window is part of this context’s language; aggregation is served from the recorded signals.Where the model answers it

-----

Telemetry Distribution

Model: Telemetry Distribution Context — realised by contracts-service.

TD-1TD-2TD-3TD-4IDAs a staff member, I want to add or remove dashboard columns from a catalog of everything the platform can measure, so that the live view fits my operation instead of a default.As a staff member, I want my columns kept in exactly the order I arranged them, so that what matters most is always first on screen.As a standard user, I want live values to appear without refreshing, so that the dashboard always shows now.As a domain administrator, I want a freshly registered building to come with a sensible default set of columns, so that the first visit shows data, not configuration work.StoryThe Metric Catalog is assembled from each upstream context’s self-description; a chosen column becomes an allowed metric in the building’s Preference.Order is part of the model: the allowed metrics are an ordered collection, and reordering is a meaningful change rather than an incidental detail.The distribution rule: a reading reaches a building’s view only if that building’s Preference includes its metric. (The word “instant” is a quality, not a rule — see the differences table below.)Building Management supplies the default Preference at creation, acting as the customer in a Customer/Supplier relationship.Where the model answers it

-----

Alerting

Model: Alerting Context — realised by notification-service.

AL-1AL-2AL-3AL-4AL-5IDAs a staff member, I want to be told the moment a room crosses its bounds — in the app while I am watching, as a push on my devices when I am not — so that I can intervene before an incident escalates.As a staff member, I want to choose which kinds of alert I receive, separately for each organization, so that my attention is spent only where I asked for it.As a staff member, I want to enroll several devices and revoke one without losing my choices, so that replacing a phone is painless.As a staff member, I want devices I no longer use to stop being targeted automatically, so that my delivery stays healthy without housekeeping.As a domain administrator, I want alerts to reach only members of the building’s organizations who opted in, so that tenants never see each other’s incidents.StoryA breach becomes a Domain Event published into the real-time flow; Delivery Channels — one per device, secured by DeliveryKeys — carry the push leg to closed browsers.The AlertPreference aggregate — at most one per principal per domain — holds a per-AlertType subscription, and the type set is modelled to grow.Two aggregates by design: revoking a DeliveryChannel never touches the AlertPreference, and changing preferences never touches the devices.The pruning rule: a channel that becomes permanently undeliverable is removed from the model rather than kept around.Recipient selection asks Building Management which domains the building belongs to — translating the answer through an anti-corruption layer — and intersects them with opted-in preferences.Where the model answers it

-----

Cross-Context Epics

Three stories refused to stay inside one context. We kept them whole — as epics — because the tension they created is exactly what produced the context map. Each one is stated, decomposed, and followed by the difference between what the story asks and what the design does.

X-1 — One dialog, two models

> As a staff member, I want to edit a room’s name, appearance, capacity and maximum temperature in a single dialog, so that keeping the twin honest is one gesture, not a scavenger hunt.

The story reads as atomic, but it speaks two dialects. Name and appearance are structure — Building Management, story BM-3. Capacity and maximum temperature are judgement bounds — Telemetry Ingestion, story TI-2. We chose to keep the contexts separate and let the client compose the dialog from both, which means the two updates are not transactional: it is possible for one to succeed and the other to fail.

X-2 — Monitorable from the first minute

> As a domain administrator, I want a building I register to be immediately monitorable and displayable, so that there is no second setup step to forget.

The epic decomposes along the map: BM-1 creates the structure, then Building Management acts as the customer supplying a threshold baseline to Telemetry Ingestion and a default Preference to Telemetry Distribution (TD-4). The user’s mental model is one atomic act; the design makes downstream provisioning best-effort — if seeding a downstream context fails, the Building still comes into existence and the baseline is re-established later.

X-3 — Tenancy everywhere, instantly

> As a member of an organization, I want every screen — twin, dashboard, alerts — to show only my organizations’ data, with no noticeable delay, so that I can trust what I am looking at.

This is the story behind the platform’s most consequential decision. Every context must answer “may this caller see this?” — and the story’s “no noticeable delay” rules out asking a central authority on every request. So identity travels: the signed token carries all memberships (IA-5) as a Published Language, every context conforms to it and authorizes locally, and the domain name — the minimal shared identifier — is recorded by each context so the check needs only data it already holds.

entries.title: Functional Requirements
entries.content:
Functional Requirements

To effectively contribute to CrowdVision, developers must understand both what the system is expected to do (Functional) and how well it is expected to perform those tasks (Non-Functional).

-----

1. Real-Time Data Visualisation

- The Requirement: The system must stream high-frequency crowd headcount and temperature data from simulated or physical IoT sensors directly to the client interface. The 3D view and data tables must reflect this data instantly.
- Why this is required: The core value proposition of CrowdVision is immediate situational awareness. Facility managers must know exactly what is happening right now to dispatch security to an overcrowded room or adjust HVAC systems before a room becomes uncomfortably hot. Stale, polled data is insufficient for emergency responses.

2. Automated Alerting & Push Notifications

- The Requirement: The system must continuously evaluate live sensor data against pre-defined thresholds (e.g., maximum capacity or maxTemperature). Breaches must trigger broadcasts via WebSockets for active users and Web Push Notifications via VAPID for offline browsers.
- Why this is required: Administrators cannot stare at a dashboard 24/7. The system must act as an automated watchman, proactively pushing critical safety alerts to staff members’ devices so they can intervene only when necessary.

3. Digital Twin Lifecycle Management (CRUD)

- The Requirement: Administrators must be able to upload JSON schemas defining building structures (room 3D coordinates/dimensions) and edit room metadata (capacity, thresholds, names, colours) dynamically after deployment.
- Why this is required: Physical buildings evolve—rooms are repurposed, capacities are adjusted due to changing regulations (like social distancing), and layouts are modified. The software must provide tools to keep the digital twin perfectly synced with physical reality without requiring developer intervention or database migrations.

4. Multi-Tenancy & Domain Access Control

- The Requirement: A single backend deployment must serve multiple distinct organizations (“Domains”). Access to data is strictly governed by a user’s role-based memberships (e.g., Admin, Business Staff, Viewer) within those specific domains.
- Why this is required: Deploying a separate server infrastructure for every single client is expensive and difficult to maintain. Multi-tenancy allows efficient resource sharing. Furthermore, users like external contractors or regional managers often need access to multiple organizations seamlessly using a single account.

5. Future: Historical Analytics & AI Assistant

- The Requirement: The system is planned to aggregate historical data for charting, and introduce an LLM-powered assistant capable of answering natural-language queries about the building’s status.
- Why this is required: While real-time data is for operations, historical data is for planning (e.g., deciding if the university needs to build more study rooms). The AI Assistant lowers the learning curve, allowing non-technical staff to extract complex insights just by asking questions.

entries.title: Non Functional Requirements
entries.content:
Non Functional Requirements

1. Database-per-Service Architecture

- The Requirement: Every backend microservice (Auth, Twin, Notification) must operate its own dedicated MongoDB instance.
- Why this is required: Isolation prevents “noisy neighbor” problems. If an administrator runs a massive, complex query to fetch a detailed 3D building schema in the Twin service, it must absolutely not drain database resources and prevent new users from logging in via the Auth service.

2. Decoupled Event Messaging (Redis Pub/Sub)

- The Requirement: Service-to-service communication for real-time events must use Redis pub/sub as a message broker rather than direct HTTP polling.
- Why this is required: As the system grows, multiple services might need to react to a sensor event (e.g., the Socket server to update UI, the Push server to send mobile alerts, and the LLM service to log an anomaly). Redis decouples the producer from the consumers, allowing us to add new reacting services without slowing down the initial data ingestion.

3. Strict SSO Security (OIDC with PKCE)

- The Requirement: Enterprise Single Sign-On (SSO) must utilize PKCE (code_challenge_method: S256). The OAuth state parameter must be an opaque token, with verification context stored securely on the server.
- Why this is required: Security is paramount for enterprise deployments. Encoding data directly into the OAuth state parameter makes the system vulnerable to state-tampering and CSRF attacks. Enforcing server-side state verification guarantees that a malicious actor cannot hijack an authentication flow.

4. Stateless Authentication (JWT)

- The Requirement: User sessions must rely on JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) with a 3-hour expiry. IoT device tokens are granted a 5-year expiry.
- Why this is required: Verifying tokens using middleware cryptography eliminates the need to query the database on every single API request. This drastically reduces latency and database load. A 3-hour expiry ensures that if a token is compromised, the window of vulnerability is short.

5. High-Performance Client Architecture

- The Requirement: The Vue 3 frontend must be built around the performant behavior of making the less amount of API calls possible.
- Why this is required: The dashboard receives potentially hundreds of WebSocket updates per minute.

entries.title: Strategic Design
entries.content:
Strategic Design

CrowdVision is modelled with a Domain-Driven Design (DDD) mindset. We approached the system not as one monolithic model but as a set of collaborating models, each owned by a context and each speaking its own dialect of a shared Ubiquitous Language. This page captures the strategic design decisions taken up front — the subdomains, the bounded contexts, and the relationships between them. The tactical model (aggregates, entities, value objects, domain services) is described per context on the following pages. The user stories that seeded these decisions — and the places where backlog and design deliberately part ways — are catalogued in User Stories.

A pragmatic stance
-----
We treat DDD as a design compass, not a rulebook. The strategic boundaries below are the part of the design we were most confident in. The tactical model is applied where it earns its keep, and some boundaries were left intentionally soft because the product ideas were still maturing when this design was written. The per-context pages flag where a decision is provisional or expected to evolve.

-----

Domain and Subdomains

The overall domain is real-time monitoring of physical spaces through a digital twin. We classify the subdomains by how much competitive value they carry, which in turn drives how much modelling effort each is given.

Spatial Digital TwinTelemetry Ingestion & ThresholdsTelemetry DistributionIdentity & AccessAlertingKnowledge & AssistanceReal-Time TransportSubdomainCoreCoreCoreSupportingSupportingSupportingGenericTypetwin-servicesensor-servicecontracts-serviceauth-servicenotification-serviceagent-servicesocket-serviceRealised byThe 3D building model is the product’s differentiator and receives the richest model.Turning raw sensor signals into meaningful, bounded readings is central business logic.Per-building stream shaping is what makes the live view feel tailored.Multi-tenant access control is essential but not a differentiator.Push alerting supports the core experience without defining it.The assistant augments the product; it is explicitly experimental.A commodity relay with no domain model of its own.Rationale

-----

Bounded Contexts

A guiding decision is that each core and supporting subdomain becomes exactly one bounded context, and each context is meant to stand on its own — its own model, its own storage, its own boundary. We chose this alignment so that a model can never quietly leak into another context through shared data: a context boundary is also intended to be a deployment and storage boundary.



The Real-Time Transport context is omitted from the map: it is meant to carry messages between contexts but to hold no model and enforce no rules.

-----

Context Map

We describe the relationships between contexts with the standard DDD integration patterns. They tell a contributor who depends on whom and which side dictates the contract. The interactions are stated conceptually; concrete endpoints belong to the implementation.

Identity → all other contextsBuilding Management → Telemetry IngestionBuilding Management → Telemetry DistributionTelemetry Ingestion → Telemetry DistributionBuilding Management → AlertingBuilding Management → Knowledge & AssistanceUpstream → DownstreamPublished LanguageCustomer / SupplierCustomer / SupplierOpen Host Service + Published LanguageCustomer / Supplier (Anti-Corruption)Anti-Corruption LayerPatternThe signed identity token (memberships, roles, domains) is the published language every context conforms to. Downstream contexts are Conformists: they read the token but never reshape it.When a Building is created, Building Management supplies a threshold baseline. It is the customer driving the contract.Building Management supplies default display preferences and contributes the structural metrics it owns to the catalog.Telemetry Ingestion describes its metrics in a stable, self-describing form. Distribution gathers those descriptions, so new metrics appear downstream without a coordinated change.Alerting asks Building Management which domains a building belongs to, and translates the answer into its own terms.The assistant reaches live building data only through a thin read-only layer, keeping the Building model out of the assistant’s domain.Integration

-----

The Shared Identifier Convention

Across the platform, we decided that a Domain is referenced by its name, never by an internal database identifier. This is the closest thing the design has to a Shared Kernel, and it is deliberately minimal: a single, stable, human-readable string rather than shared code.



Because the domain name travels inside the identity token and is recorded by each context, the intent is that multi-tenant isolation can be enforced symmetrically — each context answers “may this caller see this data?” from data it already holds, without consulting another context.

-----

Tactical Patterns at a Glance

The building blocks below recur throughout the per-context pages. The table states what each means in our model — not how any one service ends up implementing it.

Aggregate RootEntityValue ObjectDomain ServiceDomain EventDDD Building BlockA top-level concept that owns a consistency boundary, e.g. Account, Building, BuildingThreshold.Something with identity that lives inside an aggregate, e.g. Room within Building.An immutable, identity-less descriptor, e.g. Coordinates, Dimensions, a per-type preference.Behaviour that does not belong to a single entity, e.g. issuing the identity token, or deciding whether a reading is acceptable.Something noteworthy that happened, broadcast for other contexts to react to, e.g. a new reading or an alert.How it appears in our model

Where the model is deliberately light
-----
Some aggregates are intentionally thin at this stage — they describe data and a few rules rather than rich behaviour. This was a conscious trade-off while the contexts were still settling, and the per-context pages note where we expect behaviour to grow onto the aggregate as the design firms up.

entries.title: Identity & Access Context
entries.content:
Identity & Access Context

Realised by: auth-service · Subdomain type: Supporting

This page captures the design-time model of the Identity & Access context: the language, the aggregates, and the rules we committed to before writing code. It deliberately stays at the conceptual level — concrete routes, storage, and wire formats are decided later and documented in the Services section.

The context answers three questions: who is this principal, which organizations do they belong to, and what may they do there. It is the upstream source of identity for the platform — it produces the identity token that every other context treats as a Published Language.

-----

Ubiquitous Language

AccountDomainMembershipRoleAuth StrategyTermA principal (human or system) that can authenticate.An organization or tenant. The unit of multi-tenancy.The link that places an Account inside a Domain with a specific Role.A ranked permission level used for authorization decisions.How a Domain verifies identity: managed internally, or delegated to an external identity provider.Meaning in this context

-----

Aggregates

We model two aggregate roots — Account and Domain. They are kept separate on purpose: an Account refers to a Domain only by its name (the shared identifier described in Strategic Design, so the two can evolve independently rather than through a hard reference.



Aggregate: Account

The consistency boundary around a principal and its memberships.

- We chose to keep memberships inside the Account aggregate rather than as a separate association. The intent is that resolving a principal also resolves everything needed to make an authorization decision, without consulting another aggregate.
- Credential material (the secret used to authenticate, and any second-factor seed) lives on the aggregate but is treated as sensitive: it is part of the model, not part of what the context hands out.
- Intended invariant: an Account holds at most one Membership per Domain. Joining a Domain a principal already belongs to updates the existing Membership rather than adding a second.

Aggregate: Domain

The consistency boundary around an organization and the rules under which principals join it.

- authStrategy is the central policy switch. It is expected to drive how a principal proves identity and which value objects a Domain carries.
- inviteSecrets and ssoConfig hold confidential material. The model treats them as write-mostly: they participate in the Domain’s behaviour but are not part of its public representation.
- subdomains expresses an organizational hierarchy. We left the depth of that hierarchy open at this stage rather than fixing it to a single level.
- Intended invariant: a Domain’s strategy and its configuration agree — an internally-managed Domain carries no external-provider configuration, and an externally-federated Domain does not accept self-service joins.

-----

The Role policy

Role is modelled as a ranked value object rather than a free-form label, so that authorization can be expressed as “at least this powerful” rather than an enumeration of cases. The comparison is the single rule the context exposes for permission checks:

	role_satisfies(held, required):
	    return rank(held) >= rank(required)
	ranking (high to low):
	    admin > business_admin > business_staff > standard_customer

How this rule is invoked at request time is an implementation concern, covered under Middleware.

-----

Domain Services

Two pieces of behaviour do not belong to a single entity, so we foresee them as domain services:

Identity token issuanceRole invitationServiceAssemble and sign the identity token, embedding the principal’s memberships so downstream contexts can authorize on their own.Derive and validate the short, time-bounded codes that grant a Role within a Domain.Responsibility

-----

Published Language: the identity token

The output of this context is a signed identity token whose contents — the principal, their memberships, roles, and domains — are the contract every other context conforms to. Designing this payload as a stable Published Language is what lets the other contexts authorize locally without calling back here.



The interaction above is intentionally generic: the exact endpoints, token format, and federation handshake are design decisions deferred to the implementation. What matters at the domain level is that authentication yields a self-contained statement of identity and authority.

entries.title: Building Management Context
entries.content:
Building Management Context

Realised by: twin-service · Subdomain type: Core

This page describes the design-time model of the Building Management context — the structural backbone of the platform that the live-data contexts decorate. Concrete APIs and storage are documented in the Services section.

The context models the physical reality being monitored: buildings, the rooms inside them, and the geometry that places those rooms in the 3D digital twin.

-----

Ubiquitous Language

BuildingRoomGeometryTermA monitored physical structure, scoped to one or more domains.A bounded space inside a Building with a capacity and a position.The position and dimensions that render a Room in the 3D twin.Meaning in this context

-----

The Building Aggregate

We model Building as the single aggregate root, with Room as an entity that lives inside it. A Room has no meaning on its own — it is always created and changed in the context of its Building — which is why we draw the aggregate boundary around the Building rather than promoting Room to a root of its own.



Aggregate Root: Building

- A Building is associated with one or more domains (by name, the shared identifier). We model tenancy as a many-to-many association so that a structure can be shared across organizations without duplication.
- Intended invariant: changes to a Room flow through its Building, so the aggregate is always saved as a coherent whole rather than as independently mutated rooms.

Entity: Room

- A Room is identified within its Building.
- We deliberately keep only structural data on the Room — name, geometry. Live readings such as temperature and occupancy are not part of this model; they belong to the Telemetry contexts. This separation is a core design decision: it keeps the twin’s structure stable while readings change continuously.

Value Objects: Coordinates and Dimensions

Geometry is modelled as immutable, identity-less value objects. Two rooms sharing the same coordinates are not “the same place” — position is defined entirely by its values, which is exactly the value-object contract.

-----

Multi-Tenancy as a Domain Rule

A guiding decision for this context is that it should enforce tenancy without depending on the Identity & Access context at request time. The principal’s memberships arrive with the request (in the identity token), and a Building already records the domains it belongs to, so the rule can be evaluated entirely within this context.



This is the intended shape of the rule; how the check is wired and how buildings are queried are implementation matters left to the service.

-----

Relationships to Other Contexts

This context is meant to act as an upstream supplier to three downstream contexts. The model assumes that, when a Building comes into existence, the downstream contexts are given the baseline they need.

Telemetry IngestionTelemetry DistributionKnowledge & AssistanceDownstream ContextA threshold baseline describing the Building’s rooms.A default set of display preferences, plus the structural metrics the Building itself owns.Read-only access to Building and Room data.What this context is expected to supplyCustomer / SupplierCustomer / SupplierAnti-Corruption LayerPattern

Design decision: provisioning is best-effort
-----
We intend the downstream provisioning to be best-effort rather than transactional: if seeding a downstream context does not succeed, creating the Building should still be allowed to complete, and the baseline can be re-established later. This deliberately softens the aggregate’s consistency boundary at the network edge, trading strict consistency for availability. It is a decision we expect to revisit if the downstream baselines become safety-critical.

entries.title: Telemetry Ingestion Context
entries.content:
Telemetry Ingestion Context

Realised by: sensor-service · Subdomain type: Core

This page describes the design-time model of the Telemetry Ingestion context. How the service is structured to accept and route readings at runtime — the registry/dispatch mechanism that lets new sensor types be added — is an architectural concern and is covered in Deep Dive into Microservices and the Sensor Service page, not here.

At the domain level, this context turns raw sensor input into validated, time-stamped readings, and holds the thresholds those readings are judged against.

-----

Ubiquitous Language

SignalMetricThresholdReading windowTermA single time-stamped reading from one room (people count, temperature, air quality).A category of signal, identified by a stable key.A bound a metric is expected to stay within, for a building or a room.The time range over which signals are aggregated for display.Meaning in this context

-----

Signals as Aggregates

We treat each metric as a small aggregate root of its own: an append-only reading that shares a common identity (which building, which room, when) plus its metric-specific values. Signals are conceptually immutable — a correction is a new reading, not an edit of an old one.



We chose one aggregate per metric rather than a single generic “reading” so that each metric can carry its own fields and grow its own rules without entangling the others. Whether the set of metrics is fixed or open-ended was left flexible at this stage — the model is meant to accommodate metrics we have not designed yet.

-----

The Threshold Aggregate

BuildingThreshold is the one mutable aggregate in this context: the consistency boundary around all of a Building’s configured bounds, with optional per-room overrides modelled as value objects.



- We keep at most one threshold configuration per Building, so the aggregate owns the complete picture of that Building’s bounds.
- Intended rule: a room-level bound, when present, takes precedence over the building-level default for that room. Resolving the effective bound for a given reading is domain logic.

-----

Validation as a Domain Rule

Accepting a reading is a domain decision, not just a transport check. The intent is that an incoming reading is judged against the model before it is accepted, and that the outcome is a structured verdict the caller can act on — not an exception.

	validate(reading):
	    confirm the reading identifies a building, a room, and a time
	    confirm the metric-specific values are present and well-formed
	    return ok, or the list of reasons it was rejected

Designing validation to return a verdict (rather than throw) is a deliberate choice so that a rejected reading can be reported clearly to its source.

The intended intake path is short: once a reading is validated, it is normalized into a canonical signal and published into the platform so it can flow on to the client; an invalid reading is rejected at the door and never enters the system.



-----

Published Language: the Metric description

For the Telemetry Distribution context to make sense of readings without bespoke coupling, this context is meant to describe its own metrics — their key, label, unit, and fields — as a small, stable contract.

	Metric:
	    key         -- stable identifier, e.g. "temperature"
	    label       -- human-readable name
	    unit        -- optional unit of measure
	    fields[]    -- the shape of a reading of this metric

Publishing a self-describing metric is what allows a new metric type to become visible downstream without a coordinated change — an application of the Open Host Service / Published Language idea introduced in Strategic Design.

entries.title: Telemetry Distribution Context
entries.content:
Telemetry Distribution Context

Realised by: contracts-service · Subdomain type: Core

This page describes the design-time model of the Telemetry Distribution context. The runtime mechanics — how filtering is made fast, how streams are carried to the browser — are architectural concerns documented in the Contracts Service and Architecture Overview pages.

At the domain level, this context decides which metrics each building’s live view should carry, and shapes the flow of readings accordingly.

-----

Ubiquitous Language

PreferenceAllowed metricMetric CatalogDistributionTermThe ordered set of metrics a Building has chosen to display.A single metric included in a Preference.The combined list of metrics offered by the upstream contexts.Forwarding a reading only to the Buildings whose Preference includes its metric.Meaning in this context

-----

The Building Preference Aggregate

The aggregate root is a Building’s Preference: which metrics it shows, in what order. Order is part of the model, because it is the order the display is meant to present.



- A Preference is keyed by its Building, so there is exactly one Preference per Building.
- The allowed metrics are an ordered collection; reordering is a meaningful change to the model, not an incidental detail.
- We left open, at design time, how durable a Preference must be and how quickly a change must take effect — those service-level qualities are decided in the implementation.

-----

The Metric Catalog

This context does not invent metrics — it assembles them. The design intent is that each upstream context describes its own metrics (see the Published Language in Telemetry Ingestion and the structural metrics owned by Building Management, and this context gathers those descriptions into one catalog the display can choose from.



Gathering rather than defining keeps this context a Conformist consumer of each upstream Published Language: a new metric upstream becomes selectable here without a coordinated change.

-----

Distribution as a Domain Rule

The central rule of this context is simple to state at the domain level: a reading should reach a Building’s live view only if that Building’s Preference includes the reading’s metric.

	distribute(reading):
	    for each building preference:
	        if reading.metric in preference.allowedMetrics:
	            deliver the reading to that building's view

The model says nothing about how readings arrive or are carried onward — that the rule must hold at high throughput is a quality we hand to the implementation.

-----

A note on transport

Carrying readings to the browser is, by design, not part of this context’s model. We classify real-time transport as a generic subdomain (see Strategic Design: it holds no aggregates and enforces no rules, so it is kept out of the domain model and treated as infrastructure.

entries.title: Alerting Context
entries.content:
Alerting Context

Realised by: notification-service · Subdomain type: Supporting

This page describes the design-time model of the Alerting context. Delivery mechanics and concrete APIs are documented in the Notification Service page.

The context owns everything about delivering alerts to people. Its defining design decision is to separate two concerns that are easy to conflate: where a principal can be reached, and what a principal wants to hear about.

-----

Ubiquitous Language

Delivery ChannelAlert PreferenceAlert TypeRecipientTermA single device or endpoint an alert can be pushed to.A principal’s per-domain choices about which kinds of alert to receive.A category of alert (for example, a temperature alert).A principal eligible to receive a given alert, within a domain.Meaning in this context

-----

Two Aggregates, Two Questions

We model the context as two separate aggregate roots so that the two concerns can change independently — a principal can revoke a device without losing their choices, and change their choices without touching their devices.



Aggregate: Delivery Channel — “where can I reach this principal?”

- Represents one place an alert can be delivered, together with the material needed to deliver to it securely.
- A principal may have many channels — one per device. This is intentional, so reaching a principal can fan out across their devices.
- Intended rule: a channel that becomes permanently undeliverable is pruned from the model rather than kept around.

Aggregate: Alert Preference — “what does this principal want?”

- Scoped to a principal within a domain (the shared identifier again), so choices are tenant-specific.
- Intended invariant: at most one Alert Preference per principal per domain.
- AlertType is modelled as an extensible set. We expect to start with a small number of types and add more, so the model is built to grow rather than fixed to today’s list.

-----

How Alerting Fits In

The context is downstream of two others and feeds the platform’s real-time flow. The interaction is drawn generically — the concrete trigger and delivery details are implementation decisions.



Consumes identity from Identity & AccessResolves a building’s domains from Building ManagementEmits alerts into the real-time flowRelationshipConformist (reads the identity token, never reshapes it)Customer / Supplier, translated into this context’s own termsDomain Event published to the platformPattern

Resolving a building’s domains from another context, rather than storing its own copy, keeps this context’s model focused on delivery and intent and leaves the structural truth where it belongs.

headings.text: Aggregate: Delivery Channel — “where can I reach this principal?”
Aggregate: Alert Preference — “what does this principal want?”

entries.title: Architecture Overview
entries.content:
Architecture Overview

CrowdVision is a distributed, event-driven microservices system. The frontend is fully separated from the backend, and the backend services are isolated from one another, so the platform can scale parts independently, tolerate partial failure, and enforce strict data boundaries.

This page gives the whole-system picture — the principles, the components, and the two deployment models. Inter-service communication and the real-time data path are covered in Communication & Data Flow; the cluster specifics in Deployment & Kubernetes.

-----

Architectural Principles

- One bounded context per service. Each service owns a single context from the Domain model and nothing else.
- Database per service. No service reads another service’s database; data crosses a boundary only through an API call or an event.
- Stateless services, stateful edges. Application services hold no session state — identity travels in a signed token — so any instance can serve any request. Durable state lives only in the databases and the broker.
- One gateway in front. All external traffic enters through a single reverse proxy that routes by URL prefix.
- Event-driven where it counts. High-frequency telemetry and alerts move over a publish/subscribe broker rather than synchronous calls, decoupling producers from consumers.
- Polyglot by need. Most services are Node.js/Express; the throughput-critical telemetry filter is Rust; the assistant is Python.

-----

Component Landscape

auth-servicetwin-servicesensor-servicecontracts-servicenotification-servicesocket-serviceagent-serviceclientGatewayBrokerDatastoresComponentIdentity & Access — accounts, domains, tokensBuilding Management — the spatial modelTelemetry Ingestion — readings & thresholdsTelemetry Distribution — per-building filteringAlerting — push deliveryReal-time transport to the browserAssistant — maintained separately; see its referenceThe single-page applicationSingle entry point, routes by URL prefixPublish/subscribe backbone for telemetry and alertsOne database per stateful serviceRoleNode.js / ExpressNode.js / ExpressNode.js / ExpressRust / AxumNode.js / ExpressNode.js / Socket.IOPythonVue 3 / ViteCaddy (compose) / nginx Ingress (k8s)RedisMongoDB (per service), PostgreSQL (agent)Stack

The agent-service runs as part of the system but sits outside this guide’s microservice set; it appears here for completeness only.

-----

Deployment Model: Docker Compose (Local Development)

All incoming external traffic enters the system through a single Caddy reverse-proxy gateway exposed on port 80. Caddy strips the service prefixes from the URLs and proxies the requests to the appropriate target containers on the internal Docker bridge network.



Network Segregation & Database Isolation

Each microservice is attached to two networks:

- The shared gateway-net (so Caddy can reach them).
- A completely private database network (e.g., auth-net, twin-net, notification-net, sensor-net).

This physically enforces the database-per-service pattern. The Twin Service cannot reach the Auth Database, so cross-domain data mutations are impossible by construction.

-----

Deployment Model: Kubernetes (Production / Staging)

In Kubernetes mode, Caddy is replaced by an nginx Ingress Controller. The concept is identical — a single entry point routing by URL path — but the implementation is declarative YAML rather than a Caddyfile.



A key difference in the Kubernetes model is how the Sensor Simulator integrates. Because the simulator is an external Docker container (not a cluster workload), it reaches the cluster through the load balancer’s public port, just like a browser. See the External Sensor Simulator page. The cluster internals — StatefulSets, Secrets, Ingress rules — are detailed in Deployment & Kubernetes.

-----

Routing Table

The routing table is identical across both deployment models. Only the component enforcing it differs (Caddy vs nginx Ingress).

/auth//twin//notification//sensor//agent//socket.io//External Path Prefixauth-servicetwin-servicenotification-servicesensor-serviceagent-servicesocket-serviceclientInternal Target ServiceAccount creation, login, SSO, token verification.Building models, room CRUD, spatial data.Processing sensor events, triggering Web Push alerts.Ingesting time-series sensor readings (people count, temperature).Tool-calling assistant for live building data and documentation search.Maintaining persistent WebSocket connections to the frontend.Serves the Vue 3 SPA. Catch-all, must be defined last.Purpose

Dev Mode
-----
Developer Mode Only: when running just dev, Caddy also exposes auth-db-express and twin-db-express at /auth-db-gui/* and /twin-db-gui/*. These are not present in Kubernetes deployments for security reasons.

-----

Real-Time Data Flow

The platform’s defining behaviour — a physical event reaching a browser in real time — spans several services and the broker. That end-to-end path, along with the synchronous and asynchronous communication patterns that connect the services, is documented in Communication & Data Flow.

entries.title: Communication & Data Flow
entries.content:
Communication & Data Flow

CrowdVision uses two communication styles, chosen deliberately per interaction:

- Synchronous (request/response) over HTTP through the gateway — for user-driven actions and for the few service-to-service calls that need an immediate answer.
- Asynchronous (publish/subscribe) over Redis — for the high-frequency telemetry and alert flows, where producers must not be coupled to, or blocked by, consumers.

This page maps both, and then walks the two end-to-end real-time paths.

-----

Synchronous Communication

All external requests enter through the single gateway, which routes by URL prefix (see the routing table in the Overview. A handful of service-to-service calls also happen over HTTP on the internal network:

twin-servicetwin-servicesensor-servicenotification-servicecontracts-serviceCallersensor-servicecontracts-servicenotification-servicetwin-servicesensor-service / twin-serviceCalleeOn building creation/update, push a threshold baseline for the building’s rooms.Seed a new building’s default display preferences.Forward a temperature reading that may warrant an alert.Resolve which domains a building belongs to before selecting recipients.Discover each service’s self-describing metric catalog.Purpose

These calls are intentionally few. The design keeps the request path short and avoids synchronous chains on the hot path; anything high-frequency goes over the broker instead.

Identity travels with the request
-----
There is no central session store. auth-service mints a signed identity token; every other service verifies it locally and reads the principal’s memberships, roles, and domains from it. No service calls auth-service to authorize a request.

-----

Asynchronous Communication

Redis is the publish/subscribe backbone. Three channels carry the event flow:

telemetry:rawtelemetry:filtered:{buildingId}notificationsChannelsensor-servicecontracts-servicenotification-servicePublishercontracts-servicesocket-servicesocket-serviceSubscriberEvery accepted reading, as a normalized event.Readings a given building has opted to display.Alert events for delivery to the browser.Carries

Publishers never know who (if anyone) is listening, and the broker absorbs bursts, so a slow or restarting consumer cannot back-pressure a sensor.

-----

Path 1: Live Telemetry to the Browser

The defining flow: a reading becomes a live update on the dashboard, shaped per building.



contracts-service is the shaping stage: it consults each building’s display preferences and forwards a reading only to the buildings that asked for that metric. socket-service then delivers it to exactly the clients watching that building (see Socket Service.

-----

Path 2: Threshold Alert to the User

When a reading crosses a configured bound, the alert path runs in parallel to the telemetry path.



The alert path is owned by notification-service: it suppresses repeats with a short cooldown, resolves recipients via twin-service, and fans out over two channels — an in-app event through the broker and socket, and a Web Push to offline devices.

-----

Why Two Styles

User actions (login, CRUD)Provisioning between servicesTelemetry & alertsConcernSynchronousSynchronous (best-effort)AsynchronousStyleThe caller needs an immediate, ordered result.Simple to reason about; failures are tolerated and re-tried later.High volume, many consumers, and producers that must never block.Reason

headings.text: Path 1: Live Telemetry to the Browser
Path 2: Threshold Alert to the User

entries.title: Deployment & Kubernetes
entries.content:
Deployment & Kubernetes

This page covers how CrowdVision is deployed on Kubernetes: how the cluster is structured, what each manifest type does, how the nginx Ingress works, and how data is persisted across pod restarts. For the high-level deployment models and the gateway, see the Architecture Overview.

-----

🗂️ Manifest Structure

All Kubernetes configuration lives in the k8s/ directory:

	k8s/
	├── namespace.yml              # The crowdvision namespace
	├── ingress.yml                # nginx Ingress routing rules (2 objects)
	├── configmaps/
	│   └── common.yml             # Non-sensitive shared environment variables
	├── secrets/                   # GITIGNORED — sensitive credentials
	│   ├── authentication.yml
	│   ├── twin.yml
	│   ├── notification.yml
	│   └── agent.yml
	├── stateful/                  # Databases and stateful services
	│   ├── auth-db.yml
	│   ├── twin-db.yml
	│   ├── notification-db.yml
	│   ├── sensor-db.yml
	│   └── redis.yml
	└── deployments/               # Stateless application services
	    ├── auth-service.yml
	    ├── twin-service.yml
	    ├── notification-service.yml
	    ├── sensor-service.yml
	    ├── socket-service.yml
	    ├── agent-service.yml
	    └── client.yml

-----

🔑 ConfigMaps and Secrets

Kubernetes separates configuration into two object types based on sensitivity.

ConfigMap (k8s/configmaps/common.yml) — stores non-sensitive, shared configuration injected as environment variables into all pods that reference it:

	data:
	  REDIS_URL: "redis://redis:6379"
	  TWIN_SERVICE_URL: "http://twin-service:3000"
	  AUTH_SERVICE_URL: "http://auth-service:3000"
	  CLIENT_URL: "http://localhost"
	  PORT: "3000"

Secrets — store sensitive values (database URIs, JWT keys, API keys). The k8s/secrets/ directory is intentionally empty and gitignored. Secrets are created directly on the cluster from your local .env file using just k8s-secrets — they never touch the git repository. Kubernetes base64-encodes them automatically before storing in etcd.

Warning
-----
stringData is not encryption — it is base64 encoding. Real security comes from RBAC controlling who can kubectl get secret. For production, consider integrating an external secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) that injects values at runtime.

-----

🗄️ StatefulSets vs Deployments

CrowdVision uses two different Kubernetes workload types depending on whether the service needs persistent identity.

Deployments are used for all stateless application services (auth-service, twin-service, sensor-service, client, etc.). Pods in a Deployment are interchangeable — if one dies, the controller creates an identical replacement anywhere in the cluster. This is correct for services that store no local state.

StatefulSets are used for all databases (MongoDB × 4) and Redis. StatefulSets give each pod:

- A stable, predictable name — auth-db-0, auth-db-1 (never random hashes).
- A dedicated PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) — the storage is bound to auth-db-0 specifically. If the pod is rescheduled to a different node, Kubernetes remounts the same volume there.
- A headless Service (clusterIP: None) — enables direct per-pod DNS (auth-db-0.auth-db.crowdvision.svc.cluster.local).



-----

🌐 Ingress Configuration

The Ingress resource is split into two objects in k8s/ingress.yml because they require different annotation sets.

Ingress 1: API services (with URL rewriting)

All API services use a regex path with a capture group and the rewrite-target: /$2 annotation. This strips the service prefix before forwarding — so a request to /auth/login reaches auth-service as /login, not /auth/login.

	annotations:
	  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/use-regex: "true"
	  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /$2
	# Path pattern — two capture groups:
	path: /auth(/|$)(.*)
	# Group 1: (/|$)  — matches the slash or end of path
	# Group 2: (.*)   — captures everything after /auth/
	# $2 in rewrite-target = the captured remainder → forwarded as /login

Ingress 2: WebSocket and client (no rewriting)

The second Ingress object handles the Vue client and Socket.IO. No URL rewriting is needed — the client serves from / and Socket.IO expects to receive requests at /socket.io/. This object also sets sticky session cookies so that Socket.IO polling handshakes are consistently routed to the same backend pod.

	annotations:
	  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-read-timeout: "3600"
	  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-send-timeout: "3600"
	  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/affinity: "cookie"
	  nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/session-cookie-name: "route"

Note
-----
The / catch-all rule for the Vue client uses the path /()(.*) — an empty first capture group followed by the full path as group 2. This ensures rewrite-target: /$2 forwards /assets/bundle.js correctly rather than rewriting all asset requests to /.

-----

🚀 Deployment Startup Order

Services have dependencies — auth-service must not start before auth-db is accepting connections. CrowdVision uses initContainers in each Deployment to enforce this ordering without any cluster-level dependency management:

	initContainers:
	  - name: wait-for-db
	    image: busybox:1.36
	    command:
	      - sh
	      - -c
	      - until nc -z auth-db 27017; do echo "waiting..."; sleep 2; done

The initContainer runs before the main container. It polls auth-db:27017 every 2 seconds using netcat. Only when the TCP connection succeeds does Kubernetes start the main auth-service container. The pod shows Init:0/1 in kubectl get pods during this wait.

-----

🔄 Rolling Updates & Rollbacks

Every application Deployment uses imagePullPolicy: Always in VPS production — every rollout restart pulls the freshest :latest image from GHCR. When you run kubectl rollout restart deployment/auth-service, Kubernetes creates a new pod with the fresh image, waits for its readiness probe to pass, then terminates the old pod. If the new pod never becomes Ready, the old pod continues serving traffic indefinitely — your service never fully goes down.

Note
-----
Local k3d development: Set imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent instead of Always. When you load images with just k8s-load, Kubernetes must use the locally-imported image — Always would cause it to attempt a GHCR pull and either fail (if unauthenticated) or silently use a stale remote image.

	# Roll out a single service
	kubectl rollout restart deployment/auth-service -n crowdvision
	# Watch the transition
	kubectl rollout status deployment/auth-service -n crowdvision
	# Something broke — instant rollback to the previous version
	kubectl rollout undo deployment/auth-service -n crowdvision

-----

🔍 Debugging Reference

	    # See why a pod is not Ready
	    kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n crowdvision
	    # Look at the Events section at the bottom — shows OOMKilled, probe failures, etc.
	    # View live logs
	    kubectl logs deployment/auth-service -n crowdvision
	    # View logs from the previous crashed container
	    kubectl logs <pod-name> -n crowdvision --previous
	    # View initContainer logs specifically
	    kubectl logs <pod-name> -n crowdvision -c wait-for-db
	    # Open a shell inside a running pod
	    kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n crowdvision -- sh
	    # Decode a secret value to verify it stored correctly
	    kubectl get secret authentication-service-secret -n crowdvision \
	      -o jsonpath='{.data.JWT_SECRET}' | base64 --decode
	    # Recent cluster events sorted by time
	    kubectl get events -n crowdvision --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -20
	    # Verify which image is actually running in a pod (useful when debugging stale image issues):
	    kubectl get pod <pod-name> -n crowdvision -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].image}'
	    # After k3d cluster restart, if kubectl fails with "connection refused":
	    docker ps --filter name=k3d-crowdvision-serverlb
	    # Note the host port mapped to 6443 in the PORTS column, then:
	    kubectl config set-cluster k3d-crowdvision --server=https://127.0.0.1:<PORT>

entries.title: Service Anatomy
entries.content:
Service Anatomy

At the macro level CrowdVision is a distributed system; at the micro level, most services are internally a small, layered, monolithic application. Standardising that internal shape means a developer can move between the Auth, Twin, Notification, and Sensor services and immediately know where the routing, the business logic, and the data access live.

This page describes the shared anatomy and the deliberate exceptions to it. Each service then has its own architecture page (linked below) for its structure and key decisions; the code-level walkthrough and API for each live in the Implementation Reference section.

-----

The Shared Layered Architecture

The REST services follow a strict four-tier internal layering. Data flows in one direction: a request enters at the Router, passes to the Controller, is processed by the Service, and reaches the database through the Model.



Layer Responsibilities

- Router — the entry point. Declares HTTP methods and paths, attaches middleware (such as authorization), and delegates to a Controller.
- Controller — HTTP concerns only. Reads the body, params, and headers, calls the Service, and shapes the JSON response or error. It contains no business logic.
- Service — the heart of the service. All business logic, threshold evaluation, and orchestration lives here.
- Model — the schemas and data access. It maps the aggregates from the Domain model onto storage and validates data.

-----

Deliberate Exceptions

Not every service fits the four-tier mould — and that is by design.

sensor-servicecontracts-servicesocket-serviceServiceLayered, but the Controllers are generic: a microkernel resolves a per-metric module (Strategy) at request time, so new sensor types plug in without touching the core. It also splits a fast validation path from a slow persistence path.Written in Rust/Axum, not Node. It pairs an HTTP API with a background task that filters the telemetry stream, and keeps its hot state in memory, backed by its own database for durability.A single flat gateway with no Controller/Service/Model and no database — it only bridges the broker to WebSocket clients.Why it differs

-----

Cross-Cutting Concerns

Two concerns are factored out of every Express service into middleware rather than repeated in each controller:

- Authorization. A middleware verifies the identity token and evaluates the principal’s role for the requested context. See Auth Service: Middleware.
- Error handling. Controllers and services throw typed errors; a single middleware at the bottom of the stack formats them into a consistent JSON shape. Also covered in Auth Service: Middleware.

-----

The Services

Each service realises exactly one bounded context. Its architecture page covers internal structure and key decisions; the matching Implementation Reference page covers the code-level flows and API.

auth-servicetwin-servicesensor-servicecontracts-servicenotification-servicesocket-serviceServiceIdentity & AccessBuilding ManagementTelemetry IngestionTelemetry DistributionAlertingReal-time transport (generic)Bounded ContextNode.js / ExpressNode.js / ExpressNode.js / ExpressRust / AxumNode.js / ExpressNode.js / Socket.IOStackAuthentication ServiceDigital Twin ServiceSensor ServiceContracts ServiceNotification ServiceSocket ServicePage

entries.title: Authentication Service Architecture
entries.content:
Authentication Service Architecture

Bounded context: Identity & Access · Stack: Node.js / Express · Code-level walkthrough: Authentication Service

The auth-service realises the Identity & Access context. Architecturally it is the platform’s trust anchor: it authenticates principals and issues the signed identity token that every other service verifies on its own.

-----

Architectural Style

A layered Express service (see Service Anatomy built around a stateless authorization model. There is no server-side session — all the authority a request needs is carried in a signed token — so any instance can serve any request and no other service has to call back here to authorize.

-----

Internal Structure

The HTTP surface is split across several controllers by concern, with the heavier logic isolated in dedicated services.



-----

Key Architectural Decisions

- Stateless JWT issuance. Memberships are embedded in the token at sign-in, so authorization everywhere else is a local check rather than a call back to this service.
- Controllers split by concern (authentication, domain, administration) to keep each cohesive and small.
- Specialised domain services. Token handling and the TOTP invite-code logic are kept out of the controllers so they can be reasoned about and tested in isolation.
- Federation as account-linking. External SSO augments an existing account rather than creating one — a deliberate limit on what federation is allowed to do.
- Cross-cutting concerns in middleware. Authorization and error shaping are factored into middleware instead of being repeated per controller (see Auth Service: Middleware.

-----

Integration

- Owns auth-db; no other service reads it.
- Produces the identity token consumed platform-wide — see Communication & Data Flow.
- Has no synchronous dependency on any other service.

For the login, SSO, and TOTP flows, the code-level decisions, and the API, see the Authentication Service internals page.

entries.title: Digital Twin Service Architecture
entries.content:
Digital Twin Service Architecture

Bounded context: Building Management · Stack: Node.js / Express · Code-level walkthrough: Digital Twin Service

The twin-service realises the Building Management context. It is the spatial source of truth — the structural model of buildings and rooms that the live-data services decorate.

-----

Architectural Style

A layered Express service that runs independently of the auth-service. It enforces multi-tenant isolation purely from the identity token presented with each request, never by querying another service’s data.

-----

Internal Structure



-----

Key Architectural Decisions

- Server-owned identifiers. Building identities are generated by the service, not accepted from the client, so the public identifier is decoupled from any client- or storage-specific id.
- Tenancy from the token. A building records the domains it belongs to; access is granted by matching those against the principal’s memberships in the identity token — no call to the auth-service.
- Best-effort downstream provisioning. When a building is created, the service seeds the dependent contexts (a threshold baseline for ingestion, default display preferences for distribution). This is intentionally non-transactional: a failed seed does not fail the building creation (see the design note in Building Management.
- Structure treated as static. Geometry and topology are modelled as stable; only metadata evolves. This keeps the spatial model dependable while live readings churn elsewhere.

-----

Integration

- Owns twin-db; no other service reads it.
- Calls sensor-service and contracts-service to provision a new building — see Communication & Data Flow.
- Exposes read-only building data to the assistant through an anti-corruption layer.

For the registration flow, name-normalisation rules, synchronisation details, and the API, see the Digital Twin Service internals page.

entries.title: Notification Service Architecture
entries.content:
Notification Service Architecture

Bounded context: Alerting · Stack: Node.js / Express · Code-level walkthrough: Notification Service

The notification-service realises the Alerting context. Architecturally it is a rule engine and dispatcher: it decides whether an event warrants an alert and fans it out to the right people across more than one channel.

-----

Architectural Style

A layered Express service organised around two responsibilities that map directly onto the context’s two aggregates: managing where a principal can be reached, and what they have chosen to receive.

-----

Internal Structure



-----

Key Architectural Decisions

- Two responsibilities, two models. Delivery channels and alert preferences are kept apart so a device can be revoked without losing choices, and choices can change without re-registering devices.
- Dual-channel delivery. An alert is dispatched both as an in-app event (through the broker, to connected clients) and as a push to offline devices, so reachability does not depend on the app being open.
- Repeat suppression. Because readings arrive continuously, the service is designed to debounce repeated alerts for the same condition rather than notify on every reading.
- Recipient resolution by collaboration. The service asks the twin-service which domains a building belongs to and translates that into recipients, rather than keeping its own copy of building structure (an anti-corruption boundary).
- Self-pruning channels. A delivery channel that the provider reports as permanently dead is removed automatically, keeping the model clean without a scheduled job.

-----

Integration

- Owns notification-db; no other service reads it.
- Receives temperature events from sensor-service; resolves domains from twin-service; publishes alert events to the broker — see Communication & Data Flow.

For the throttle mechanics, VAPID delivery, the cleanup behaviour, and the API, see the Notification Service internals page.

entries.title: Sensor Service Architecture
entries.content:
Sensor Service Architecture

Bounded context: Telemetry Ingestion · Stack: Node.js / Express · Code-level walkthrough: Sensor Service

The sensor-service realises the Telemetry Ingestion context. It is the platform’s ingestion gateway, and its architecture is shaped by two pressures: accepting readings from constrained hardware without making it wait, and absorbing new sensor types without rewriting the core.

-----

Architectural Style

A layered Express service that departs from the plain four-tier shape in two deliberate ways: a modular microkernel with Strategy modules, and a fast-path / slow-path execution model.

-----

Internal Structure

Generic controllers hand off to a kernel that resolves the module owning the incoming metric; each module owns its validation, persistence, and side effects.



-----

Key Architectural Decisions

- Microkernel for extensibility. The core knows nothing about specific sensors; a new metric is a new module registered at startup. The system is open for extension and closed for modification.
- Strategy per metric. Each module owns its own validation and persistence behind a common contract, so metric-specific logic never leaks into the controllers or the kernel.
- Fast path vs slow path. Validation is synchronous and answered immediately; persistence and downstream publishing happen after the response is released, so a constrained sensor is never held open behind a database write.
- Validation as a gate. A reading is judged against the model before it is accepted, returning a structured verdict rather than throwing — bad data is rejected at the edge.
- Publish, do not couple. Accepted readings are published to the broker for any interested consumer; the temperature path additionally notifies the alerting context.

-----

Integration

- Owns sensor-db; no other service reads it.
- Publishes readings to the broker, forwards temperature events to notification-service, receives a threshold baseline from twin-service, and exposes a self-describing metric catalog to contracts-service — see Communication & Data Flow.

For the request lifecycle, the module base-class template, the extension guide, and the API, see the Sensor Service internals page.

entries.title: Contracts Service Architecture
entries.content:
Contracts Service Architecture

Bounded context: Telemetry Distribution · Stack: Rust / Axum · Code-level walkthrough: Contracts Service

The contracts-service realises the Telemetry Distribution context. It sits between the raw reading stream and the browser feed, shaping each building’s live view. It is the one service written in Rust, chosen for throughput on a hot path that inspects every reading.

-----

Architectural Style

Not a layered REST service. It runs two concurrent components in one process: an HTTP API for managing preferences, and a background stream processor that filters telemetry. Its frequently-read state is kept in memory, with a database behind it for durability.

-----

Internal Structure



-----

Key Architectural Decisions

- Rust on the hot path. The filter inspects a high-frequency stream; the language choice is an architectural decision to keep that path cheap and predictable.
- In-memory projection. Preferences are read on every reading but changed rarely, so they are held in a concurrent in-memory structure for lock-free reads; the database is touched only on startup (hydration) and on change (cold path).
- Filter by preference, fan out per building. A reading is forwarded only to the buildings whose preference includes its metric; one reading can fan out to many building feeds.
- Gather, do not define, the catalog. The service discovers each upstream service’s self-describing metric catalog and aggregates it, so new metrics appear without any change here — an Open Host Service consuming each upstream’s Published Language.

-----

Integration

- Subscribes to the raw-reading channel and publishes per-building filtered feeds on the broker — see Communication & Data Flow.
- Discovers metric catalogs from sensor-service and twin-service; receives default preferences seeded by twin-service.

For the concurrency model, the tunnel internals, persistence status, and the API, see the Contracts Service internals page.

entries.title: Socket Service Architecture
entries.content:
Socket Service Architecture

Bounded context: real-time transport (generic) · Stack: Node.js / Socket.IO · Code-level walkthrough: Socket Service

The socket-service is the bridge between the broker and the browser. It carries filtered telemetry and alerts to connected clients over WebSockets. It is classified as a generic subdomain (see Telemetry Distribution: pure transport, with no domain model and no database.

-----

Architectural Style

Deliberately the simplest service in the system: a single flat gateway with no Controller/Service/Model layering and no persistence. Its only job is to move messages from the broker to the right sockets, and to track which clients care about which building.

-----

Internal Structure



-----

Key Architectural Decisions

- Pure transport, no model. Treating real-time delivery as a generic subdomain keeps all domain logic out of it and concentrates modelling effort on the core services.
- Room-scoped telemetry, broadcast alerts. Clients receive readings only for buildings they have joined, so each connection’s stream stays minimal; alerts, which anyone may need, are broadcast.
- Stateless relay. The service holds no durable state, so an instance can be replaced freely.
- Scaling is an explicit concern. Running more than one instance requires sticky sessions for the handshake and a broker-backed adapter to deliver across instances — a known extension point rather than a current capability.

-----

Integration

- Subscribes to the alert channel and the per-building filtered feeds, and emits them to clients — see Communication & Data Flow.
- Holds no database and calls no other service synchronously.

For the channel-to-event mapping, the client subscription protocol, and scaling notes, see the Socket Service internals page.

entries.title: Authentication Service
entries.content:
Authentication Service

Implementation reference for the auth-service. For its architectural role and design rationale, see Authentication Service Architecture; for the domain model, see Identity & Access.

The service is a Node.js / Express application in TypeScript. It manages accounts, domains (tenants), role assignment, and federated sign-in, and it issues the JSON Web Token that the rest of the platform trusts.

-----

Source Layout

src/index.tssrc/router.tssrc/controller/src/services/src/models/src/middlewares/src/config/PathExpress bootstrap: JSON body capture (with raw-body buffer for HMAC), cookie parsing, router mount, error and metrics middleware, Mongo connection.Route table; binds paths to controllers and attaches authentication/authorization middleware.HTTP adapters: authentication.ts, domain.ts, administration.ts, status.ts.Business logic: authentication.ts, domain.ts, token.ts, totp.ts.Mongoose schemas and shared types: account.ts, domain.ts, role.ts, token.ts, error.ts.authentication.ts (auth + HMAC guards), error.ts (global handler), metrics.ts (Prometheus).config.ts (environment accessors), db.ts (Mongo), registry.ts (Prometheus registry).Responsibility

-----

Configuration

All runtime configuration is read through config/config.ts.

JWT_SECRETVITE_SERVER_URLCLIENT_URLINTERNAL_ADMIN_SECRETNODE_ENVVariable—http://localhost:3000http://localhost:5173——DefaultHMAC secret used to sign and verify every token. Must be identical across all services.Public base URL of the gateway; used to build the OIDC redirect URI and to call sibling services.Frontend origin; used as the post-SSO redirect target.Shared secret for the HMAC-signed internal provisioning endpoint.When production, the auth cookie is marked Secure (HTTPS only).Purpose

The session cookie is fixed: name authentication_token, httpOnly, sameSite=strict, maxAge 3 hours.

-----

Data Model

The aggregates are described conceptually in Identity & Access; this section records their persistence-level detail.

Account

	interface IAccount {
	  name: string;        // unique
	  email: string;       // unique
	  password: string;    // bcrypt hash, cost factor 10
	  memberships: IDomainMembership[]; // embedded subdocuments
	  totpSecret?: string;
	}

memberships is embedded, so a single findOne returns everything needed to mint a token.

Domain

	interface IDomain {
	  name: string;                     // unique tenant identifier (used everywhere by value)
