	  subdomains: ObjectId[];           // references to other Domain documents
	  authStrategy: "internal" | "oidc";
	  totpSecrets: IDomainTOTPSecrets;  // select:false — excluded from default queries
	  ssoConfig?: ISSOConfig;           // clientSecret is select:false
	  isVisibleFromOutside: boolean;    // default false
	}

- totpSecrets and ssoConfig.clientSecret carry select: false; they are returned only when a query explicitly re-selects them (.select("+totpSecrets"), .select("+ssoConfig.clientSecret")).
- IDomainMembership is { domainName, role, externalId? } and is the value object embedded in Account.memberships.

Role

role.ts defines a ranked enumeration and the single comparison used for all authorization:

	export const ROLE_WEIGHTS = {
	  admin: 100, business_admin: 80, business_staff: 60, standard_customer: 10,
	} as const;
	export const hasRequiredRole = (held: Role, required: Role): boolean =>
	  (ROLE_WEIGHTS[held] ?? 0) >= (ROLE_WEIGHTS[required] ?? Infinity);

-----

HTTP API

POST /registerPOST /loginPOST /logoutGET /mePOST /business/registerGET /domainsPOST /domainsGET /domains/:accountNamePOST /domains/:accountName/subscribeDELETE /domains/:accountName/unsubscribeGET /subdomains/:domainNamePOST /subdomains/:domainNameGET /auth/sso/login/:domainNameGET /auth/sso/callbackGET /domains/:domainName/totp/qr/:accountNameGET /health/, GET /metrics/Method · PathPublicPublicPublicCookie/JWTHMACJWTJWT + business_adminJWTJWTJWTJWTJWT + business_adminJWTPublicJWT + business_adminPublicAuthCreate an account; optional otp immediately claims a role. Sets the auth cookie. 201.Verify credentials, set the auth cookie. 200.Clear the auth cookie. 200.Return the authenticated accountName.Internal provisioning of an enterprise account; guarded by requireHmacSignature.List publicly visible domains.Create a domain (see side effects below). 201.Return the account’s memberships.Join an internal domain as standard_customer.Leave a domain.Resolve the full subdomain tree (recursive).Create a domain and attach it as a subdomain.Begin the OIDC flow; returns { redirectUrl }.OIDC redirect target; links membership and redirects to the client.Return TOTP provisioning URIs for the roles the caller may grant.Liveness and Prometheus metrics.Description

-----

Token Service

services/token.ts mints two token shapes, both signed with JWT_SECRET.

Standard (user)DeviceTokenaccountId, accountName, accountMembershipsdomainName, buildingId, deviceName, deviceType, isBuildingSpecificClaims3 hours1825 daysLifetime

generateStandardToken re-reads the account before signing, so the embedded accountMemberships always reflect current state. Because memberships are baked into the token, a role change is not visible until the client obtains a fresh token — callers must re-authenticate (or re-fetch) after claiming a role.

-----

Authentication Flows

Internal credentials

Registration hashes the password with bcrypt (genSalt(10)), rejecting duplicates with a 409 Conflict. Login compares with bcrypt.compare; a wrong password is a 400, an unknown account a 404. On success the controller signs a standard token and sets it as the httpOnly cookie.

OIDC / SSO

GET /auth/sso/login/:domainName is authentication-protected — SSO links a domain to an already-authenticated account rather than creating one.



The groups claim is mapped to a role in priority order:

admin, platform-adminbusiness-admin, business_adminstaff, business-staffanything else / noneIdP group(s)adminbusiness_adminbusiness_staffstandard_customerRole

Known gap: SSO state is stateless base64
-----
generateSSOLoginUrl encodes the PKCE verifier, username, and domain into the OAuth state as base64 JSON (marked TODO in code). This exposes the verifier to the browser and lets a crafted state be replayed. The intended design stores this server-side under a short-lived Redis key. See Domains.

TOTP role claiming

Roles above standard_customer are granted with a six-digit, time-based code derived from the domain’s totpSecrets (one secret per grantable role, generated at domain creation). resolveRoleFromOTP validates the submitted code against every domain’s secrets (window: 1); a match yields a { domainName, role } membership, which grantTOTPRoles applies by removing any existing membership for that domain and pushing the new one.

-----

Domain Operations

Creation side effects

addDomain performs three steps:

1. Generate totpSecrets for every role at or above business_staff.
2. Persist the domain (ssoConfig is stored only for oidc strategy).
3. Grant the creator a business_admin membership, and call the notification-service to initialise default preferences.

Subdomain resolution

getDomainSubdomains uses a MongoDB $graphLookup aggregation, so the hierarchy can be arbitrarily deep and is resolved in a single query.

Subscription rules

subscribe joins an account to a domain as standard_customer and notifies the notification-service. It rejects oidc domains — those grant membership only through the SSO callback. unsubscribe removes the membership and disables the corresponding notification preference.

-----

Error Handling

models/error.ts defines a BaseError hierarchy carrying an HTTP code and a type: ValidationError (400), UnauthorizedError (401), ForbiddenError (403), NotFoundError (404), ConflictError (409), InternalError (500). Controllers and services throw these; the global error middleware serialises them into a consistent JSON envelope. See Auth Service: Middleware for the authorization and error-handling middleware in detail.

entries.title: Auth Service: Middleware
entries.content:
Auth Service: Middleware

Implementation reference for the cross-cutting Express middleware shared by the Node services. Controllers and services hold business logic; authorization, request authentication, and error shaping are delegated to the middleware documented here. The canonical implementations live in the auth-service.

-----

1. Authentication — requireAuthentication

File: src/middlewares/authentication.ts

Verifies the caller’s identity token and attaches it to the request. It performs authentication only — it does not evaluate roles.

	export const requireAuthentication = (req, _res, next) => {
	  const token = req.cookies?.[COOKIE_NAME];        // "authentication_token"
	  if (!token) throw new NotFoundError("Could not find token for the cookie");
	  req.account = TokenService.verifyToken(token);   // throws if invalid/expired
	  next();
	};

- The token is read from the httpOnly cookie authentication_token, not from an Authorization header.
- On success the decoded payload (accountId, accountName, accountMemberships) is attached to req.account for downstream handlers.
- A missing token raises NotFoundError; an invalid or expired signature throws from verifyToken and is converted to a 500-class response by the error handler unless caught earlier.

Always trust req.account, never the body
-----
Controllers must read the caller identity from req.account. A userId/accountName in the request body is attacker-controlled and must never be used for authorization.

-----

2. Authorization — requireAuthorization(level)

File: src/models/role.ts

A middleware factory that enforces a minimum role. It is contextual: when the route carries a :domainName parameter, the check is scoped to the caller’s role in that domain.

	router.get("/domains",  requireAuthentication, getAllAllowedDomains);
	router.post("/domains", requireAuthentication,
	            requireAuthorization("business_admin"), createDomain);

Evaluation pipeline:

1. Resolve the payload. Prefer req.account (set by requireAuthentication); otherwise parse a Bearer token from the Authorization header and verify it.
2. Select memberships. If :domainName is present, keep only memberships for that domain; otherwise consider all.
3. Compare weights. Pass if any selected membership satisfies hasRequiredRole(role, level) — i.e. its weight is at least the required weight (admin 100, business_admin 80, business_staff 60, standard_customer 10).
4. Reject with 401 when the token is missing or malformed, and 403 when no membership meets the threshold.

-----

3. Internal Signature — requireHmacSignature

File: src/middlewares/authentication.ts

Guards the internal provisioning endpoint (POST /business/register) for service-to-service calls that are not user-authenticated.

- The client sends x-signature, the hex HMAC-SHA256 of the raw request body keyed by INTERNAL_ADMIN_SECRET.
- The server recomputes the HMAC over req.rawBody (captured by the JSON body parser in index.ts) and compares with crypto.timingSafeEqual to avoid timing leaks.
- Any mismatch, malformed signature, or missing secret raises ForbiddenError / InternalError.

-----

4. Global Error Handling — errorHandler

File: src/middlewares/error.ts

The last middleware in the stack. Controllers and services never write error responses directly; they throw typed errors, and this handler serialises them.

	export const errorHandler = (err, _req, res, _next) => {
	  if (err instanceof BaseError)
	    return res.status(err.code).json({ type: err.type, message: err.message });
	  return res.status(500).json({
	    type: "Internal Server Error",
	    message: "An unexpected error occurred. Please try again later.",
	  });
	};

Any error that is not a BaseError is scrubbed to a generic 500, so stack traces and database internals never reach the client.

Error taxonomy

File: src/models/error.ts

ValidationErrorUnauthorizedErrorForbiddenErrorNotFoundErrorConflictErrorInternalErrorClass400401403404409500StatusValidation ErrorUnauthorized ErrorForbidden ErrorNot Found ErrorConflict ErrorInternal Errortype

Every error carries an HTTP code and a human-readable type, producing a stable { type, message } envelope the frontend can branch on.

Convention
-----
This authentication/authorization/error pattern is shared across the Node services (auth, twin, notification). New endpoints should attach requireAuthentication (and requireAuthorization where a role is needed) and throw typed errors rather than handling HTTP status codes inline.

entries.title: Digital Twin Service
entries.content:
Digital Twin Service

Implementation reference for the twin-service. For its architectural role, see Digital Twin Service Architecture; for the domain model, see Building Management.

A Node.js / Express service in TypeScript. It is the spatial source of truth: it stores buildings and rooms, and on every change it synchronises a lightweight clone to the dependent services.

-----

Source Layout

src/router.tssrc/controller/buildings.tssrc/services/buildings.tssrc/services/names.tssrc/services/sensors.tssrc/services/contractsService.tssrc/models/building.tsPathRoute table for building CRUD, plus health, metrics, and the /contracts metric catalog.HTTP adapters over the building service.Core CRUD logic and orchestration of downstream synchronisation.Pure helpers for normalising and backfilling building/room display names.Pushes the threshold clone to the sensor-service.Seeds default display preferences in the contracts-service.Mongoose schema for Building, Room, and the geometry value objects.Responsibility

-----

Configuration

SENSOR_SERVICE_URLCONTRACTS_SERVICE_URLNODE_ENVVariablefalls back to VITE_SERVER_URL, then http://localhost:3000http://localhost:3001—DefaultTarget for the threshold clone sync.Target for default-preference seeding.When test, threshold sync and preference seeding are skipped.Purpose

-----

Data Model

	interface IBuilding {
	  id: string;        // server-generated UUID (crypto.randomUUID); unique
	  name: string;
	  rooms: Room[];     // embedded
	  domains: string[]; // tenant names; indexed { domains: 1 }
	}
	interface Room {
	  id: string;        // taken from the uploaded model file; stable
	  name: string;
	  capacity: number;
	  position: { x: number; y: number; z: number };   // value object
	  dimensions: { width: number; height: number; depth: number }; // value object
	  color?: string;
	}

- id is generated server-side; a client-supplied id on creation is ignored.
- The { domains: 1 } index makes Building.find({ domains }) an index seek rather than a collection scan.
- Geometry is stored as _id: false subdocuments. The schema enables timestamps.

-----

HTTP API

POST /registerGET /building/:idGET /buildings/:domainGET /domain/:buildingNamePATCH /building/:buildingIdPATCH /building/:buildingId/room/:roomIdGET /contractsGET /health/, GET /metrics/Method · PathCreate a building from { name, rooms, domains }. Assigns a UUID, normalises names, persists, then synchronises downstream. Returns the full document. 201.Fetch one building by UUID; backfills names on read. 404 if absent.List buildings scoped to a tenant (domains contains :domain). Returns [] when none.Return the flattened list of domains for buildings matching the name. Used by notification-service for recipient resolution.Update name and/or domains (and an optional maxTemperature forwarded to the clone). Re-syncs.Update a room’s name, color, or capacity. Re-syncs.Return this service’s metric catalog (roomName, roomMaxOccupancy).Liveness and Prometheus metrics.Description

Authentication is not enforced at this layer
-----
As wired in router.ts, the building routes do not attach an authentication middleware. Tenant scoping is performed by the :domain path parameter against each building’s domains array, but the identity token is not verified inside the service. This is a gap relative to the intended design, where tenancy is enforced from the verified token. Treat token verification here as a required hardening step.

-----

Name Normalisation

services/names.ts keeps display names consistent on both write and read.

- normalizeBuildingName(name, id) resolves to the trimmed name, else id, else the literal "Building".
- normalizeRoomNames(rooms) trims each room name, falling back to the room id.
- backfillNames(building) re-applies these rules when a building is read and persists only if something changed — so older documents are healed lazily.

-----

Downstream Synchronisation

A building’s structure is mirrored to two services. The two paths have different failure semantics, which matters operationally.



- Sensor threshold clone (syncBuildingClone): a PUT carrying { name, rooms: [{ id, name }], maxTemperature? }. It is gated by shouldSyncThresholds() (skipped under test) and throws on a non-OK response, so a sensor-service failure fails the building operation.
- Contracts preference seeding (initBuildingPreferences): a POST that is skipped under test and swallows errors (logged only), so it never blocks building creation.

Known gap: synchronous coupling
-----
The sensor clone is an inline, synchronous REST call inside addBuilding/updateBuilding. The roadmap is to replace it with a published TWIN_UPDATED event so the sensor-service can update its copy asynchronously, decoupling the two services.

-----

Error Handling

The service uses the same BaseError hierarchy and global error middleware as the auth-service (ValidationError 400, NotFoundError 404, …) — see Auth Service: Middleware. Building/room lookups raise NotFoundError when the target does not exist.

entries.title: Notification Service
Sensor Service
entries.content:
Sensor Service

Implementation reference for the sensor-service. For its architectural role, see Sensor Service Architecture; for the domain model, see Telemetry Ingestion.

A Node.js / Express service in strict TypeScript. It ingests sensor readings from constrained hardware, persists them, publishes them for live distribution, and manages per-building thresholds. Its design answers two pressures: never make a sensor wait, and never edit the core to add a sensor type.

-----

Source Layout

src/kernel/sensorKernel.tssrc/modules/ISensorModule.tssrc/modules/BaseSensorModule.tssrc/modules/*Module.tssrc/services/*ModuleService.tssrc/controllers/src/models/src/config/PathThe microkernel: a startup-populated registry that resolves a module by its type.The module contract and the immutable ValidationResult value object.Abstract base implementing the sealed ingestion pipeline (Template Method).Concrete strategies: TemperatureModule, PeopleCountModule, AirQualityModule.Per-metric persistence, aggregation, and threshold logic.ingestionController.ts, readController.ts, thresholdController.ts.Signal schemas, buildingThreshold.ts, and the metric catalog.db.ts (Mongo), redis.ts (broker).Responsibility

-----

Configuration

REDIS_URLMONGO_URI / DB URLVariableredis://localhost:6379—DefaultBroker for telemetry:raw publishing and the read-path cache.Time-series storage for signals and thresholds.Purpose

The temperature module additionally calls the notification-service push endpoint when a reading breaches its bound.

-----

The Microkernel

SensorKernel is a pure dispatch registry. Modules are registered once at startup; request handling is then a lookup with no branching on sensor type in the core.

	class SensorKernel {
	  register(module: ISensorModule): this  // throws on duplicate type
	  resolve(type: string): ISensorModule | undefined
	  getRegisteredTypes(): readonly string[]
	}

Duplicate registration throws synchronously — it is a configuration bug and should crash at boot, not silently overwrite.

-----

Ingestion: Fast Path / Slow Path

The critical constraint is that hardware clients are released immediately. The controller validates synchronously, flushes the response, and only then processes.



- A missing/blank type is 400; an unregistered type is 404; a failed validation is 422 with structured details.
- setImmediate defers module.process() until after the 202 is on the wire, so persistence and broker latency never affect the sensor’s round-trip.
- Background failures are caught and logged; they never reach the (already closed) connection.

-----

The Module Contract

ISensorModule and ValidationResult

validate() is pure and synchronous (no I/O) and returns an immutable ValidationResult rather than throwing, so the controller can return machine-readable errors before processing. process() is impure and runs only after a successful validate().

BaseSensorModule — Template Method

Concrete modules extend BaseSensorModule<T> rather than implementing ISensorModule directly. The base seals the ingestion pipeline:

	async process(payload: unknown): Promise<void> {
	  await this.persist(payload);                         // child-specific
	  this.publishTelemetry(this.buildTelemetryEvent(payload)); // base-owned
	}

- The order is fixed — persist, then publish — so a module can never publish a telemetry:raw event before the reading is queryable from the read path, nor forget the publish.
- publishTelemetry writes to telemetry:raw fire-and-forget (failures logged), keeping ingestion latency independent of Redis.
- getLatest is shared (most-recent document per room); the threshold methods default to no-ops and are overridden only by modules that support thresholds.

A child therefore implements only what is unique: type, model, validate, persist, buildTelemetryEvent, getAllLatest, getDashboardData.

-----

Data Models

Signals

Each metric is its own append-only collection. All share building, roomId, timestamp plus metric-specific fields, and carry two indexes:

- { building: 1, timestamp: -1 } — building-wide range scans.
- { building: 1, roomId: 1, timestamp: -1 } — per-room point and range queries.

TemperaturePeopleCountAirQualityCollectiontemperaturepeopleCountpm25, pm10, co2, voc, temperature, humidity, aqi, indoor_aqi, optional scenarioMetric-specific fields

BuildingThreshold

One document per building (buildingId unique). It holds building-level default bounds and an array of per-room overrides, each with optional temperature { minTemp, maxTemp }, peopleCount { maxPeople }, and airQuality { maxAqi, maxCo2 }.

-----

HTTP API

POST /ingestGET read endpointsPATCH /thresholds/...PUT /thresholds/buildings/:idGET /contractsMethod · PathIngest a reading. 202 on accept, 422 on validation failure, 404 on unknown type.Latest-per-room, all-latest-per-building, and bucketed dashboard time-series, served by readController.Update building- or room-level thresholds, served by thresholdController.Receives the building clone (rooms, name) pushed by the twin-service.The metric catalog (peopleCount, temperature, airQuality).Description

-----

Thresholds

Thresholds are static, human-defined configuration. They live in a single shared BuildingThreshold document with nested slices per metric. When a domain service updates its slice it uses scoped dot-notation so it cannot corrupt a neighbour’s configuration:

	findOneAndUpdate({ buildingId }, { $set: { "temperature.maxTemp": 25 } })

A room-level bound, when present, overrides the building default for that room during evaluation.

-----

Read-Path Caching

getAllLatest(buildingId) — which drives the initial dashboard load — runs a MongoDB aggregation. To collapse duplicate work when many users open the same building, results are cached in Redis under sensor:latest:{type}:{buildingId} with a 10-second TTL. The live telemetry path bypasses the cache entirely: every write publishes to telemetry:raw, so the frontend receives real-time updates via the socket without waiting for cache expiry.

-----

Extension Guide

Adding a sensor type requires no change to the controller or kernel:

1. Create a module extending BaseSensorModule<T> and implement the unique members (type, model, validate, persist, buildTelemetryEvent, read methods).
2. Register it during boot:

	const kernel = new SensorKernel()
	  .register(new TemperatureModule())
	  .register(new PeopleCountModule())
	  .register(new AirQualityModule())
	  .register(new LightLevelModule()); // new type

The base class supplies the telemetry:raw publish and the shared getLatest, so a new module only writes what is genuinely specific to it.

entries.title: Contracts Service
entries.content:
Contracts Service

Implementation reference for the contracts-service. For its architectural role, see Contracts Service Architecture; for the domain model, see Telemetry Distribution.

The only Rust service in the platform. Built on Axum and Tokio, it runs an HTTP API and a background telemetry tunnel in a single process, holding per-building preferences in memory for a lock-free hot path.

-----

Source Layout

src/main.rssrc/state.rssrc/db.rssrc/tunnel.rssrc/discovery.rssrc/models.rssrc/api/data.rssrc/api/init.rssrc/api/dashboard.rsPathBoot: connect Mongo, hydrate the in-memory map, start the tunnel, serve the Axum router.AppState: the shared DashMap projection plus the typed Mongo collection handle.Mongo access: connect (with unique index), load_all (hydration), upsert_preference.The background task that filters telemetry:raw and republishes per building.Resolves downstream metric endpoints from *_METRICS_URL environment variables.PreferenceDocument, MetricContract, and the discovery response types.GET/POST /preferences/{building_id}.POST /preferences/init/{building_id}.GET / — the aggregated metric catalog.Responsibility

-----

Configuration

MONGO_URIREDIS_URLPORT*_METRICS_URLVariablemongodb://localhost:27017redis://127.0.0.1/3000—DefaultDurable store for preferences (database crowdvision, collection preferences).Broker for the telemetry tunnel.HTTP listen port.Any variable with this suffix is treated as a service base URL for metric discovery.Purpose

-----

State and Data Model

	pub struct AppState {
	    pub building_preferences: Arc<DashMap<String, Vec<String>>>, // hot path
	    pub mongo_col: Collection<PreferenceDocument>,               // durable
	}
	pub struct PreferenceDocument {
	    pub id: Option<ObjectId>,
	    pub building_id: String,      // unique index
	    pub allowed_columns: Vec<String>,
	}

DashMap shards its internal locks across cores, so the tunnel’s millions of reads almost never contend with an occasional write. The Mongo collection enforces one document per building via a unique index on building_id.

-----

Startup Sequence

	1. connect(MONGO_URI)          -> ensure unique index on building_id
	2. load_all()                  -> seed the DashMap from persisted documents
	3. start_telemetry_tunnel()    -> spawn the background filter task
	4. axum::serve(router)         -> begin handling HTTP

Hydration at boot means a restart restores all preferences before the first request is served.

-----

Persistence Model (Hot Path / Cold Path)

Reads and the tunnel operate purely in memory; durability is handled off the request path.

- Hot path (reads, tunnel): O(1) lock-free DashMap lookups; the tunnel never blocks on a write.
- Cold path (writes): update_preferences mutates the DashMap immediately, then spawns a fire-and-forget Tokio task to upsert_preference into Mongo. The HTTP response returns 200 without waiting for the database. The same write-through applies to init when it creates a new entry.

Eventual durability
-----
Because the database write is fire-and-forget, a crash in the narrow window between the in-memory insert and the persisted upsert can lose the most recent change. This is an accepted trade-off for hot-path latency; the next successful save reconciles it.

-----

HTTP API

GET /preferences/{building_id}POST /preferences/{building_id}POST /preferences/init/{building_id}GET /Method · PathReturn { building_id, allowed_columns }. 404 if the building has no entry.Replace the allowed-column list (body { allowed_columns }). Instant cache write + async persist. Returns { status, building_id, active_columns }.Idempotently seed defaults ["roomName", "roomMaxOccupancy"] with an atomic or-insert, so a repeat call is a safe no-op. Called by the twin-service on building creation.The aggregated metric catalog (see Metric Discovery).Description

-----

The Telemetry Tunnel

Started at boot, the tunnel opens a dedicated Redis PubSub connection and subscribes to telemetry:raw.



- Filtering is a type-based gate: it matches the message’s type against each building’s allowed_columns; the payload is forwarded intact (no field stripping).
- One raw message fans out to every building subscribed to that metric type.
- Each message is processed in its own task::spawn, so processing time never back-pressures the subscription stream.

-----

Metric Discovery

GET / builds the catalog the frontend uses to populate column pickers.

1. discover_services() collects every environment value whose key ends in _METRICS_URL.
2. Each service’s GET /contracts is fetched and deserialised (either a ServiceMetricsContract or a bare metric list).
3. Each metric is tagged with its source_service and added via push_unique_metric, which deduplicates on (metric_key, interface_name, source_service).

New metric types therefore appear automatically once a service advertises them; no change is needed here.

Known gap: deletion cleanup
-----
There is no DELETE /preferences/{building_id}. When a building is removed from the twin-service, its entry remains in both the DashMap and Mongo, and the tunnel keeps evaluating it. A deletion endpoint (called from the twin on removal) is the intended fix.

entries.title: Socket Service
entries.content:
Socket Service

Implementation reference for the socket-service. For its architectural role, see Socket Service Architecture.

A small Node.js service built on Socket.IO and a Redis subscriber. It is the bridge between the broker and the browser: it relays alerts and per-building telemetry to connected clients over WebSockets. It has no database, no controllers, and no domain model — the entire service is a single index.ts.

-----

Configuration

REDIS_URLCLIENT_URLVariableemptyhttp://localhost:5173DefaultBroker connection for the subscriber.Allowed CORS origin for the Socket.IO server (credentials enabled).Purpose

The HTTP/WebSocket server listens on port 3000.

-----

Broker Subscriptions and Emissions

At startup the service connects a Redis subscriber and binds two channels to two Socket.IO events:

notificationstelemetry:filtered:* (pattern)Redis subscriptionemit notification to every socketemit telemetry to room building:{id}Socket.IO emissionAll connected clients (broadcast).Only clients in that building’s room.Audience

For the pattern subscription, the building id is recovered from the channel name (stripping the telemetry:filtered: prefix) and used to target the room. Each incoming message is JSON-parsed before emission.

-----

Client Subscription Protocol

Clients declare their interest by joining and leaving rooms over the socket connection:

join_room(userId)subscribe_building(buildingId)unsubscribe_building(buildingId)Client eventJoins a per-user room.Joins room building:{buildingId} to start receiving that building’s telemetry.Leaves room building:{buildingId} to stop receiving it.Handler effect



Telemetry is room-scoped so each browser receives only the buildings it is displaying; alerts are broadcast because any client may need them.

-----

Scaling Considerations

The service runs as a single Socket.IO process. Two changes are required to run multiple replicas:

- Sticky sessions. The HTTP long-polling handshake must return to the same instance; the Kubernetes ingress sets cookie affinity for this (see Deployment & Kubernetes.
- Cross-instance rooms. With multiple replicas a room spans instances, so emissions must propagate via the Socket.IO Redis adapter. This is a known extension point, not yet in place.

For where this service sits in the end-to-end real-time path, see Communication & Data Flow.

entries.title: Agent Service
entries.content:
Agent Service

The Python agent-service is CrowdVision’s tool-calling assistant. It answers questions
by combining a permission-filtered documentation knowledge base with live building and
room data from twin-service.

It is built on FastAPI — an async Python web framework chosen because the agent spends
most of its time waiting on network I/O (the language-model provider, the database, the
twin-service), and an async stack lets one worker handle many in-flight requests instead of
blocking a thread per call. For local commands and troubleshooting, see the service
README.md. For writing a new tool, see Adding an Agent Tool.

-----

How It Works

A request to POST /ask is not a single model call. The agent runs a tool-calling loop:
the language model is given the question plus the JSON schemas of the available tools, and on
each turn it may either answer or ask to call one or more tools. We run the requested tools,
feed their results back, and let the model decide again. This “agentic” pattern is preferred
over stuffing everything into one prompt because the model itself decides what data it needs
and can chain lookups (for example, resolve a building name to an ID, then fetch that
building’s rooms).



The loop is bounded by MAX_TOOL_HOPS so a confused model cannot spin forever; hitting the
limit returns the IDK marker with decision=tool_loop_exhausted. Tool errors are handed back
to the model as ordinary tool-result messages rather than raised, so the model gets a chance
to recover (retry with different arguments, or give up cleanly).

stream=true runs the same loop and then emits the finished answer as a single Server-Sent
Events token event followed by done. It is not per-token streaming yet — the event
shape exists so the client contract is stable when true streaming is added later.

-----

Scope

The assistant is deliberately not a general-purpose chatbot. Its system prompt restricts
it to CrowdVision topics — buildings and rooms, live occupancy and sensor state, and how the
platform works — and instructs it to decline anything else (general knowledge, math, coding,
translation, writing, other products, personal advice) in one short sentence, without calling
a tool.

The motivation is product trust and cost: a building-occupancy assistant that also writes
poems or answers trivia invites misuse, leaks the fact that it is “just an LLM”, and spends
the shared provider budget on off-topic work. Two refusal behaviors are intentionally kept
distinct:

- Out of scope → a brief scoped refusal (“I can only help with CrowdVision…”), no tool call.
- In scope but unanswerable → the prompt instructs the model to return the exact IDK marker
	after tools come up empty. The service also returns that marker when the tool-loop limit is
	exhausted; it does not enforce that every model-emitted IDK was preceded by a tool call.

Greetings are treated as in scope and answered directly.

-----

Capabilities

The model can choose from five read-only tools:

search_docslist_buildingsget_buildinglist_roomsget_roomToolAgent Postgres databaseTwin ServiceTwin ServiceTwin ServiceTwin ServiceData sourceHybrid vector and full-text search over ingested documentation.List buildings for a domain.Fetch complete building details by ID.List room occupancy, capacity, and temperature.Fetch one room by ID.Purpose

The agent answers with validated documentation citations, a tool-call trace, token usage,
estimated/provider-reported cost, an idk flag, and a final decision.

Warning
-----
The current twin tools do not forward the caller JWT or perform their own ToolContext
authorization. Do not add sensitive or write-capable tools without explicit
authorization, confirmation, and audit behavior.

-----

Knowledge Base And Retrieval

In Docker Compose, the one-shot agent-ingester waits for the service to become healthy,
then ingests .qd, .md, and .markdown files from documentation/user and
documentation/developer.

	just agent-ingest

Ingestion is idempotent by global content hash: identical content is skipped. It does not
replace or delete an older document when a source file changes, so a clean rebuild requires
clearing the agent tables before re-ingesting. Each source is split by a Markdown-aware
chunker that keeps headings, fenced code, and tables intact, because a chunk that splits a
code block or a table mid-row retrieves badly.

search_docs runs hybrid retrieval:

1. Embed the query into a vector.
2. Run a pgvector cosine-similarity search (semantic match).
3. Run a PostgreSQL full-text search via tsvector and websearch_to_tsquery (exact-term match).
4. Merge the two ranked lists with Reciprocal Rank Fusion.
5. Pass the merged candidates through the configured reranker (noop is the only current implementation).
6. Return the top TOP_K_FINAL chunks.

The reason for running both searches is that they fail in opposite ways: vector search
understands meaning but can miss a rare exact token (an error code, a config key), while
full-text search nails exact tokens but is blind to paraphrase. Reciprocal Rank Fusion
combines them without the usual problem of incompatible score scales — instead of trying to
add a cosine similarity to a ts_rank, it scores each document by 1 / (k + rank) summed
across the lists, so only the position in each list matters and documents that both methods
rank highly rise to the top.

Both searches filter chunks against the caller’s JWT roles and domains at the SQL boundary, so
a user never even retrieves a chunk they are not allowed to see:

	chunk permissions are empty            (visible to every authenticated caller)
	OR
	chunk permissions overlap the caller's JWT roles/domains

-----

Citations

The system prompt asks the model to mark any claim drawn from search_docs with a
[^<chunk-id>] footnote. After the model answers, the service validates those markers
against the chunks actually returned:

1. extract_citations resolves each marker to a real retrieved chunk.
2. Valid markers become structured citation objects in the response.
3. Unknown markers are reported in retrieval.hallucinated_citations.
4. strip_hallucinated removes the invented markers from the displayed answer.

This guards against a model citing a source it never saw. Note the limit: it proves a marker
points at a real retrieved chunk, not that the chunk actually supports the sentence.

-----

API

Docker Compose exposes the service through Caddy at http://localhost/agent. Kubernetes
uses the same /agent ingress prefix. Inside the service network it listens on port 3000.

GET /agent/healthPOST /agent/askPOST /agent/ingestGET /agent/docsEndpointPublicJWT cookie or Bearer tokenJWT cookie or Bearer tokenPublic FastAPI UIAuthenticationLiveness and database reachability.Run the tool loop; JSON with stream=false, SSE by default.Chunk, embed, and store one Markdown document.Interactive OpenAPI documentation.Purpose

Warning
-----
/ingest is authenticated but currently does not enforce an administrator role. Treat it
as an internal endpoint.

The request schema currently exposes top_k, but it is not wired into retrieval. Retrieval
depth is controlled by search_docs and the service’s TOP_K_* configuration.

-----

Authentication And Permissions

Protected routes accept either the configured JWT cookie (authentication_token by default)
or an Authorization: Bearer <jwt> header. Tokens are verified with HS256 against
JWT_SECRET. CrowdVision uses stateless JWTs so the agent can authorize a caller without
a network round-trip to the auth-service — roles and domains travel inside the signed token.

Roles and domains are collected from the token’s direct claims and accountMemberships, then
combined into AuthUser.permissions and used both for retrieval filtering and role gates.
Role-sensitive operations use the same weighted hierarchy as auth-service:
standard_customer (10), business_staff (60), business_admin (80), admin (100). The one
role-gated operation today is the per-request answer-model override.

REQUIRE_AUTH=false creates an anonymous user and is intended only for isolated local use.

-----

Provider And Models

One OpenAI-compatible endpoint drives both chat/tool-calling and embeddings. Using a single
OpenAI-compatible contract (rather than a vendor SDK) means the model can be swapped — between
OpenRouter, a local server, or another gateway — by changing configuration, not code.
OpenRouter is the default:

	OPENROUTER_API_KEY=<your-openrouter-key>
	LLM_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
	ANSWER_MODEL=openai/gpt-4o-mini
	EMBEDDING_MODEL=openai/text-embedding-3-small
	EMBEDDING_DIM=768
	MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS=2048

LLM_API_KEY, DEEPSEEK_API_KEY, and GOOGLE_API_KEY are accepted as legacy key aliases,
but the supplied key must authenticate against LLM_BASE_URL.

Privileged callers may override the answer model per /ask request:

	MODEL_OVERRIDE_MIN_ROLE=business_admin
	ALLOWED_MODELS=openai/gpt-4o-mini,google/gemini-2.5-flash

The override spends the server’s shared provider balance, which is why it is gated: it is
rejected with 403 below the configured role and with 400 when a non-empty allowlist
excludes the model. An empty ALLOWED_MODELS permits any model for callers that pass the role
gate. The embedding model is never changed by an answer-model override, because changing it
would invalidate the indexed vectors.

-----

Database

Alembic (the schema-migration tool for SQLAlchemy) creates two tables:

- documents: source, content hash, metadata, permissions.
- chunks: document relation, text, section, kind, embedding, generated tsvector,
	permissions, and metadata.

plus pgvector, full-text GIN, permission GIN, and relation indexes. pgvector is the
Postgres extension that adds a vector column type and similarity operators, which is what
lets semantic search live in the same database as everything else instead of a separate vector
store.

The initial migration defines embedding vector(768). The vector dimension is fixed in the
schema, so changing EMBEDDING_DIM requires a matching migration and re-ingestion — an
environment-variable change alone is not enough. Runtime queries use async SQLAlchemy with
asyncpg; Alembic uses a synchronous driver for migrations.

-----

Evaluation

Behavior is checked with a golden dataset: hand-written questions, each paired with the
behavior we expect rather than one exact answer string. A row can assert which tool should
fire, which documentation source should be cited, which keywords the answer must contain,
whether the response should be the IDK marker, and — for scope — that an off-topic question is
declined. Pinning behavior instead of wording keeps the checks robust to harmless rephrasing
while still catching real regressions.

These evaluations are deliberately separate from the unit tests. They drive the real running
stack against a real provider and depend on an already-ingested corpus, so they are slow,
cost money, and are not hermetic — the opposite trade-off from the fast unit tests, and the
reason they are run on demand rather than in the normal test run.

Most assertions are deterministic: a tool name appears in the trace, a citation source
matches, a substring is present. But some behavior is semantic and substring matching cannot
capture it — the clearest case is “did the assistant decline an off-topic question, or did it
actually answer it?” Those rows opt into an LLM-as-a-judge: a second model grades the
answer against a rubric. The judge defaults to a different model family than the answer
model, because a model asked to grade output in its own style tends to favour it, and an
independent family reduces that self-preference bias.

Not every failure is a regression. Some rows test behavior the agent is known not to handle
yet — an accepted, tracked gap. Marking such a row xfail (“expected to fail”, a convention
borrowed from pytest) keeps it in the suite without turning the run red: when it fails as
predicted it is reported as XFAIL and the build stays green. The point is that the gap stays
visible and counted on every run instead of being deleted and forgotten — and every xfail
must carry a written reason, so an accepted gap is never silent debt. If an xfail row ever
starts passing — because the gap was actually fixed — it is reported as XPASS, the signal
that its marker is now stale and should be removed so the row goes back to guarding the
behavior normally.

For the dataset schema, the scoring and exit-code contract, model sweeps, and how to configure
the judge, see the agent-service README.md.

-----

Langfuse Observability

The agent is instrumented with OpenTelemetry (OTel), a vendor-neutral tracing standard, so
each request produces a trace tree of spans. The motivation is that an agent’s behavior is
otherwise opaque — you cannot tell from the final answer alone which tools ran, what retrieval
returned, or where the time and tokens went. Langfuse is the backend that ingests those
traces and renders them with LLM-specific views (token counts, cost, generations):

	agent.answer
	  agent.hop.N
	    gen_ai.chat <model>
	  tool.<name>
	    retrieve.embed / retrieve.vector / retrieve.keyword / retrieve.rerank

Spans are typed so Langfuse renders them meaningfully: generation spans carry model, tokens,
and cost; retriever spans carry the query and the ranked documents; tool spans carry arguments
and results, and failed tools get an error status plus the exception.

Capturing the actual query, document text, and tool I/O is gated by OBSERVE_PAYLOADS, which
defaults to off because that content can be sensitive; the development Compose override
turns it on for useful local traces. Span types, counts, and error statuses are still emitted
when payload capture is disabled.

Local Langfuse login
-----
URL http://localhost:3030 (http://localhost/langfuse redirects there); default email
admin@crowd-vision.local, password langfuse-admin. Set LANGFUSE_INIT_USER_EMAIL and
LANGFUSE_INIT_USER_PASSWORD before the first Langfuse startup to change the initial
login; changing them later does not update an existing user.

With OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT set, spans are exported over OTLP. Without it, the agent
prints compact one-line span summaries to its logs. Langfuse and the OTLP variables are part
of the Compose stack but are not generated by just env — see
Setting Up Your Environment. Langfuse is not deployed by the current
Kubernetes manifests.

-----

Current Deployment Differences

Agent service + pgvector databaseAutomatic documentation ingestionLangfuse trace backendProvider configurationCapabilityIncludedIncluded through agent-ingesterIncludedOPENROUTER_API_KEY and model variables forwarded by ComposeDocker ComposeIncludedNot deployed; ingest manuallyNot deployedVerify agent-service-secret; current secret generation still uses legacy key namesKubernetes

The source of truth for runtime settings is server/agent-service/app/config.py.
LLM_TEMPERATURE controls the default generation temperature, while TWIN_TIMEOUT_SECONDS
controls live-data request timeouts. Invalid numeric bounds, unsupported model-override roles
or log formats, and malformed service URL schemes fail during settings loading. Startup also
fails when the provider key is missing, or when authentication is enabled without JWT_SECRET.
CORS_ORIGINS is currently defined there but is not consumed by FastAPI middleware.

entries.title: Chat Service
entries.content:
Chat Service

The chat-service is the conversation layer in front of the agent. The Agent Service
is intentionally stateless — each POST /ask is an independent call that takes an optional
history array but stores nothing. The chat-service is what turns that into a real chat
product: it persists conversations, owns them per user, and feeds the recent history back into
each agent call.

It is a Node.js / Express service in TypeScript, matching the rest of the backend. Keeping
conversation storage out of the Python agent is deliberate: the agent stays a pure
question-answering engine, while session persistence — a different concern with a different
database and scaling profile — lives in its own service.

-----

Source Layout

src/router.tssrc/controller/conversation.tssrc/services/conversation.tssrc/services/agent.tssrc/models/conversation.tssrc/middlewares/authentication.tsPathRoute table for conversation CRUD and message sending, plus health and metrics.HTTP adapters over the conversation service.Core logic: ownership checks, validation, history assembly, persistence.Thin client that calls agent-service /ask and validates its response.Mongoose schema for Conversation, its embedded messages, and citations.Verifies the JWT and extracts the owning account id.Responsibility

-----

Data Model

A conversation is a single MongoDB document with its messages embedded rather than stored
in a separate collection. A chat is loaded and saved as a whole, and message counts are
bounded, so embedding avoids a join and makes each individual MongoDB save atomic.

Sending a message is not atomic end-to-end: it loads the conversation, waits for an external
agent request, then saves the updated document without a transaction or per-conversation lock.
Concurrent sends can therefore use stale history or race while saving. The current client
prevents concurrent sends from one widget, but server-side concurrency control is future work.

	interface IConversation {
	  userId: string;          // owner; indexed { userId: 1 }
	  title: string;           // defaults to "New chat"; auto-set from the first question
	  messages: IChatMessage[]; // embedded, capped at MAX_MESSAGES
	}
	interface IChatMessage {
	  role: "user" | "assistant";
	  content: string;         // 1..MAX_MESSAGE_LENGTH chars
	  citations?: ICitation[]; // grounding sources, copied from the agent's answer
	  createdAt: Date;
	}

Citations are persisted on the assistant message so that reopening an old conversation shows
the same grounded sources the agent originally returned, without re-querying.

-----

HTTP API

Conversation routes require authentication. GET /health and GET /metrics are public
operational endpoints. The service is exposed through Caddy at http://localhost/chat (and the
same /chat ingress prefix in Kubernetes).

POST /conversationsGET /conversationsGET /conversations/:idPATCH /conversations/:idDELETE /conversations/:idPOST /conversations/:id/messagesGET /health/, GET /metrics/Method · PathCreate an empty conversation for the caller. Optional title.List the caller’s conversations, newest first, without message bodies.Fetch one owned conversation with its full message history.Rename an owned conversation.Delete an owned conversation.Send a user message; returns the assistant reply (see below).Liveness and Prometheus metrics.Description

Every conversation route resolves the document by { _id, userId }, so one user can never read
or mutate another user’s chat — an unmatched id returns 404 rather than 403, to avoid
revealing that the conversation exists.

-----

Sending A Message

POST /conversations/:id/messages is where the chat-service orchestrates the agent. It loads
the conversation, takes the last HISTORY_MAX_MESSAGES turns as history, calls the agent, and
persists both the user message and the assistant reply.



Two design points worth calling out:

- The caller’s JWT cookie is forwarded to the agent, not a service account. This means the
	agent runs /ask under the user’s identity, so its permission-filtered retrieval only
	returns documents that user is allowed to see. The chat-service never widens access.
- History is truncated to the last HISTORY_MAX_MESSAGES turns before being sent. This
	bounds the prompt size (and therefore cost and latency) the agent has to process, and the
	agent already treats prior messages as untrusted context rather than instructions.

The first user message also seeds the conversation title (the prior default is "New chat"),
so a freshly created chat gets a meaningful name without a separate request.

-----

Configuration

AGENT_BASE_URLJWT_SECRETJWT_COOKIE_NAMEHISTORY_MAX_MESSAGESVariablehttp://agent-service:3000—authentication_token10DefaultWhere to reach the agent’s /ask endpoint.HS256 secret; must match the issuing auth-service. Authenticated requests fail if missing.Cookie the JWT is read from.Number of recent turns sent to the agent as context.Purpose

MAX_MESSAGES (100), MAX_MESSAGE_LENGTH (8000), and MAX_TITLE_LENGTH (120) are fixed
in-code limits that bound document growth and reject oversized input.

-----

Authentication

requireAuthentication reads the JWT from the configured cookie, verifies it with HS256, and
extracts accountId as the owning userId. The token is verified locally with no call to the
auth-service. The implementation follows the repository’s Node-service middleware conventions,
but chat-service owns its authentication middleware and error classes rather than importing
the auth-service implementation; see Auth Service: Middleware for the broader
pattern. A failure to reach the agent surfaces as a 502 Bad Gateway, distinguishing an
upstream agent problem from a client error.

-----

Client

In the frontend, the assistant is the floating ChatWidget.vue, driven by the
useChatSessions composable, which calls these /chat/* routes. For what the assistant can
and cannot answer from a user’s perspective, see Using the Assistant in the user guide.

entries.title: Communication Patterns in our Architecture
entries.content:
Communication Patterns in our Architecture

In a microservices architecture, how services communicate is just as important as what they do. CrowdVision’s long-term architectural goal is to restrict direct, synchronous service-to-service HTTP communication to prevent tight coupling and cascading failures.

However, we balance this strict decoupling with development pragmatism. Currently, the platform relies on four primary communication paradigms:

1. API Gateway Routing (External to Internal)
2. Event-Driven Pub/Sub (Internal Asynchronous)
3. Stateless Trust (Cross-Service Authorization)
4. Internal REST (Internal Synchronous - Transitional)

-----

1. Client-to-Service (API Gateway)

All external communication from the Vue frontend or IoT sensors is routed through the Caddy Reverse Proxy.

The client never speaks to a microservice directly. Caddy acts as our ingress controller and API Gateway. It terminates the SSL/TLS connection, reads the URL path (e.g., /api/twin/buildings), strips the prefix, and forwards the request to the correct internal Docker/Kubernetes container on the isolated gateway-net.

-----

2. Service-to-Service (Event-Driven via Redis)

When one microservice needs to inform another that a state change occurred (e.g., the Notification Service detects a crowded room and needs the Socket Service to update the UI), we prioritize asynchronous, event-driven architecture.

CrowdVision uses Redis as a central message broker implementing the Publish/Subscribe (Pub/Sub) pattern.



Why Redis Pub/Sub?

- Decoupling: The Producer (notification-service) does not need to know the IP address, port, or even the existence of the Consumers. It simply fires the event into the ether and forgets about it.
- Extensibility: If we want the Python-based agent-service to log an anomaly every time a room gets overcrowded, we don’t need to touch the Notification Service code. We simply instruct the Agent to SUBSCRIBE to the relevant Redis channel.

-----

3. Stateless Trust (Cross-Service Authorization)

A major challenge in a Database-per-Service architecture is Authorization.

If a user asks the Twin Service to delete a building, the Twin Service needs to know if the user has Admin privileges. However, the Twin Service cannot query the Auth Database due to strict data isolation boundaries.

To solve this without making synchronous HTTP calls back to the Auth Service on every request, CrowdVision uses Stateless JSON Web Tokens (JWTs).



The Embedded Payload Pattern

When the Auth Service mints a token, it embeds the user’s IDomainMembership array directly into the JWT payload. Because the backend services share the same cryptographic JWT_SECRET, they can mathematically verify the token’s authenticity. Once verified, the receiving service simply reads the memberships from the payload to authorize the request—entirely eliminating network overhead.

-----

4. Internal REST

> ⚠️ Architectural Note: This is currently a transitional pattern.

While Pub/Sub and Stateless JWTs handle events and authorization, there are instances in the current codebase where services must perform synchronous reads across bounded contexts (e.g., Service A immediately needs specific entity details owned by Service B to complete a transaction).

In these specific cases, services communicate directly via Internal HTTP REST calls.

- These calls are made using internal Docker/Kubernetes DNS resolution (e.g., http://auth-service:3000).
- They never traverse the public internet.
- Future Roadmap: We are actively evaluating strategies to phase this out.

-----

5. IoT Sensor to Service (Multiplexing)

Physical or simulated IoT sensors communicate their telemetry (temperature, people counts, etc.) to the backend using either standard HTTP POSTs.

The Multiplexing Pattern

Instead of spinning up a unique WebSocket server or connection for every physical room, CrowdVision uses a many-to-one multiplexing pattern:

- Hundreds of sensor clients connect to the single socket-service instance via the Gateway.
- To differentiate the continuous data streams, each sensor injects its corresponding roomId and sensorId directly into the JSON payload of every emitted event.
- The backend aggregates, validates, and routes this telemetry to the sensor-service and connected frontends.

entries.title: Users and Account
entries.content:
Users and Account

Identity and Access Management (IAM) in CrowdVision is handled exclusively by the Auth Service. Because of our Database-per-Service architecture, it is critical that user identity and organizational permissions are perfectly structured so that other services can securely authorize actions without relying on cross-database queries.

-----

👤 The Account Entity

The Account is the central identity record in the system.

	export interface IAccount extends Document {
	    name: string;
	    email: string;
	    password: string;
	    memberships: IDomainMembership[];
	    totpSecret?: string;
	}

The “Embedded Memberships” Design Choice

Notice that an account’s organizational permissions (memberships) are embedded directly as an array of subdocuments within the Account document itself.

- Why we did this: In standard SQL relational design, memberships would be a join table (e.g., account_domains). However, in CrowdVision, every single login or token refresh requires evaluating all of a user’s permissions. By embedding them, a single Account.findOne() query retrieves the user’s identity and their entire permission matrix instantly.
- The Trade-off: We trade slight write-complexity (updating memberships requires array manipulation in Mongo) for massive read-performance. Given the low cardinality of memberships (a user rarely belongs to more than a few domains), this is the optimal design choice.
- Payload Ceiling: Note that because these memberships are later embedded into JWTs under the accountMemberships key, this design implies a hard ceiling on how many domains a single user can join before HTTP header size limits (usually ~8KB) are breached.

-----

⚖️ Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

CrowdVision utilizes a Hierarchical Weight System to evaluate roles. Instead of explicitly defining what every single role can do, roles are assigned a numeric weight.
If a route requires a business_staff role, the system checks if the user’s role weight is greater than or equal to the required weight. The weights are defined as a const object in server/auth-service/src/models/role.ts and are shared across all services.

AdminBusiness AdminBusiness StaffStandard CustomerRole Name100806010Internal WeightFull system control, can manage other domains.Organization owner, can manage buildings, sensors, and generate invite codes.Facility managers, can edit room properties (capacity, temp thresholds).General users/viewers. By default, all new accounts are assigned this role.Privileges

-----

🛡️ The Authorization Middleware

To protect routes, developers use the requireAuthorization(requiredLevel) middleware, defined in server/auth-service/src/models/role.ts.

How the Token is Read

CrowdVision uses httpOnly cookies as the primary transport for JWTs. When requireAuthentication runs first in the middleware chain, it reads the token from the cookie and attaches the decoded payload to req.account. requireAuthorization then reads from req.account directly, bypassing the need to re-parse the header.

If requireAuthentication has not run (e.g., on a public route using requireAuthorization standalone), the middleware falls back to reading a Bearer token from the Authorization header.



Contextual Evaluation (domainName)

If the Express route includes domainName as a parameter (e.g., /:domainName/rooms), the middleware filters the user’s embedded accountMemberships and ensures their weight is sufficient only for that specific domain context.

A user might be a business_admin in the “Main Campus” domain, but only a standard_customer in the “Engineering Lab” domain. The same token is valid for both — the middleware evaluates the correct membership based on the URL.

> ⚠️ API Design Rule: URL Context
> Because the middleware is strictly stateless, it cannot query the database to find out which domain a roomId or sensorId belongs to. Therefore, all protected API routes MUST be nested under the domain parameter.
> 
> - DO: PATCH /domains/:domainName/rooms/:roomId
> - DON’T: PATCH /rooms/:roomId (The middleware will not know which domain to check permissions for).

-----

🔑 Role Elevation (The Invite System)

When a user signs up, they default to standard_customer. To be elevated to business_staff or business_admin without an Admin manually intervening, CrowdVision uses Domain TOTP Secrets (Time-Based One-Time Passwords).

1. Domain Creation: When an organization is created, the system generates cryptographic totpSecrets for roles at or above business_staff — meaning a separate rotating secret is created for business_staff and business_admin.
2. Invite Generation: A current Business Admin opens the QR generator in the dashboard. The client calls GET /domains/:domainName/totp/qr/:accountName, which uses the stored totpSecret to produce a live, rotating 6-digit OTP code encoded as a scannable QR code.
3. Role Claiming at Registration: The only way to claim an elevated role is to submit the 6-digit OTP code in the otp field of the registration request body (POST /register). There is currently no endpoint to claim a role on an existing account after creation.
4. Validation: The Auth Service scans every domain’s totpSecrets, validates the submitted OTP against each role’s secret (with a ±1 time-step window), and — if a match is found — replaces the account’s existing membership for that domain with the matched role via grantTOTPRoles.

-----

🛠️ Architecture Roadmap & Suggested Fixes

This section outlines technical debt and planned architectural updates for the IAM system.

1. Implement Rate-Limiting on Role Claiming:
	- Issue: The TOTP invite system relies on a 6-digit code (1,000,000 combinations) valid for a short time window. Without rate-limiting, a malicious script could brute-force the /register endpoint with repeated OTP guesses.
	- Fix Required: Implement an IP-based and Account-based rate limiter (e.g., using Redis) on the registration endpoint for requests that include an otp field. Lock out or apply progressive delays after 3–5 failed OTP attempts.
2. Post-Registration Role Claiming:
	- Issue: Users currently can only submit an OTP invite code at registration time. There is no way for an existing account to claim an elevated role (e.g., if they receive an invite link after already having signed up).
	- Fix Required: Add a dedicated authenticated endpoint (e.g., POST /me/claim-role) that accepts an OTP and calls grantTOTPRoles on the authenticated user’s account.
3. Evaluate Horizontal Permissions:
	- Issue: The current hierarchical weight system (10, 60, 80, 100) assumes higher roles always inherit lower role abilities.
	- Fix Required: If we ever introduce actions that a standard_customer can perform but a business_admin cannot (or vice versa in specific contexts), we will need to transition from a pure weight system to a hybrid system using explicit feature flags or a permissions matrix.

entries.title: Domains
entries.content:
Domains

A “Domain” represents a distinct organization, campus, or physical environment. Every piece of spatial data and every user account is scoped to one or more of these domains.

-----

🌍 The Domain Entity

The Domain model is managed by the Auth Service. It dictates not just the existence of an organization, but the rules for how users can join and authenticate against it.

	export interface IDomain extends Document {
	    name: string;
	    subdomains: Types.ObjectId[];
	    authStrategy: "internal" | "oidc";
	    totpSecrets: IDomainTOTPSecrets;
	    ssoConfig?: ISSOConfig;
	    isVisibleFromOutside?: boolean;
	}

Key Field Breakdown:

- name: The unique, human-readable identifier for the organization (e.g., university.edu). This string — not a MongoDB ObjectId — is what gets embedded in JWTs and stored in the Twin DB, making it the universal key for cross-service isolation.
- authStrategy: Determines how identity is verified for this organization. Either internal (credentials managed by CrowdVision) or oidc (delegated to an external identity provider). This field fundamentally changes the entire login flow.
- subdomains: An array of ObjectIds pointing to other Domain documents. Subdomain resolution uses MongoDB’s $graphLookup aggregation stage, meaning the hierarchy can be arbitrarily deep (e.g., university.edu → engineering.university.edu → robotics.engineering.university.edu).
- isVisibleFromOutside: When true, the domain is publicly discoverable and users can self-subscribe via POST /domains/:accountName/subscribe. When false, its existence is hidden from unauthenticated users — it cannot be joined without a direct invite.
- totpSecrets: A subdocument with select: false in the Mongoose schema. It is never returned in API responses by default and must be explicitly re-selected with .select("+totpSecrets") in queries that need it.
- ssoConfig.clientSecret: Similarly select: false — the OIDC client secret is scrubbed from all default queries and must be explicitly selected with .select("+ssoConfig.clientSecret").

-----

🔗 The Tripartite Relationship (Account ↔ Domain ↔ Building)

Because CrowdVision separates the Auth and Twin databases, multi-tenancy must be enforced symmetrically across both sides of the service boundary without any cross-database joins.



The link between all three is the domain name string — not a database reference. This is a deliberate design choice. Neither service needs to query the other’s database to enforce access: the domain name is embedded directly in the JWT payload, and the Twin Service queries Building.find({ domains: domainName }) against its own database without ever contacting the Auth Service.

How isolation is enforced end-to-end:

1. User logs in → Auth Service embeds their memberships array into the JWT.
2. User calls GET /twin/buildings/:domain → Twin Service verifies the JWT locally and checks that the domain param matches one of the accountMemberships in the payload.
3. If authorized, it queries Building.find({ domains: domain }) — entirely within its own database.
4. No HTTP call between services occurs at any point.

-----

📋 Domain Creation Side Effects

When a new domain is created via POST /domains, three things happen atomically:

1. The Domain document is created with freshly generated totpSecrets for all roles at or above business_staff (i.e., business_staff, business_admin, and admin).
2. The creator’s Account is immediately granted a business_admin membership for the new domain via $push.
3. The Notification Service is called to initialize default push notification preferences for the creator.

OIDC domains follow the same creation flow. The ssoConfig subdocument is only persisted when authStrategy is "oidc" and the config is provided — it is omitted entirely for "internal" domains.

-----

🔐 Authentication Strategies

Organizations have wildly different security requirements. The authStrategy field dictates how CrowdVision handles sign-ins for a domain.

Strategy 1: internal

The default. User credentials are hashed with bcrypt and stored in the Auth DB. Domain membership is managed via the TOTP invite system (see Users and Accounts.

One important constraint: OIDC domains explicitly block manual subscription. Calling POST /domains/:accountName/subscribe against a domain with authStrategy: "oidc" throws a NotFoundError. Membership in OIDC domains can only be granted via the SSO callback flow.

Strategy 2: oidc (Enterprise SSO)

For enterprise clients using platforms like Keycloak, Auth0, or Entra ID, CrowdVision acts as an OIDC relying party. When a domain uses oidc, it must have its ssoConfig populated:

	export interface ISSOConfig {
	    issuerUrl: string;   // e.g. "https://keycloak.company.com/realms/main"
	    clientId: string;
	    clientSecret: string; // select: false — never returned by default queries
	}

The SSO Flow Is Account-Linking, Not Signup

A critical design detail: the SSO flow requires the user to already have a CrowdVision account. The GET /auth/sso/login/:domainName endpoint is protected by requireAuthentication. The OIDC callback does not create a new account — it updates the memberships array of the existing authenticated account, replacing any prior membership for that domain with the role resolved from the IdP.

IdP Group → CrowdVision Role Mapping

During the callback, processSSOCallback reads the groups claim from the IdP’s ID token and maps it to a CrowdVision role using the following priority order:

admin, platform-adminbusiness-admin, business_adminstaff, business-staff(anything else / no group)IdP Group Name(s)adminbusiness_adminbusiness_staffstandard_customerMapped CrowdVision Role

Configure your IdP to include a groups claim in the ID token with one of the group names above. Any user not matching a group defaults to standard_customer.

-----

⚠️ Known Security Issue: State Parameter Encoding

The documentation intent describes storing PKCE state server-side. The current implementation does not do this. The generateSSOLoginUrl function encodes the PKCE verifier, username, and domain name directly into the OAuth state parameter as base64 JSON:

	// authentication.ts — generateSSOLoginUrl()
	// TODO: In production, store this in Redis/Session. Currently we use base64 for statelessness.
	const statePayload = JSON.stringify({
	    cv_username: accountName,
	    domain: domainName,
	    cv_verifier: code_verifier,  // ← PKCE verifier is client-side
	});
	const state = Buffer.from(statePayload).toString("base64");

This has two concrete problems:

1. The PKCE verifier is exposed client-side. Because the verifier travels to the browser (inside the redirect URL’s state query parameter) and back, it is not kept server-side. A man-in-the-middle who intercepts the redirect can extract the verifier and use the authorization code themselves, defeating the purpose of PKCE.

2. 

3. State tampering is possible. Anyone can craft a base64-encoded state payload with an arbitrary domain or cv_username and submit a spoofed callback. The callback handler trusts whatever is in the decoded state.

Warning
-----
Roadmap Fix Required: Replace the base64 state encoding with a short-lived Redis key. generateSSOLoginUrl should store { verifier, domainName, accountName } under a random opaque key with a TTL of ~10 minutes and pass only that key as the state parameter. processSSOCallback should look up and immediately delete the Redis entry. This is the standard secure pattern for PKCE state management.
