entries.title: Buildings
entries.content:
Buildings

The Building domain model is the physical core of CrowdVision’s digital twin paradigm. It is entirely responsible for storing the 3D spatial layout of a facility alongside the static operational parameters of its rooms.

-----

🏢 The Building Aggregate

The Building acts as the Aggregate Root for all spatial data, managed by the Twin Service in its own isolated twin-db.

	export interface IBuilding {
	    id: string;
	    name: string;
	    rooms: Room[];
	    domains: string[];
	}

The Decoupled Multi-Tenancy Design

Notice that the domains array contains plain strings, not MongoDB ObjectId references.

- Why we did this: The Twin Service operates on a completely isolated database (twin-db). It cannot execute a join against the Auth Service’s Domain collection. By storing the domain name directly as a string, the Twin Service can independently authorize requests by matching these strings against the domain names embedded in the user’s JWT — with zero inter-service communication.

-----

🚪 The Room Entity

A Building is composed of an array of Room entities stored as embedded subdocuments. Embedding ensures that fetching a building returns its complete 3D geometry in a single query.

	export interface Room {
	    id: string;
	    name: string;
	    capacity: number;
	    position: Coordinates;
	    dimensions: Dimensions;
	    color?: string;
	}

The Twin Service Room is intentionally sparse. It stores only structural and organizational data — identity, capacity, visual styling, and spatial placement. It does not store any live sensor readings or safety thresholds.

Both name fields (IBuilding.name and Room.name) default to their respective id if left blank, both at schema level and via a backfillNames function that runs on every read, patching legacy records on the fly without a migration script.

-----

📐 Spatial Value Objects

To render the 3D Digital Twin on the frontend using TresJS, the backend must provide exact spatial coordinates. CrowdVision strictly types these as Value Objects with _id: false in their Mongoose schemas — they have no identity of their own and only exist as descriptors of the Room.



- Coordinates (x, y, z): The exact center-point of the room block in the 3D scene.
- Dimensions (width, height, depth): The volumetric size of the room block.

-----

🗂️ The Three-Layer Data Model

A complete “room view” as seen by the frontend is assembled from three separate data sources, each living in a different service and database.



- Static / Structural (twin-db): The spatial layout and capacity. Changes only when a building is reconfigured.
- Threshold / Safety (sensor-db, BuildingThreshold collection): Per-room and per-building maxTemperature limits. Changes when an admin edits room safety parameters.
- Live / Operational (sensor-db, Temperature and PeopleCount collections): Time-series readings from the IoT sensors. Updated every ~10 seconds by the simulator or physical devices.

The Twin Service owns the first layer. The Sensor Service owns both the second and third.

-----

🪞 The Sensor Service’s BuildingThreshold Shadow

To evaluate whether a temperature reading exceeds a room’s safety threshold, the Sensor Service needs to know each room’s maxTemperature limit. It has two options: query the Twin Service on every incoming sensor reading, or maintain a local copy with only the fields it needs.

CrowdVision uses the second approach. The Sensor Service stores a lightweight BuildingThreshold document in its own database — a shadow of the building containing only alerting-relevant data.

	// sensor-service/src/models/buildingThreshold.ts
	export interface BuildingThreshold {
	    buildingId: string;
	    maxTemperature: number;   // building-level default (default: 27°C)
	    rooms: ThresholdRoom[];
	}
	export interface ThresholdRoom {
	    id: string;
	    maxTemperature: number;   // room-level override
	}

This shadow is kept in sync automatically. The Twin Service calls syncBuildingClone via an internal HTTP call to PUT /sensor/thresholds/buildings/:buildingId after every operation that mutates a building:

- POST /twin/register — creates the threshold clone with the building’s rooms
- PATCH /twin/building/:buildingId — updates the clone (can optionally pass a new maxTemperature)
- PATCH /twin/building/:buildingId/room/:roomId — re-syncs the clone to reflect updated room metadata

Threshold Resolution Priority

When a temperature signal arrives, resolveThreshold applies a three-level fallback:



Building-Level Threshold Cascade

When a building’s maxTemperature is updated via PATCH /twin/building/:buildingId, the Sensor Service applies a cascade rule: any room whose current threshold exactly equals the previous building-level value is also updated to the new value. Rooms with a custom per-room override are left untouched.

This means a building’s maxTemperature acts as a template value for all rooms that have not been individually configured.

-----

⚙️ The Digital Twin Lifecycle (CRUD)

Managing buildings is a two-step lifecycle process.

1. Deployment (POST /twin/register)

A building cannot be created room-by-room via the UI. The entire physical structure must be defined in a JSON payload containing the full array of rooms with their coordinates and dimensions. An administrator uploads this via the Dashboard, which POSTs it to the Twin Service.

On registration, the Twin Service immediately calls syncBuildingClone to create the corresponding BuildingThreshold record in the Sensor Service with the default maxTemperature of 27°C applied to all rooms.

2. Runtime Mutations

Once deployed, physical spaces often change. A lecture hall might have its capacity reduced for an exam. Instead of forcing a full re-upload, the Twin Service exposes targeted endpoints:

PATCH /twin/building/:buildingIdPATCH /twin/building/:buildingId/room/:roomIdEndpointBuilding-level fields: name, domains, maxTemperature (forwarded to sensor clone).Room-level fields: id, name, color, capacity. Re-syncs the threshold clone automatically.What it updates

Note
-----
maxTemperature is not a field on the twin-service Room model and is not stored in twin-db. To update a room’s temperature threshold, use the Sensor Service endpoints directly: PATCH /sensor/thresholds/buildings/:buildingId (building-level) or PATCH /sensor/thresholds/buildings/:buildingId/rooms/:roomId (room-level).

entries.title: Notifications
entries.content:
Notifications

CrowdVision’s notification system is designed around a fundamental distinction: users who are actively watching the dashboard need a different delivery mechanism than users who are away from their browser. The system addresses both with two parallel, independently-managed channels.

-----

📡 Two Delivery Channels



Channel 1 — WebSocket (active users): The Notification Service publishes the alert payload to a Redis notifications channel. The Socket Service, which holds the persistent Socket.IO connections, subscribes to this channel and forwards every message to all connected browsers via io.emit('notification', data). The client stores incoming events in a reactive socketState.notifications array, incrementing an unread badge counter on the notification bell.

Channel 2 — Web Push / VAPID (offline users): The Notification Service uses the web-push library and VAPID keys to push an encrypted payload directly to the browser’s push endpoint. A registered service worker (/service-worker.js) wakes up to display an OS-level notification, even if the CrowdVision tab is closed or the browser is minimized.

Both channels fire on every alert. A user who is actively watching the dashboard receives both a real-time in-app update and an OS notification simultaneously.

-----

🗃️ The Two-Model Design

The Notification Service stores two separate documents per user in notification-db, deliberately kept apart.



WebPushSubscription answers “what device can I push to?” — it stores the browser-issued push endpoint URL and the VAPID encryption keys (p256dh and auth). The endpoint field is unique. A single user can have multiple records if they use multiple browsers or devices.

NotificationSubscription answers “what does this user want to receive?” — it stores per-domain, per-type opt-in preferences. The compound index { accountName, domainName } is unique, so there is exactly one preferences document per user per domain.

The separation is intentional. A user can disable temperature alerts for a specific domain without losing their push endpoint registration. Conversely, if a user’s push subscription expires, only WebPushSubscription needs to be rebuilt — their domain preferences are untouched.

Warning
-----
Known interface mismatch: The INotificationSubscription TypeScript interface declares the field as notificationTypes: SubscriptionPreference[], but the Mongoose schema defines it as preferences. All runtime queries and upserts use preferences, so the interface declaration is stale. It should be renamed to match.

-----

🔔 The Subscription Setup Flow

A user subscribing to push notifications follows this sequence entirely client-side before any server call is made:



The VAPID key rotation check (areKeysEqual) prevents stale subscriptions from silently failing. If the server’s public key has changed since the user last subscribed (e.g., after a key rotation), the old subscription is discarded and rebuilt before being sent to the server.

-----

📤 Alert Dispatch: Automated vs. Manual

Two distinct code paths produce alerts, with different triggering logic and throttling behaviour.

Automated: Temperature Alert (POST /notification/push/temperature)

Called internally by the Sensor Service whenever a temperature reading exceeds a room’s threshold. This path has a 5-minute Redis TTL cooldown per buildingId + roomId pair, preventing notification spam from high-frequency sensor readings.

The cache key format is: temp_alert:{buildingId}:{roomId}

If the key exists in Redis, the request is silently acknowledged with 200 OK and no push is sent. After a successful dispatch, the key is written with EX 300.

When no domainName is provided in the request body, the service resolves the target domains by calling GET /twin/domain/:buildingId on the Twin Service through the internal gateway.

Manual: General Alert (POST /notification/trigger)

Triggered by an administrator from the dashboard. This path has no cooldown — every call dispatches immediately. It:

1. Resolves the building’s domains via the Twin Service (GET /twin/domain/:buildingName).
2. Publishes to the Redis notifications channel for live WebSocket delivery.
3. Sends a Web Push to all opted-in users in each resolved domain.

-----

🔇 Server-Side Domain Filtering: A Gap

The Socket Service broadcasts all Redis messages to every connected client with a single io.emit('notification', data):

	// socket-service/src/index.ts
	await redisSubscriber.subscribe('notifications', (message) => {
	    const data = JSON.parse(message);
	    io.emit('notification', data);  // ← no domain filter
	});

The notification payload includes a domainName field, but the Socket Service does not use it to route messages to specific rooms or namespaces. Every connected user receives every notification regardless of domain membership. Domain-based filtering is the responsibility of the frontend.

Warning
-----
Roadmap Fix: The Socket Service should be extended to use Socket.IO rooms keyed by domainName. On connection, the frontend would emit join_room(domainName) for each of the user’s memberships (the join_room handler already exists in the socket service). The broadcast would then become io.to(domainName).emit('notification', data), eliminating cross-domain notification leakage.

-----

🧹 Stale Subscription Cleanup

The webpush.sendNotification call can fail with two meaningful HTTP status codes from the push provider:

- 410 Gone — the browser has permanently unregistered this endpoint (user revoked permission or cleared site data).
- 403 Forbidden — the subscription is invalid or the VAPID credentials are wrong.

In both cases, pushService.ts automatically calls Subscription.deleteOne({ endpoint }) to remove the stale record from notification-db. No manual cleanup is needed.

All other push errors (network failures, server-side 500s) are logged but do not remove the subscription, since they may be transient.

entries.title: Sensor Data Communication
entries.content:
Sensor Data Communication

To ensure high throughput and low latency, the system utilizes a unidirectional data tunnel orchestrated via Redis Pub/Sub. The architecture strictly separates concerns: production, filtering, and distribution are handled by three independent services that never call each other directly.

-----

Core Components

1. Sensor Service (The Producer) — Blindly emits the absolute truth of the physical world.
	- Publishes full telemetry payloads to telemetry:raw.
	- Each message includes a type field identifying the metric ("temperature", "peopleCount", "airQuality").

2. 

3. Contracts Service (The Filter) — A Rust/Axum microservice that acts as the intelligent gatekeeper.
	- Subscribes to telemetry:raw.
	- Maintains an O(1) in-memory DashMap mapping each Building ID to its list of allowed metric types.
	- For each incoming message, looks up which buildings have subscribed to that metric type and republishes the payload to their dedicated channel.
	- Publishes the tailored payload to telemetry:filtered:<building_id>.

4. 

5. Socket Service (The Distributor) — Stateless WebSocket relay.
	- When a client connects via WebSocket, it subscribes to telemetry:filtered:<building_id>.
	- Pushes data directly down the TCP socket to the browser.

-----

Sequence Diagram



-----

Why Type-Based Filtering?

The raw telemetry payload is a flat JSON object with a type discriminator field:

	{ "type": "temperature", "buildingId": "alpha", "roomId": "r1", "timestamp": 1718000000, "value": 22.4 }

The Contracts Service checks whether payload["type"] is in the building’s allowed_columns list. If it is, the entire payload is forwarded unchanged. This approach:

- Preserves context: Downstream consumers always receive roomId, timestamp, and buildingId — they never need to re-fetch spatial context.
- Keeps the filter O(1): A Vec::contains on a short list of metric keys is faster than any field-by-field JSON manipulation.
- Scales to N buildings: Because fan-out is performed by iterating the DashMap, a single telemetry:raw message can be simultaneously forwarded to multiple buildings that share the same metric subscription — with no duplicate Redis subscriptions.

-----

Frontend Subscription Pattern

The Vue client listens for telemetry on a single "telemetry" Socket.IO event (not per-channel). The socket-service emits this event only to the building:<id> room, so each client only receives data for buildings it has joined.

Lifecycle rules enforced in useSensorData:

1. On buildingId change: emit('subscribe_building', id) to join the Socket.IO room.
2. Register handler via socket.on('telemetry', telemetryHandler) — always capturing the exact function reference.
3. On cleanup: socket.off('telemetry', telemetryHandler) — must match the same reference to prevent listener accumulation.
4. Data mutations are batched to the next requestAnimationFrame via triggerRef(), decoupling Vue’s scheduler from the Three.js render loop. This is critical for the digital twin view’s 60fps camera animation.

-----

Channel Naming Convention

telemetry:rawtelemetry:filtered:<building_id>alerts:temperatureChannelsensor-servicecontracts-servicesensor-servicePublishercontracts-servicesocket-servicenotification-serviceSubscriberFull sensor payload for all buildingsFiltered payload for one specific buildingThreshold violation eventsContent

entries.title: Setting Up the Environment
entries.content:
Setting Up the Environment

Welcome to the CrowdVision developer setup guide. Because CrowdVision is a multi-service architecture with real-time sockets, push notifications, and AI capabilities, getting the local environment running requires setting up several databases, environment variables, and cryptographic secrets.

Don’t worry — we have provided scripts to automate the hardest parts.

-----

1. Prerequisites

Install these two tools first — everything else depends on them:

- just (cross-platform task runner — install at https://just.systems/)
- mise — multi-language toolchain manager. The repo root contains a .mise.toml that pins Node 24, Python 3.12, Rust stable, moon, and uv. Run mise install once after cloning and all tools are at the correct versions on any OS. Install at https://mise.jdx.dev/.

Once just and mise are available, the remaining tools can be managed by mise automatically or installed manually:

- Node.js v24, Python 3.12, Rust (stable) — handled by mise install.
- uv — handled by mise install (or manually at https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).
- Docker & Docker Compose (for running the isolated databases and services)
- Git
- A local Kubernetes cluster + kubectl (only if you plan to deploy via k8s)

Note
-----
For a deep dive on how mise resolves tool versions (and why every script runs through mise exec), and how moon orchestrates per-project tasks with caching and affected-detection, see The Toolchain: mise & moon.

-----

2. Installation

Clone the repository, then install toolchain versions and all language-specific dependencies:

	git clone https://github.com/NickGhignatti/crowd-vision.git
	cd crowd-vision
	mise install   # installs Node 24, Python 3.12, Rust stable, moon, uv (requires mise)
	just install

just install installs every language’s dependencies in parallel (via scripts/install.mjs): Node deps for tooling/, tooling/eslint-config/ (the @crowdvision/eslint-config shared ESLint package), the client, every server service, and the sensor simulator; plus the Python and Rust services’ dependencies. The exact set of packages is derived from moon’s project registry (.moon/workspace.yml, via moon query projects), so the install list can never drift from the rest of the tooling — see The Toolchain: mise & moon for the full picture. There is no root package.json — each service manages its own node_modules. Shared JS dev tooling (semantic-release, commitlint) lives in tooling/; the shared ESLint flat config is the @crowdvision/eslint-config package at tooling/eslint-config/.

The JS install uses npm ci (not npm install): it installs exactly what each package-lock.json pins and never rewrites the lockfile, so running setup on Windows or macOS can’t accidentally change a Linux-resolved lock. Python uses uv sync --locked and Rust uses cargo fetch. Set JOBS=N to cap parallelism (default 4): JOBS=2 just install.

Lockfiles & Cross-Platform Regeneration

CrowdVision’s CI runs on Linux, so every committed package-lock.json must contain the Linux-specific optional native packages (e.g. @emnapi/*, @unrs/resolver-binding-*) that some dev tools pull in. A plain npm install run on Windows or macOS resolves the lock for your platform and strips those Linux entries — which makes npm ci fail in CI with errors like Missing: @emnapi/core@… from lock file.

To avoid this, never let a plain npm install rewrite a committed lockfile. Regenerate locks only through the dedicated recipe, which forces Linux resolution:

	just clean-install   # wipes every node_modules + lock, regenerates for Linux, reinstalls

To regenerate just one service’s lockfile (e.g. after adding a dependency) without the full wipe:

	npm install --prefix server/twin-service --package-lock-only --cpu=x64 --os=linux

The --cpu=x64 --os=linux flags make npm record the Linux optional tree even when you run the command on another OS; --package-lock-only updates the lock without touching node_modules.

Warning
-----
Never commit a Windows- or macOS-resolved lockfile. If git diff shows optional @emnapi/* / @unrs/* entries being removed from a package-lock.json, you ran a plain npm install — discard it and regenerate with just clean-install (or the per-service command above). Run just deps-check before pushing to confirm every lockfile still passes npm ci.

-----

3. Environment Variables & Secrets

Microservices require secrets to communicate securely, encrypt tokens, and send push notifications. Never commit .env files to version control.

Automatic Setup (Recommended)

Run the interactive setup script. It skips any variable already present in .env, so it is safe to re-run at any time:

	just env

You will be prompted for the following:

MONGO_PORTCLIENT_PORTBACKEND_URLCLIENT_URLVITE_SIMULATOR_URL_AQVITE_SIMULATOR_URL_TPVariable270178080http://localhosthttp://localhost:8080http://localhost/simulator/aqhttp://localhost/simulator/tpDefaultExposed port for MongoDB containers (local Docker only).Port the Vue client is served on.URL of the API gateway. Use http://localhost for Docker dev, http://crowdvision.local for k8s dev.Browser origin used for CORS. Must match where you open the app.Air quality simulator endpoint (external service).Temperature/presence simulator endpoint (external service).Description

The following secrets are generated automatically by just env and written to .env — you do not need to supply them manually:

- JWT_SECRET — used by Auth, Twin, and Notification services to sign/verify JWTs.
- INTERNAL_ADMIN_SECRET — internal service-to-service credential.
- VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY / VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY — web push notification keys.

Warning
-----
Security Note: All microservices that share JWT_SECRET must have the exact same value. If they differ, cross-service authorization will fail with 401 Unauthorized.

Manual VAPID Key Generation

If you need to regenerate VAPID keys separately:

	node scripts/env/vapid.js

Copy the output into your .env file.

-----

4. Running Locally with Docker Compose

With your .env set up, start the full development stack:

	just dev

This starts all services with hot-reloading. When you save a source file, the change is reflected inside the running container instantly — no manual restart needed.

Skip services you are not actively working on:

	just dev exclude="simulator"   # skip the simulator containers
	just dev exclude="client"      # skip the Vue client (e.g. run it separately via Vite)

See the Running the Application page for the full Docker Compose reference.

-----

5. Bootstrapping the System (Provisioning)

With empty databases you cannot log in. Run the provisioning script to create the initial Global Admin account and default Domain:

	node scripts/ops/provision.js

Run this once after just dev reports all services as healthy. The script prompts you for the admin email, password, and domain name, then calls the live auth-service to register them.

-----

6. Kubernetes Deployment

If you are deploying CrowdVision to a local or remote Kubernetes cluster, follow the steps below. For ongoing day-to-day k8s operations, see the Kubernetes Configuration page.

Step 1: Create the k3d Cluster (run once per machine)

	k3d cluster create crowdvision \
	  --servers 1 \
	  --agents 3 \
	  --api-port 127.0.0.1:6550 \
	  -p "80:80@loadbalancer" \
	  -p "443:443@loadbalancer" \
	  --k3s-arg "--disable=traefik@server:*"

--servers 1--agents 3--api-port 127.0.0.1:6550-p "80:80@loadbalancer"--k3s-arg "--disable=traefik@server:*"FlagOne control plane node.Three worker nodes.Binds the K8s API server explicitly to localhost. Required on Windows — prevents kubectl connection failures when host.docker.internal resolves to a non-loopback IP.Maps host port 80 to the k3d load balancer so http://localhost/ reaches the nginx Ingress controller.Disables the default Traefik ingress. CrowdVision uses nginx instead.Purpose

Verify the cluster is reachable:

	kubectl cluster-info
	kubectl get nodes
	# All nodes should show STATUS: Ready

Step 2: Configure Local DNS

	just k8s-hosts
	# Prints: 127.0.0.1   crowdvision.local

Open your hosts file as Administrator and add that line:

- Windows: C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts
- Linux/Mac: /etc/hosts

Step 3: Add GHCR Credentials to .env

The cluster needs credentials to pull images from GitHub Container Registry. Add to your .env:

	GHCR_USERNAME=nickghignatti
	GHCR_TOKEN=<your GitHub PAT with read:packages scope>

Create a PAT at https://github.com/settings/tokens with read:packages scope.

Step 4: Full Bootstrap

	just k8s-setup

This single command runs in order:

1. Installs the nginx Ingress Controller into ingress-nginx namespace.
2. Waits until the Ingress controller pod is Running.
3. Creates the crowdvision namespace.
4. Runs just k8s-secrets — creates all Kubernetes Secret objects from your .env.
5. Applies ConfigMaps, StatefulSets (databases), Deployments, Ingress, and monitoring.

Warning
-----
just k8s-secrets must run after the namespace exists. just k8s-setup handles this ordering automatically. If you run just k8s-secrets manually before creating the namespace, it will fail with “namespaces not found”.

Accessing the Platform

After just k8s-setup completes, the platform is available at http://crowdvision.local. All services route through the nginx Ingress controller on port 80.

Vue FrontendAuth APITwin APISensor APISocket.IOGrafanaPrometheusInterfacehttp://crowdvision.local/http://crowdvision.local/auth/http://crowdvision.local/twin/http://crowdvision.local/sensor/http://crowdvision.local/socket.io/http://crowdvision.local/grafana/http://crowdvision.local/prometheus/URL

entries.title: The Toolchain: mise, moon & just
entries.content:
The Toolchain: mise, moon & just

CrowdVision is a polyglot monorepo: TypeScript services, a Python AI agent, a Rust contracts service, and shared JavaScript tooling. To keep that manageable on any operating system, the developer experience rests on three cooperating layers, each with a single, well-defined responsibility.

justmisemoonLayerThe human-facing command entrypoint. Every workflow is a just <recipe>. It also drives the tasks the layers below do not — Docker, Kubernetes, and documentation.Tool versions. The single source of truth for which Node, Python, Rust, uv, and moon you run, on every platform.Per-project task orchestration — build, test, lint, audit, deps — with result caching and affected-detection.ResponsibilityJustfile.mise.toml.moon/Configuration

Underneath, two small Node scripts (scripts/install.mjs, scripts/clean.mjs) handle dependency installation, the one job that does not fit moon’s model. Everything else is mise plus moon, fronted by just.



-----

The problem this solves

Before this setup, tools were resolved from whatever happened to be on the developer’s PATH. That is fragile: a globally installed Node (from nvm) or Rust (from rustup) silently shadows the versions the project expects, and a tool with no global fallback — notably uv — simply fails:

	'uv' is not recognized as an internal or external command

The governing principle of this page follows from that failure: never trust the ambient PATH. Resolve every tool through mise, and let one configuration file be the source of truth for each concern.

-----

just — the task entrypoint

just is a command runner: a Justfile of named recipes that wrap the longer commands a developer would otherwise memorise. It is the only layer a contributor interacts with directly; mise and moon sit behind it.

Two design points matter:

- Cross-platform shell. The Justfile selects bash on Unix and PowerShell on Windows, so the same recipe name works on every machine.
- Tools are always wrapped. Every recipe that calls a mise-managed tool prefixes it with mise exec -- (see below). Tools that mise does not manage — docker, k3d, kubectl — are invoked directly.

Run just --list to see every recipe. The recipe catalogue is documented in Running the Application.

-----

mise — the toolchain manager

mise pins every language runtime and CLI to an exact version, per project, across platforms. The pins live in .mise.toml at the repository root:

	[tools]
	node = "24"
	python = "3.12"
	rust = "stable"
	"ubi:moonrepo/moon" = "latest"
	uv = "latest"

Running mise install once after cloning fetches every tool at the pinned version into mise’s own store, independent of anything else on the machine.

How tools are resolved

mise can place those tools on the PATH in two ways. The project is designed to work with or without the first.

Shell activationmise exec --MechanismInteractive work in your own terminal.Scripts, CI, and the Justfile — anywhere that must work without assuming activation.When it is usedmise activate pwsh | Out-String | Invoke-Expression (PowerShell), or mise activate bash/zsh. Added to a shell profile, the pinned tools are then on PATH directly.mise exec -- <command> resolves the tool from .mise.toml and runs it, injecting the pinned tools onto PATH for that command and every child process it spawns.How

Activation is optional
-----
You do not need to activate mise for just to work. Every recipe that calls a managed tool already wraps it in mise exec --, so a fresh clone with only just and mise installed runs immediately. Activation is purely a convenience for invoking tools by hand.

The same rule inside scripts

The Node scripts that just launches (install, clean) spawn tools of their own. They route those through the same gate with a small helper, scripts/lib/mise.mjs:

	// Resolve a command's tool through mise — unless a mise-provided environment is
	// already present (the Justfile launches scripts under `mise exec`, which exports
	// __MISE_DIFF), in which case we avoid wrapping it twice.
	export function withMise(cmd) {
	  const inside = process.env.__MISE_DIFF || process.env.MISE_SHELL;
	  return inside ? cmd : `mise exec -- ${cmd}`;
	}

A script therefore works both when launched via just (already under mise exec) and when run directly as node scripts/install.mjs on a shell that never activated mise.

Diagnosing “command not found”
-----
If any tool reports “command not found” (classically uv), something bypassed mise. Run it through mise exec -- <tool>, or activate mise in your shell. The tool is installed; it is simply not on the un-activated PATH.

-----

moon — the task orchestrator

moon runs the per-project tasks — build, test, lint, lint-fix, audit, deps — across the whole monorepo, with two capabilities a hand-rolled script set never had:

- Caching — a task’s result is hashed by its inputs. If nothing it depends on changed, the cached result is replayed in milliseconds instead of re-running.
- Affected detection — --affected runs a task only for the projects touched on the current branch.

One project registry

Every package is registered once in .moon/workspace.yml. This is the single list of packages in the repository; nothing else maintains a parallel copy.

	projects:
	  client: "client"
	  auth-service: "server/auth-service"
	  twin-service: "server/twin-service"
	  # … the other services …
	  contracts-service: "server/contracts-service"
	  sensor-simulator: "simulators/sensor-simulator"
	  # Support packages: deps-only, no build/test/lint
	  tooling: "tooling"
	  eslint-config: "tooling/eslint-config"
	  tests: "server/tests"

Tasks defined once per language

Rather than repeat the same build/test/lint configuration in a dozen moon.yml files, the pipeline is defined once per language in .moon/tasks/*.yml, each scoped with inheritedBy so it applies only to projects of that language.

.moon/tasks/typescript.yml.moon/tasks/python.yml.moon/tasks/rust.yml.moon/tasks/javascript.ymlFilelanguage: typescriptlanguage: pythonlanguage: rustlanguage: javascriptApplies tobuild, test, lint, lint-fix, deps, audittest, lint, lint-fix, deps, auditbuild, test, lint, deps, auditdeps only — for support packagesTasks defined

A language file is scoped by its inheritedBy block:

	# .moon/tasks/typescript.yml
	inheritedBy:
	  language: 'typescript'
	tasks:
	  test:
	    command: 'npm test'
	    inputs: ['src/**/*', '__tests__/**/*', 'package.json']
	  audit:
	    command: 'npm audit --audit-level=high --omit=dev'
	    options: { cache: false }
	  # … build, lint, lint-fix, deps …

Why npm test for the Python service
-----
The agent-service package.json proxies npm test to uv run pytest and npm run lint to uv run ruff … && pyright. This gives moon one uniform task interface across languages while the real work still runs through uv.

Each project’s moon.yml is minimal

Because the pipeline is inherited, a standard service’s entire moon configuration is a single line declaring its language; the tasks come from the language file. Projects declare only what genuinely differs:

clientsensor-simulatortooling, eslint-config, testsProjectUses Vite/Vitest: overrides build inputs and test (to npm run test:unit), with mergeArgs: replace / mergeInputs: replace so the overrides replace the inherited values rather than appending.Has no unit suite wired into CI, so it excludes the inherited test task.Tagged language: 'javascript' so they inherit only deps. (server/tests holds an integration-only Jest suite that runs via just test-integration, never as a moon :test.)Override

Inspecting the graph

These read-only commands print resolved configuration without running anything:

	mise exec -- moon task auth-service:test   # one task: command, inputs, inheritance
	mise exec -- moon query projects           # every project moon knows (id + language)
	mise exec -- moon query tasks              # which projects expose each task

-----

How the layers cooperate

The payoff of the design is that each fact lives in exactly one place.

Which version of Node, uv, or Rust?Which packages exist in the repository?How is a TypeScript project tested, linted, or audited?Question.mise.toml.moon/workspace.yml.moon/tasks/typescript.ymlAnswered by

Crucially, install and clean derive their package list from moon rather than keeping a parallel hand-written list. scripts/lib/workspaces.mjs shells out to moon query projects and buckets the result by language:

	const { projects } = JSON.parse(execSync(withMise('moon query projects')));

So just install installs exactly the packages moon knows about; no drift is possible.

Why install and clean stay scripts
-----
install and clean are scripts, not moon tasks, on purpose: npm ci wipes and reinstalls node_modules, which is not a cacheable artifact, and making moon manage installs would require enabling moon’s own toolchain — downloading a second Node and conflicting with mise as the single source of truth. So moon owns build/test/lint/audit/deps; the scripts own install/clean; mise owns versions.

-----

Adding a new service or package

Thanks to the single registry, onboarding a package is a one-place change:

1. Register it in .moon/workspace.yml under projects:.
2. Add a minimal moon.yml in the package with its language: (and any genuine task overrides).
3. It now flows automatically into :build / :test / :lint / :audit / :deps, and into just install / just clean-install (both derive from moon query projects).

Choose the language deliberately
-----
typescript / python / rust inherit the full build/test/lint pipeline — use them only for packages that have those scripts. Use javascript for support packages that need dependency installs but have no build/test/lint (such as tooling).

-----

Troubleshooting

'uv' is not recognized / command not foundlink.exe not found during cargo (Windows)just test / just lint re-runs everythingA new service is not tested or installedSymptomA tool was called outside mise. Use mise exec -- <tool>, or activate mise. The tool is installed; it is not on the un-activated PATH.Rust needs the MSVC linker. Install Visual Studio Build Tools with the “Desktop development with C++” workload. (macOS needs xcode-select --install; Linux needs build-essential.)Expected on the first run. The second run replays from moon’s cache. Use --affected to scope to changed projects.It is not registered in .moon/workspace.yml, or its moon.yml is missing or has the wrong language. Verify with mise exec -- moon query projects.Cause and fix

entries.title: Docker Compose Orchestration
entries.content:
Docker Compose Orchestration

Locally, the entire platform runs under Docker Compose. There is no single hand-maintained compose file that lists every service; instead, each service owns its own compose fragment, and a small Node orchestrator assembles them into one runtime file on demand. This page explains that mechanism end to end, so a new contributor can predict exactly what just dev does.

-----

The shape of the configuration

Compose configuration is split across three kinds of file.

docker-compose.yml (root)server/<service>/docker-compose.ymlserver/<service>/docker-compose.dev.ymlFileShared infrastructure only — the gateway, broker, observability, and the Langfuse stack. It defines no application service.One per service: the service container and its private database/network.Optional per-service development override (hot-reload watch rules, dev-only ports).Role

The root file holds the pieces every run needs regardless of which services are included:

- gateway — the Caddy reverse proxy on port 80 (see Architecture Overview. Its depends_on entries are marked required: false, so the gateway still starts when a service is excluded and simply retries the connection when that service appears.
- redis — the publish/subscribe broker.
- prometheus and grafana — metrics and dashboards.
- The langfuse-*, clickhouse, and minio stack — the agent’s trace backend.

Why services are not in the root file
-----
Keeping each service’s compose fragment next to its code means the fragment travels with the service if it is ever extracted to its own repository, and a service can be added or excluded without editing a central file. The trade-off — assembling the fragments correctly — is what the orchestrator handles.

-----

The orchestrator

scripts/compose/compose-run.mjs (invoked by the Justfile, never directly) turns the scattered fragments into one runnable configuration.



Why a generated include: file, not multiple -f flags

Passing several files with repeated -f flags makes Compose resolve every relative path (such as build.context: .) against the first file’s directory, which breaks per-service Dockerfile lookups. Compose’s include: directive instead resolves paths relative to each included file, so build.context: . inside server/twin-service/docker-compose.yml correctly points at that service’s directory. The orchestrator therefore writes a small compose.runtime.yml whose body is an include: list:

	# Auto-generated by scripts/compose/compose-run.mjs — do not commit.
	include:
	  - path: docker-compose.yml
	  - path:
	      - server/twin-service/docker-compose.yml
	      - server/twin-service/docker-compose.dev.yml
	  - path: server/auth-service/docker-compose.yml
	  # … one entry per included service …

A single-path entry becomes one sub-project; a multi-path entry merges a service’s base file with its dev override into one sub-project (two separate include entries cannot define the same service — that raises a conflict).

Do not commit the runtime file
-----
compose.runtime.yml is regenerated on every just dev / start / down / build. It is a build artifact, not source — treat it as disposable and never edit it by hand.

How the include list is built

1. Always include the root docker-compose.yml.
2. Walk server/ (skipping tests/) and simulators/, adding each folder’s docker-compose.yml and — in development modes — its docker-compose.dev.yml if present.
3. Add client unless excluded.
4. For the integration mode, also include server/tests/docker-compose.integration.yml.

Excluding services

In dev, dev-build, and start, you can omit services by substring of their folder name. This is how you run a lighter stack when you do not need the agent or the simulators:

	just dev exclude="agent"            # skip agent-service, agent-db, agent-ingester
	just dev exclude="agent simulator"  # also skip aq-simulator, sensor-simulator

The down, integration, and build modes always operate on the full set and ignore exclude patterns.

-----

The modes

The first argument to the orchestrator selects the docker compose invocation. Each maps to a just recipe.

just devjust dev-buildjust startjust downjust test-integration(CI) compose-run.mjs buildRecipedevdev-buildstartdownintegrationbuildModeup --watch --remove-orphans — hot-reload; rebuilds/syncs containers as source changes.up --watch --build — as dev, but forces an image rebuild first.up --build -d --remove-orphans — production-style, detached, in the background.down --remove-orphans — stop and remove everything.up --build --exit-code-from integration-tests --abort-on-container-exit.build only — used by the ci-docker workflow to validate the whole stack builds.Resulting command

The orchestrator runs docker compose with COMPOSE_BAKE=true, which delegates builds to BuildKit Bake for faster, parallel image builds.

Same assembly in CI
-----
The ci-docker workflow builds the stack through the very same orchestrator (build mode). Path resolution in CI is therefore identical to local development, so “it builds on my machine” and “it builds in CI” cannot diverge over compose path handling.

-----

Environment and the dev loop

Every stack-starting recipe (dev, dev-build, start) depends on the env recipe, which regenerates .env (VAPID keys, JWT secret, admin credentials, Langfuse secrets) before Compose runs — see Setting Up Your Environment.

Typical local loop:

	just dev                 # full stack with hot-reload
	just dev exclude="agent" # lighter stack without the AI agent
	just logs auth-service   # follow one service's logs
	just down                # tear it all down

just logs [service] follows logs against the generated compose.runtime.yml, so it targets exactly the services the last run started.

-----

Related operations

just db-clearjust db-clear-sensorjust test-integrationRecipeDrop the auth, twin, notification, and agent databases (MongoDB plus the agent’s Postgres).Drop only the sensor database.Bring up the full stack plus the integration test container, exit with its status, then tear down.Purpose

For the production-grade orchestration (StatefulSets, ingress, secrets), see Kubernetes Configuration; for the runtime topology and gateway routing, see the Architecture Overview.

headings.text: Why a generated include: file, not multiple -f flags
How the include list is built

entries.title: Kubernetes Configuration
entries.content:
Kubernetes Configuration

Kubernetes is the intended environment for staging and production, providing scaling, self-healing, and stateful data management. This page is the operational guide — how to deploy, inspect, and update the cluster. For the cluster’s internal design (StatefulSets, ingress rewriting, init containers), see Deployment & Kubernetes.

All Kubernetes operations run through just recipes from the repository root, so you do not need to memorise long kubectl invocations. The recipes assume a running cluster (k3d, k3s, Minikube, or a managed provider) with your kubectl context set correctly.

-----

First-time setup and the core lifecycle

	# First time on a new cluster — installs the ingress controller, creates the
	# namespace, injects secrets from .env, then applies all manifests:
	just k8s-setup
	# Re-apply all manifests after a change (idempotent — safe to run repeatedly):
	just k8s-apply
	# Tear everything down (deletes the namespace and ALL data inside it):
	just k8s-delete

k8s-delete is destructive
-----
just k8s-delete removes the namespace and every PersistentVolumeClaim in it — all database data is permanently lost, with no undo. To pause without losing data, use just k8s-stop and later just k8s-start.

-----

Deployment order

Manifests must be applied in dependency order, because a service crashes if its database is not yet reachable. just k8s-apply applies them in exactly this sequence; the manual equivalent is shown for understanding.

[DynamicValue(unwrappedValue=Namespace, evaluationContext=com.quarkdown.core.context.SubdocumentContext@d005ed8)][DynamicValue(unwrappedValue=Configuration, evaluationContext=com.quarkdown.core.context.SubdocumentContext@d005ed8)][DynamicValue(unwrappedValue=Stateful layer, evaluationContext=com.quarkdown.core.context.SubdocumentContext@d005ed8)][DynamicValue(unwrappedValue=Deployments, evaluationContext=com.quarkdown.core.context.SubdocumentContext@d005ed8)][DynamicValue(unwrappedValue=Ingress and monitoring, evaluationContext=com.quarkdown.core.context.SubdocumentContext@d005ed8)]Stepk8s/namespace.ymlk8s/configmaps/k8s/stateful/k8s/deployments/k8s/ingress.yml, k8s/monitoring/ManifestsCreates the isolated crowdvision namespace everything else lives in.Shared, non-sensitive environment values (service URLs, ports).MongoDB (one per service), the agent’s Postgres, and Redis. Must be Running before the services start.The stateless application services and the client.The nginx routing rules and the Prometheus/Grafana stack.Why

	kubectl apply -f k8s/namespace.yml
	kubectl apply -f k8s/configmaps/
	kubectl apply -f k8s/stateful/      # wait for these pods to reach Running
	kubectl apply -f k8s/deployments/
	kubectl apply -f k8s/ingress.yml
	kubectl apply -f k8s/monitoring/

-----

Secrets

Secrets are created directly on the cluster from your local .env and are never committed. just k8s-secrets reads values such as JWT_SECRET, VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY, VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY, the agent provider keys, GHCR_USERNAME, and GHCR_TOKEN, and creates the corresponding Secret objects. It is idempotent — safe to re-run after rotating a key.

Namespace before secrets
-----
The namespace must exist before secrets can be created. Run kubectl apply -f k8s/namespace.yml (or the full just k8s-setup) before just k8s-secrets if invoking it in isolation.

Agent provider key naming
-----
The current agent-service-secret generator forwards the legacy GOOGLE_API_KEY and DEEPSEEK_API_KEY names, while the recommended provider setup uses OPENROUTER_API_KEY. Before deploying the agent, confirm the generated secret contains a key compatible with its configured LLM_BASE_URL. Kubernetes also does not deploy the documentation ingester or Langfuse — see the Agent Service guide.

-----

Day-to-day operations

	just k8s-status                 # pods, deployments, services, StatefulSets
	just k8s-logs auth-service      # tail logs (works for Deployments and StatefulSets)
	just k8s-restart auth-service   # rolling restart one service (picks up a new :latest)
	just k8s-restart-all            # restart every deployment (e.g. after rotating secrets)
	just k8s-rollout auth-service   # watch a rolling update to completion
	just k8s-validate               # dry-run every manifest without touching the cluster

A rolling restart starts new pods and waits for their readiness probe to pass before terminating the old ones, so a healthy service never goes fully down. If a rollout misbehaves, roll back instantly:

	kubectl rollout undo deployment/auth-service -n crowdvision

-----

Image management

Production images (GHCR)

	just k8s-build        # build all images, tagged for GHCR
	just k8s-push         # push to GHCR (requires: docker login ghcr.io)
	just k8s-build-push   # build and push in one step

Local k3d (no registry)

Build images locally and import them straight into the k3d cluster, skipping GHCR entirely:

	just k8s-build   # build all images locally
	just k8s-load    # import them into k3d
	just k8s-apply   # apply manifests

Image pull policy for local images
-----
When using locally loaded images, set imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent in the deployment manifests. With Always, Kubernetes attempts a GHCR pull and ignores the locally imported image.

-----

Local DNS

For local k3d access, add the ingress hostname to your machine’s hosts file:

	just k8s-hosts
	# Prints: 127.0.0.1   crowdvision.local

Edit the hosts file as administrator (Windows: C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts; Linux/macOS: /etc/hosts) and add that line.

-----

Recipe reference

just k8s-setupjust k8s-applyjust k8s-secretsjust k8s-build / k8s-push / k8s-build-pushjust k8s-loadjust k8s-statusjust k8s-logs <service>just k8s-restart <service> / k8s-restart-alljust k8s-rollout <service>just k8s-ingressjust k8s-validatejust k8s-stop / k8s-startjust k8s-deletejust k8s-hostsRecipeFirst-time setup: ingress controller, namespace, secrets, all manifests.Apply all manifests in dependency order (idempotent).Create or update secrets from .env.Build, push, or both, for the GHCR-tagged images.Import locally built images into the k3d cluster.Show all workloads in the namespace.Tail a service’s logs.Rolling restart one service, or all deployments.Watch a rolling update to completion.Install or reinstall the nginx Ingress Controller.Dry-run all manifests without touching the cluster.Pause (data preserved) or resume the cluster.Destructive: delete the namespace and all data.Print the line to add to your hosts file.What it does

Manifests are also validated in CI by the ci-k8s workflow (kubeconform, pluto, kube-score) — see CI/CD Pipeline.

headings.text: First-time setup and the core lifecycle

entries.title: Run the Application
entries.content:
Run the Application

CrowdVision can be run in two distinct modes: Docker Compose for everyday local development, and Kubernetes for production-like environments. Both modes are managed via just recipes from the monorepo root.

-----

Docker Compose Mode

Development (Hot-Reloading)

For active development with live code reloading, use:

	just dev

What happens:

1. just env runs first to verify your .env and generate any missing keys.
2. A runtime compose file is generated that includes all service compose files.
3. Docker Compose launches with --watch — saving a file triggers an instant hot-reload inside the running container.
4. All services start: Caddy proxy, Redis, all MongoDB instances, all backend microservices, and the Vue client.

Excluding Services

Run a lighter stack by excluding services you are not working on:

	# Exclude by substring of folder name:
	just dev exclude="simulator"
	# Exclude multiple services (space-separated):
	just dev exclude="simulator client"
	# Exclude the client (useful when running it separately with Vite):
	just dev exclude="client"

Note
-----
Exclusion matches by folder name substring. "simulator" will skip aq-simulator and sensor-simulator. Use a more specific substring to target a single service.

Accessing the Application (Docker mode)

Vue FrontendAPI GatewayAuth DB GUITwin DB GUIGrafanaPrometheusInterfacehttp://localhost:8080http://localhost:80http://localhost/auth-db-gui/http://localhost/twin-db-gui/http://localhost/grafana/http://localhost/prometheus/URLThe main CrowdVision user interface.Caddy proxy — routes to all microservices.Mongo Express for the auth database. Dev mode only.Mongo Express for the twin database. Dev mode only.Metrics dashboards. Dev mode only.Raw metrics scraper. Dev mode only.Description

Production / Detached Mode

	just start

Runs the stack using the base compose configuration in detached mode (-d). No hot-reloading, no database GUIs. Same exclude= syntax works here too.

Stopping & Resetting

	# Graceful shutdown — stops all containers and removes orphans:
	just down
	# Follow logs for all services:
	just logs
	# Follow logs for one service:
	just logs auth-service
	# Wipe all databases (destructive — you will need to re-provision):
	just db-clear
	# Wipe only the sensor database:
	just db-clear-sensor

Warning
-----
just db-clear permanently deletes all persistent Docker volumes. You will need to run node scripts/ops/provision.js again after a clear to recreate the admin account.

-----

Bootstrapping (First-Time Provisioning)

With empty databases you cannot log in. Use the provisioning script to seed the initial Global Admin account and default Domain:

	node scripts/ops/provision.js

Run this once after your services are healthy. The script prompts you for the admin email, password, and domain name, then calls the running auth-service to register them.

-----

Kubernetes Mode

See the Kubernetes Configuration page for the full reference. Quick summary:

First Time: Creating the Cluster

	k3d cluster create crowdvision \
	  --servers 1 \
	  --agents 3 \
	  --api-port 127.0.0.1:6550 \
	  -p "80:80@loadbalancer" \
	  -p "443:443@loadbalancer" \
	  --k3s-arg "--disable=traefik@server:*"

Deploying (Production images from GHCR)

	# First-time full setup (ingress controller → namespace → secrets → manifests):
	just k8s-setup
	# Subsequent deploys — re-apply manifests:
	just k8s-apply

Deploying with Locally Built Images (k3d dev)

	just k8s-build      # build all images locally
	just k8s-load       # import into k3d — no GHCR needed
	just k8s-apply      # apply manifests

Updating a Single Service

	# After code changes — rebuild the image, then rolling restart:
	docker build -t ghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-auth:latest ./server/auth-service
	just k8s-load
	just k8s-restart auth-service
	# Watch the rollout:
	just k8s-rollout auth-service

Managing Cluster Lifecycle

	# Pause the cluster (preserves all pods, volumes, and secrets):
	just k8s-stop
	# Resume from exactly where you left off (~30 seconds):
	just k8s-start
	# Verify everything came back up:
	just k8s-status
	# Destroy the cluster completely (data is permanently lost):
	just k8s-delete

Note
-----
Use just k8s-stop at the end of your working day. All pod state and persistent volume data survives the stop/start cycle — your databases are intact.

Verifying Cluster Health

	# All pods should show 1/1 Running:
	just k8s-status
	# Check persistent volumes are Bound:
	kubectl get pvc -n crowdvision
	# Check Ingress has an ADDRESS:
	kubectl get ingress -n crowdvision
	# Tail logs for a service:
	just k8s-logs auth-service
	# Describe a pod for detailed events (useful for debugging):
	kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n crowdvision

-----

Testing

	# Run all unit tests across all services (moon caches results — unchanged services are instant):
	just test
	# Run only tests for services touched by your current branch:
	just test-affected
	# Per-service unit tests:
	just test-auth
	just test-twin
	just test-sensor
	just test-notification
	just test-socket
	just test-client
	# Full backend integration tests (spins up a composed stack, then tears it down):
	just test-integration

Note
-----
just test and just lint both delegate to moon, which caches task results by input hash. If nothing in a service has changed since the last run, the cached result is replayed in milliseconds. Use just test-affected / just lint-affected on a feature branch to only process the services your changes actually touch.

-----

Auditing & Dependency Checks

Two recipes run a security audit and a lockfile-sync check across every project. Both delegate to moon (moon run :audit / :deps), so they are cached and accept --affected to scope to the projects changed on your branch. See The Toolchain: mise & moon for how these tasks are defined.

	# Security audit — npm audit (--audit-level=high --omit=dev) + uv audit + cargo audit:
	just audit
	# Lockfile-sync check — npm ci + uv sync --locked + cargo check:
	just deps-check
	# Scope either to only the projects changed on your branch:
	mise exec -- moon run :audit --affected

Note
-----
just deps-check is the fastest way to catch a lockfile drift before pushing (the classic npm ci can only install packages when your package.json and package-lock.json are in sync). If it fails, regenerate the offending lockfile with just clean-install (see Setting Up the Environment → Lockfiles & Cross-Platform Regeneration). The Rust cargo audit leg is marked allow-failure: if cargo-audit isn’t installed it is reported but does not fail the run (cargo install cargo-audit to enable it).

-----

Full Just Reference — Docker Compose

just dev [exclude="..."]just start [exclude="..."]just downjust logs [service]just db-clearjust db-clear-sensorjust testjust test-affectedjust test-<service>just test-integrationjust lintjust lint-affectedjust lint-fixjust auditjust deps-checkjust clean-installjust docsjust docs-preview [dir=...]just docs-serveRecipeFull stack with hot-reloading. Optionally exclude services.Full stack in detached/production mode.Graceful shutdown, removes orphan containers.Follow logs. Omit service to tail all.Drop all databases (destructive).Drop only the sensor database.Run all unit tests (moon-cached; unchanged services are instant).Run unit tests only for services changed on the current branch.Run unit tests for one service.Run backend integration tests.Lint every service (moon-cached).Lint only services changed on the current branch.Auto-fix linting issues across every service.Security audit across all projects via moon run :audit (npm + uv + cargo).Lockfile-sync check across all projects via moon run :deps (npm ci + uv sync —locked + cargo check).Wipe all node_modules + lockfiles and regenerate them for Linux, then reinstall.Compile both Quarkdown guides to landing-page/{user,dev} (mirrors CI).Quarkdown live-reloading server for one guide while editing.Build both guides, then live-serve the whole landing-page/ site at :8080.What it does

-----

Debugging Tips

A service is responding with 502 Bad Gateway

The Caddy proxy forwards to the service container but nothing is listening. Most common causes:

1. The dev script in package.json points to a non-existent file.
Every Node.js/TypeScript service must reference src/index.ts via ts-node/esm, not a compiled .js file. Check the service’s package.json:

	# Correct pattern (all TypeScript services):
	"dev": "nodemon --watch src --exec node --no-warnings=ExperimentalWarning --loader ts-node/esm src/index.ts"

If it says src/index.js and that file does not exist, nodemon exits silently and the service never starts — manifesting as 502.

2. Environment variable is empty or wrong.
Open the service logs: just logs <service-name>. Look for connection errors to MongoDB or Redis at startup.

3. Missing .env variable.
Run just env to fill any gaps. The script skips variables already present, so it is safe to re-run.

-----

just dev exclude="..." is not excluding the service

Exclusion works by matching the folder name substring. Examples:

	just dev exclude="simulator"         # skips aq-simulator and sensor-simulator
	just dev exclude="simulator client"  # also skips the Vue client

If exclusion suddenly stops working after editing the Justfile: verify that dev still takes only ONE parameter (exclude=""). Adding a second parameter before exclude in the recipe definition breaks named-argument resolution in just.

-----

Windows: kubectl fails to connect after just k8s-start

After a cluster restart the kubeconfig may point to a stale port. Fix it:

	# Find the actual port the k3d load balancer is using:
	docker ps --filter name=k3d-crowdvision-serverlb
	# Look for the port mapped to 6443 in the PORTS column, e.g. 0.0.0.0:32774->6443/tcp
	# Update the kubeconfig to use the correct port:
	kubectl config set-cluster k3d-crowdvision --server=https://127.0.0.1:<PORT>

-----

just k8s-secrets fails: “namespaces not found”

Secrets are scoped to the crowdvision namespace — it must exist first. Create it manually:

	kubectl apply -f k8s/namespace.yml
	just k8s-secrets

Or use just k8s-setup which handles the ordering automatically.

-----

k3d image pull fails (ImagePullBackOff)

If pods are stuck in ImagePullBackOff, Kubernetes is trying to pull from GHCR instead of using the locally loaded image.

	# Load images from your local Docker daemon into k3d:
	just k8s-build
	just k8s-load

Also check that the deployment manifest sets imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent, not Always — Always forces a remote pull even when the image exists locally.

-----

nginx Ingress rejects configuration-snippet annotation

Newer versions of the nginx Ingress Controller disable snippet annotations by default. If you see:

	annotation nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/configuration-snippet is not allowed

Re-install the ingress controller with snippets enabled:

	helm upgrade ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx \
	  --namespace ingress-nginx \
	  --set controller.allowSnippetAnnotations=true

entries.title: External Simulators
entries.content:
External Simulators

CrowdVision ships two simulators that exist outside the main application cluster. They are not microservices — they are test stand-ins for systems that in production would be real external hardware or third-party services.

Sensor SimulatorAir Quality SimulatorSimulatorsimulators/sensor-simulator/simulators/aq-simulator/LocationPhysical IoT sensors installed in buildings (people count + temperature)External AQ monitoring stations sending air-quality readingsWhat it replaces

-----

Architectural Decision: Keep Simulators External

Decision: Simulators are never included in Kubernetes manifests and are not part of the production system definition.

Why: In production, CrowdVision receives data from physical IoT sensors and third-party AQ stations. Those devices are external — they know nothing about the cluster; they just POST to public API endpoints. The simulators are designed to model exactly this reality.

Running simulators outside Kubernetes enforces that boundary at the architecture level:

- The K8s manifests describe only the real system — no test tooling bleeds into production configs.
- Each simulator connects to the cluster the same way real hardware would: via the public-facing Ingress URL (http://crowdvision.local/sensor/).
- Deploying to production requires zero changes — the simulators simply point at a different URL.

This is the same reasoning behind not running load-test tooling inside a production cluster: the test tool is not the system under test.

-----

Running the Simulators

Both simulator images are built and pushed to GHCR as part of the CD pipeline. To run them locally:

	# Sensor simulator (people count + temperature):
	docker run -p 3001:3000 ghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-simulator:latest
	# Air quality simulator:
	docker run -p 3002:3000 ghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-aq-simulator:latest

Alternatively, both simulators are included in the Docker Compose stack and start automatically with just dev (unless excluded):

	just dev exclude="simulator"   # skip both simulators
	just dev exclude="aq"          # skip only the AQ simulator

To stop a standalone container:

	docker stop <container-id>

-----

How Connections Work

The simulator sends data to CrowdVision’s /sensor/ endpoints via HTTP POST. When the user starts the simulator from the dashboard UI, the Vue client:

1. Reads window.location.origin to determine the current application’s base URL (e.g., http://localhost).
2. Appends /sensor/ to form the target URL (e.g., http://localhost/sensor/).
3. POSTs this target URL to the simulator’s /control/start endpoint, along with the building ID and room IDs.
4. The simulator stores this URL and begins firing data at it every 10 seconds.



-----

The host.docker.internal Resolution

This is the most important implementation detail to understand when running the simulator against a local k3d cluster.

The problem: When the Vue frontend runs in a browser, window.location.origin returns http://localhost. The simulator receives this as its target URL. But the simulator is a Docker container — from inside a Docker container, localhost resolves to 127.0.0.1 of the container itself, not your laptop. The k3d load balancer is not listening inside the simulator container, so every fetch() call fails with ECONNREFUSED.

The solution: Docker Desktop (Windows and macOS) provides a special hostname host.docker.internal that always resolves to the host machine from inside any container. The simulator automatically rewrites localhost and 127.0.0.1 to host.docker.internal before making any outbound request:

	// simulatorService.ts — startOrAdd()
	if (parsedUrl.includes("localhost") || parsedUrl.includes("127.0.0.1")) {
	  parsedUrl = parsedUrl
	    .replace(/localhost/g, "host.docker.internal")
	    .replace(/127\.0\.0\.1/g, "host.docker.internal");
	}

The rewrite is transparent to the user — they type http://localhost:3001 as the simulator URL in the dashboard, and the simulator resolves the routing correctly.

Note
-----
Linux Docker users: host.docker.internal is not automatically available on Linux. Start the simulator with --add-host host.docker.internal:host-gateway to enable it:

docker run --add-host host.docker.internal:host-gateway -p 3001:3000 ghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-simulator:latest

Note
-----
Remote VPS deployment: When the application is deployed on a VPS, the target URL the frontend sends will be https://yourdomain.com/sensor — a fully qualified hostname with no localhost in it. The rewrite block never runs, and the simulator sends directly to the public URL. No configuration changes are required.

-----

Simulator Control API

The simulator exposes three HTTP endpoints that the Vue dashboard calls directly (bypassing the cluster Ingress entirely):

POSTPOSTGETMethod/control/start/control/stop/control/status?buildingId=XEndpoint{ buildingId, roomIds, targetUrl }{ buildingId }—BodyStarts sending simulated data for the given building. If the building is already registered, it replaces it (idempotent).Stops sending data for the given building. If no buildings remain active, the simulator loop halts entirely.Returns { isRunning: boolean } indicating whether the given building is currently being simulated.Description

The simulator can manage multiple buildings simultaneously. Each building sends signals for all its rooms independently.

-----

Simulation Parameters

The following values are currently fixed in simulatorService.ts. They can be adjusted by editing the service and rebuilding the image:

delaypeopleCountRangetemperatureRangeParameter10000 ms[0, 50][18.0, 30.0]DefaultInterval between signal batches (10 seconds).Random headcount generated per room per tick.Random temperature (°C) generated per room per tick. Rounded to 2 decimal places.Description

-----

Connecting the Simulator from the Dashboard

From the Vue dashboard (Graph view), a gear icon opens the simulator configuration modal. Enter the simulator’s base URL (e.g., http://localhost:3001) and click Save. The dashboard remembers this URL in localStorage, so you only need to enter it once per browser.

Once configured:

- Click Start simulator to begin sending data. The button turns green with an animated indicator while active.
- Click Stop simulator to halt data transmission for the current building.
- The dashboard polls /control/status every time the building selection changes to keep the button state in sync with the actual simulator state.
