entries.title: Contributing & Golden Rules
entries.content:
Contributing & Golden Rules

This page is the contract for working in the CrowdVision repository: how the monorepo is organised, the architectural rules contributions must respect, and the day-to-day workflow from clone to merged pull request. The related conventions are detailed on their own pages: Naming Conventions, Commits & Semantic Release, and CI/CD Pipeline.

-----

The polyglot monorepo

CrowdVision is a polyglot monorepo: a Vue client and five Node services in TypeScript, a Python agent-service, and a Rust contracts-service, orchestrated by moon (task caching and affected-detection) behind a root Justfile.

Two structural facts shape everything you do:

- There is no root package.json and no npm workspaces. Each Node service installs its own node_modules. Shared JavaScript dev tooling (semantic-release, commitlint, the bump script) lives in tooling/; the shared ESLint flat config is the @crowdvision/eslint-config package at tooling/eslint-config/.
- Every package is registered once in .moon/workspace.yml, and the toolchain derives everything else from it. See The Toolchain.

Dependencies are not hoisted
-----
When you add a dependency, install it inside that service’s directory (cd server/auth-service && npm install <pkg>). Because that npm install resolves the lockfile for your operating system, regenerate it for Linux before committing — just clean-install, or per-service npm install --prefix server/auth-service --package-lock-only --cpu=x64 --os=linux — otherwise CI’s npm ci fails on missing Linux optional packages. Run just deps-check before pushing.

-----

Architectural golden rules

These rules preserve the boundaries the architecture depends on. A contribution that violates one will not be accepted, even if it passes CI.

Respect the database-per-service boundaryKeep services statelessOne bounded context per serviceKeep the frontend lightweightTest what you changeRoute every tool through miseRuleA service never opens a connection to another service’s database. If a service needs data it does not own, it reads it from the verified identity token, asks the owning service over its API, or reacts to a broker event. Never configure a cross-database Mongoose connection.Hold no session state in a service; all caller authority travels in the signed token, so any instance can serve any request. Durable state belongs only in databases and the broker.A service realises exactly one context from the Domain model. Logic that belongs to another context goes in that context’s service, reached through its contract.Avoid adding a global store (Pinia/Vuex) unless genuinely necessary; prefer composables and local state.Every backend service has a __tests__ directory. New behaviour must be covered, and just test must pass before you open a pull request.Never rely on the ambient PATH. Use just recipes (which wrap mise exec --) or mise exec -- <tool> directly. Register any new package in .moon/workspace.yml.What it means in practice

-----

The contribution workflow



1. Branch from master using the branch-naming convention, see Naming Conventions.
2. Bootstrap the repository once with just install (which runs mise install first).
3. Develop against the local stack with just dev, see Docker Compose Orchestration.
4. Format and lint with just lint-fix, then verify with just lint-affected.
5. Test with just test-affected (or just test for the full suite).
6. Verify dependencies with just deps-check (lockfile sync) and just audit (vulnerabilities) — these mirror the ci-deps and ci-audit gates.
7. Commit using Conventional Commits, see Commits & Semantic Release.
8. Open a pull request to master. The single required check is ci-gate / ci-passed.

-----

Pre-push checklist

Running the same gates locally that CI runs avoids a slow round-trip through a failed pipeline.

	just lint-affected   # mirrors ci-lint
	just test-affected   # mirrors the per-service ci-* test legs
	just deps-check      # mirrors ci-deps  (npm ci / uv sync --locked / cargo check)
	just audit           # mirrors ci-audit (npm / uv / cargo audit)

The commit message is part of the build
-----
ci-commitlint rejects any commit message that does not follow Conventional Commits, and semantic-release derives the next version directly from those messages. A malformed message blocks the pull request. See Commits & Semantic Release.

-----

Why bump-versions.mjs instead of Lerna

Release-time version bumps across every manifest are applied by tooling/bump-versions.mjs, a plain Node fs script, rather than Lerna.

Lerna 9 dropped its own package-discovery field and now delegates entirely to npm workspaces. npm workspaces cannot point upward with ../ — workspace paths must be strict descendants of the directory owning the package.json. Because tooling/ sits inside the repository rather than above the services, a tooling/package.json declaring "workspaces": ["../client", "../server/*"] is rejected by npm outright. bump-versions.mjs sidesteps the constraint entirely: it opens each manifest by relative path and writes the new version — no workspaces, no Lerna discovery, nothing beyond Node itself.

entries.title: Naming Conventions
entries.content:
Naming Conventions

Consistent naming lets a reader infer an artefact’s role from its name alone. CrowdVision spans several languages and layers, so the conventions are defined per layer below. They are enforced partly by ESLint and partly by review; treat them as mandatory.

-----

Files and directories

Every Node service follows the same internal layout, so the file you want is always in the same place.

src/router.tssrc/controller/src/services/src/models/src/middlewares/src/config/__tests__/PathRoute declarations.HTTP adapters (one file per concern).Business logic.Schemas and shared types.Cross-cutting middleware.Environment accessors and client setup.The service’s test suite.Holds

-----

Backend — Node.js / TypeScript

Controller / service filesModel filesExported model and interfaceFunctions and variablesConstantsDatabase collectionsArtefactcamelCase, layer as suffix where it adds claritycamelCase file namePascalCase; interfaces prefixed IcamelCaseUPPER_SNAKE_CASEplural, lowercaseConventionbuildings.ts, notificationService.tsaccount.ts, buildingThreshold.tsexport interface IAccount, export const Account = model(...)generateStandardToken, allowedColumnsROLE_WEIGHTS, COOKIE_NAMEaccounts, buildingsRecommendedtwin_ctrl.ts, Notification.tsAccount.ts, building_threshold.tsexport const account, export interface AccountGenerateToken, allowed_columnsroleWeightsAccount, buildingAvoid

-----

Frontend — Vue 3 / TresJS

ComponentsViews / pages (top-level routes)ComposablesUtilities / helpersArtefactPascalCase, multi-word (to avoid clashing with HTML elements), file and tagPascalCase with a View suffixcamelCase, prefixed usecamelCaseConventionBuildingCard.vue, NotificationDropdown.vue, LeftMenu.vueDashboardView.vue, AdministrationView.vueuseBuildingModel.ts, useAuth.ts, useSceneControls.tsmetrics.ts, formatTimestamp.tsRecommendedcard.vue, header.vueDashboard.vuebuildingModel.ts, getAuth.tsMetrics.tsAvoid

-----

Rust — contracts-service

The contracts-service follows idiomatic Rust style.

Modules and filesFunctionsTypes (structs, enums)ConstantsArtefactsnake_casesnake_casePascalCaseSCREAMING_SNAKE_CASEConventiontunnel.rs, discovery.rsstart_telemetry_tunnel, metric_is_allowedAppState, PreferenceDocumentRAW_CHANNEL, DEFAULT_COLUMNSExample

-----

Python — agent-service

The agent-service follows PEP 8: snake_case for modules, functions, and variables; PascalCase for classes; SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE for constants. (The agent is maintained separately; these conventions are listed for completeness.)

-----

Branches

Branch names mirror the commit types, so the branch announces its intent.

	<type>/<short-kebab-description>

featfixchoredocsrefactorTypefeat/private-domainsfix/jwt-expiry-off-by-onechore/bump-dependenciesdocs/sensor-api-referencerefactor/extract-token-serviceExample

-----

Commit scopes

Conventional-commit scopes use the service or area name, so history can be filtered by component.

	feat(twin): add per-room colour overrides
	fix(auth): reject expired refresh tokens
	docs(sensor): document the ingestion fast path
	chore(ci): pin the kubeconform version

Common scopes: auth, twin, sensor, notification, socket, contracts, client, agent, ci, docs, k8s. The full commit format is covered in Commits & Semantic Release.

-----

Other identifiers

Environment variablesDocker imagesWorkflow filesDocumentation pagesArtefactUPPER_SNAKE_CASEcrowdvision-<service> under the org namespace<category>-<name>.yml (ci-, cd-, maint-)kebab-case .qdConventionJWT_SECRET, SENSOR_SERVICE_URLghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-authci-auth.yml, cd-release.yml, maint-cleanup.ymlbuilding-data.qd, naming-conventions.qdExample

entries.title: Commits & Semantic Release
entries.content:
Commits & Semantic Release

CrowdVision automates versioning, changelog generation, and releases with semantic-release. Version numbers are not chosen by hand — they are derived from commit messages. This makes the commit message a functional input to the build, which is why its format is enforced.

-----

Conventional Commits

Every commit message must follow the Conventional Commits specification:

	<type>(<optional scope>): <subject>
	<optional body>
	<optional footer(s)>

- type — the kind of change (table below).
- scope — the affected component, e.g. auth, twin, ci (see Naming Conventions.
- subject — a concise, imperative description (“add”, not “added”).

The convention is enforced on every pull request by the ci-commitlint workflow, which runs @commitlint/config-conventional (configured in tooling/commitlint.config.js). A non-conforming message blocks the merge.

-----

Type to release impact

The type determines how the next version is computed.

featfixdocs, chore, refactor, test, ci, style, perf, buildBreaking changeTypefeat(twin): add per-room colour overridesfix(auth): reject expired refresh tokensdocs(sensor): document the ingestion fast pathfeat(auth)!: remove the /auth/v1 endpointsExampleMinor bump (1.1.0 → 1.2.0).Patch bump (1.1.0 → 1.1.1).No release.Major bump (1.1.0 → 2.0.0).Release impact

A breaking change is signalled either by a ! after the type/scope, or by a BREAKING CHANGE: footer:

	feat(auth)!: remove the deprecated /auth/v1 endpoints
	BREAKING CHANGE: clients must migrate to /auth. The v1 routes are gone.

Only feat and fix produce a release
-----
Commits typed chore, docs, ci, test, refactor, etc. are safe to merge freely — they never trigger a version bump. Only feat (minor) and fix (patch) do, and a breaking change escalates to major.

-----

The release pipeline

Releases are fully automated. On every push to master, the cd-release workflow installs tooling/ and runs npx semantic-release from there. The behaviour is configured in tooling/.releaserc.



Step by step, semantic-release:

1. Analyses commits since the last Git tag and computes the next version from their types.
2. Generates release notes grouped by type.
3. Updates the changelog — @semantic-release/changelog writes to the repo-root ../CHANGELOG.md (paths are relative to tooling/).
4. Bumps every manifest — @semantic-release/exec runs node bump-versions.mjs <version>, which writes the new version into each service package.json, server/contracts-service/Cargo.toml, and server/agent-service/pyproject.toml.
5. Commits and tags — @semantic-release/git commits the changelog and all bumped manifests with the message chore(release): <version> [skip ci] and creates the tag.
6. Publishes a GitHub Release with the generated notes.

The release commit carries [skip ci] so it does not re-trigger the pipeline, and the workflow uses a concurrency guard so two pushes cannot release at once.

Assets committed by the release

	../CHANGELOG.md
	../client/package.json            + package-lock.json
	../server/*/package.json          + package-lock.json
	../server/contracts-service/Cargo.toml
	../server/agent-service/pyproject.toml

This is the full set of files the version number lives in, kept in lockstep by bump-versions.mjs. For why a custom script is used instead of Lerna, see Contributing & Golden Rules.

-----

Worked example

A branch merges three commits since the last v1.4.2 tag:

	fix(sensor): clamp negative people counts to zero
	feat(twin): support multi-floor buildings
	docs(twin): note the floor-selection control

semantic-release sees a feat (the highest-ranked change), so the next version is a minor bump: v1.5.0. It writes the changelog, bumps every manifest to 1.5.0, tags v1.5.0, and publishes the release. The docs commit contributes a changelog line but does not affect the version. In the same push, cd-registry builds and pushes the Docker images — see CI/CD Pipeline.

entries.title: CI/CD Pipeline
entries.content:
CI/CD Pipeline

CrowdVision uses a fully automated CI/CD pipeline on GitHub Actions. Every pull request to master is gated by a suite of checks — builds, tests, linting, security audits, Docker and Kubernetes validation, and a guard that refuses to let any unreviewed path merge unchecked. Merging to master automatically triggers a versioned release and pushes Docker images to GHCR. This page documents the workflows; the commit format and release mechanics that drive them are covered in Commits & Semantic Release.

-----

Workflow naming

All workflow files live in .github/workflows/ and use a three-prefix convention that mirrors their purpose.

ci-*cd-*maint-*PrefixContinuous integration checksContinuous deliveryMaintenanceCategoryOn every pull request to masterOn every push to masterWeekly schedule or manual dispatchWhen it runs

-----

The CI gate

Every pull request must pass CI Gate / CI Passed — the single aggregated check that covers all the fast CI. The gate (ci-gate.yml) detects what changed, fans out to the relevant checks in parallel, and reports one result. The only other check worth requiring in branch protection is CodeQL (the Static security analysis section below), which runs as a separate workflow because it needs wider permissions than the gate’s read-only token.



How the gate decides

The changes job runs dorny/paths-filter once and emits a boolean per area. Every downstream leg is guarded by an if: on those booleans, so a leg runs only when a path it owns actually changed. Service legs and the cross-cutting checks are called as reusable workflows (uses: ./.github/workflows/ci-*.yml); the gate itself stays a thin orchestrator — detect, fan out, guard, aggregate.

Unaffected legs are skipped, not failed
-----
A leg whose paths did not change resolves as skipped, which the aggregator counts as a pass. This is what keeps the pipeline fast — a one-line change to one service does not rebuild the world.

The final ci-passed job depends on every leg and runs with if: always(), so it reports even when a leg was skipped. Its verdict logic is deliberately strict:

- Whitelist, not blacklist. Each leg’s result must be success or skipped. Anything else — failure, cancelled, or any future result value — fails the gate. A blacklist that only checked for failure would let an unknown state slip through green.
- The fan-out root must have succeeded. If the changes job itself failed or was cancelled, every leg was skipped without ever being evaluated — a green gate there would be a false pass. So ci-passed hard-fails unless changes ended in success.

-----

Service CI workflows

Each service has a dedicated reusable workflow that the gate calls when that service’s directory (or its own workflow file) changes. Each installs dependencies, lints, builds, runs tests, and audits its dependencies.

ci-auth.ymlci-chat.ymlci-twin.ymlci-sensor.ymlci-socket.ymlci-notification.ymlci-contracts.ymlci-client.ymlci-agent.ymlWorkflowserver/auth-serviceserver/chat-serviceserver/twin-serviceserver/sensor-serviceserver/socket-serviceserver/notification-serviceserver/contracts-serviceclientserver/agent-serviceServiceNode.js 24 / JestNode.js 24 / JestNode.js 24 / JestNode.js 24 / JestNode.js 24 / JestNode.js 24 / JestRust stable / cargo testNode.js 24 / VitestPython 3.12 / pytestLanguage / test runner

The Node services share the same shape: npm ci, install the shared tooling/eslint-config/, lint, build, test, then audit. The client splits into a quality job (lint, type-check, audit) and a test job that shards Vitest across three parallel runners. The contracts service runs cargo fmt --check and cargo clippy -- -D warnings before cargo test, then a rustsec/audit-check. The agent runs ruff check, ruff format --check, and pyright in a quality job, with pytest (unit + integration) in a separate tests job.

The dependency audit is split into two steps
-----
Every Node service runs the audit twice. npm audit --audit-level=high --omit=dev is blocking — a fixable high/critical vulnerability in a production dependency fails the build. npm audit --audit-level=high (the full tree, including dev deps) runs straight after with continue-on-error: true, so dev-only advisories are surfaced in the log without blocking a merge. The split keeps the gate honest about what ships to users while still reporting everything.

-----

Simulator CI

The two device simulators ship as GHCR images but are not application services, so they get a dedicated ci-simulators.yml — one job each, gated on simulators/.

sensor-simulatoraq-simulatorJobNode.js 24 / TypeScriptPython 3.12RuntimeESLint (shared config) + tsc build. No test suite exists yet, so there is no test step — a bare Jest with zero tests would fail.py_compile parses every module (smoke.py is not executed — it performs network I/O), then ruff check runs with default rules, which catch real defects such as undefined names and unused imports.Checks

Both were previously ungated despite shipping images; until this workflow existed they were held by the unmapped-path tripwire below.

-----

Cross-cutting checks

These legs validate properties that span the codebase rather than a single service. They are gated by their own path filters and join the same ci-passed aggregator.

ci-docker.ymlci-k8s.ymlworkflows-lintci-infra.ymlci-dependency-review.ymlLegThe docker leg. For each changed service that ships an image: hadolint on its Dockerfile, then a push: false Docker build (a PR-time build check — cd-registry does the real publish on master). The gha layer cache is scoped per image so services reuse their own layers without evicting each other.The k8s leg. Validates k8s/ with kubeconform (schema, against Kubernetes 1.32.0, -strict), pluto (deprecated/removed API versions), and kube-score (best-practice scoring, with a curated set of ignored tests).A job defined inline in ci-gate.yml that validates the workflow files themselves whenever anything under the .github/ tree changes. actionlint (digest-pinned container) catches malformed expressions and invalid action inputs; shellcheck is bundled but disabled (-shellcheck=) until its run:-block findings are triaged. zizmor (pinned version, run --offline --min-severity medium) then audits for security smells actionlint misses: template injection in run: blocks, credential persistence (it is why every read-only checkout sets persist-credentials: false), over-broad token permissions, and dangerous triggers.The infra leg. Cheap, real validation for repo scripts and release tooling whenever scripts/ or tooling/ change: node --check parses every tracked .mjs/.js, bash -n checks every shell script, and npm ci in tooling/ proves the release-tooling lockfile is still in sync. Keeps routine script edits green automatically instead of tripping the guard below.The dependency-review leg. On any PR that changes a dependency manifest or lockfile, it fails if the PR introduces a dependency carrying a known high/critical advisory (fail-on-severity: high). It complements the per-service audit steps: those see the merged lockfile state, whereas this diffs exactly what the PR adds, catching a vulnerable package at the moment it is introduced. Requires the repository’s dependency graph (on by default for public repos).What it validates

Every third-party action is pinned to a commit SHA
-----
Each uses: across every workflow references a full 40-character commit SHA (with the human-readable version kept as a trailing comment, e.g. # v6), never a moving tag like @v4. A tag can be silently repointed at malicious code by whoever controls the action; a commit SHA cannot. The repository setting Settings → Actions → “Require actions to be pinned to a full-length commit SHA” enforces this, so no future workflow can reintroduce an unpinned action. Local reusable workflows (uses: ./.github/workflows/…) are exempt — they live in this repo. Bumping a version means resolving the new tag to its SHA (git ls-remote) and updating both the SHA and the comment.

-----

The unmapped-path tripwire

A path-filtered pipeline has one structural blind spot: a path that no filter knows about runs no checks, and “no checks” reports green. A pull request touching only such a path would sail through the gate having validated nothing. The guard-unmapped job closes that hole.

It is driven by an inverted filter — a catch-all minus everything already accounted for:

	unmapped:
	  - '**'                  # suspect everything…
	  - '!server/**'          # …then subtract every path that has a real bucket
	  - '!client/**'
	  - '!k8s/**'
	  - '!.github/**'
	  - '!scripts/**'
	  - '!tooling/**'
	  - '!simulators/**'
	  - '!documentation/**'   # …and the inert content that needs no checks
	  - '!landing-page/**'
	  - '!output/**'
	  - '!**/*.md'
	  - '!LICENSE'

Whatever survives that subtraction is, by definition, a path nobody mapped. When the filter catches something, guard-unmapped runs, lists the offending files (via list-files: shell), and fails — turning the gate red and forcing a conscious decision. The remedy is one line in the filter: either map the path to a real check, or add it to the inert exclusions if it genuinely carries no risk.

Fail closed, by design
-----
Today the survivors include root config (Caddyfile, docker-compose.yml, prometheus.yml, .mise.toml, .trivyignore…), .moon/ task definitions, and seed.py. The simulators used to live here too — they were the motivating example of “ships an artifact but nothing gates it” — until ci-simulators.yml gave them a real bucket. On a solo repository where no second reviewer exists, this guard is the mechanical substitute for “a human must look at this change.”

-----

CD workflows

cd-release

Triggered on every push to master. Runs semantic-release from tooling/: it reads commits since the last tag, computes the next version, updates CHANGELOG.md, bumps every manifest, tags the repository, and publishes a GitHub Release. A concurrency guard prevents duplicate runs. The full mechanics are in Commits & Semantic Release.

cd-registry

Triggered on push to master when source changes under server/**, simulators/**, client/**, or the workflow file itself. It publishes Docker images to GHCR tagged both :latest and :<git-sha>.

The publish is gated by a vulnerability scan that runs before anything reaches the registry. Each image is built into the runner’s local daemon (push: false, load: true), scanned by Trivy, and pushed only if the scan passes — so a vulnerable image is never published. The push step rebuilds from the still-warm gha cache, so it costs no recompilation.

Build → scan → push, not push → scan
-----
Scanning after publishing would mean a flagged image is already live by the time the job fails. Ordering the scan as a gate before the push is the whole point. aquasecurity/trivy-action is pinned to a commit digest (not the moving @master) to remove that supply-chain risk.

Selective builds: only changed services are pushed

The workflow does not rebuild every image on every push. A changes job detects which services changed and emits a dynamic build matrix; a build-and-push job (needs: changes) then builds only the selected images, and is skipped entirely when nothing matches. Two cases force a full rebuild: a change to cd-registry.yml itself, and a manual workflow_dispatch with service: all (or a comma-separated list to target specific images).

Scan scope

The Trivy scan is deliberately scoped to OS packages only (vuln-type: os). Application dependencies are not re-scanned here because they are already gated pre-merge by the per-service audits. The division of labour is: the service CI workflows own language dependencies at PR time; cd-registry Trivy owns the base-OS layer at push time — the part the dependency audits cannot see.

To keep the OS scan green, every production image upgrades its base-OS packages at build time (apk upgrade on Alpine images, apt-get upgrade on the Debian-based contracts, agent, and aq-simulator images). The Debian upgrade lines carry # hadolint ignore=DL3005. Any residual fixable CVE that cannot be patched in-build is waived in the repo-root .trivyignore, where each entry must carry a justifying comment. (A change to .trivyignore itself trips the unmapped-path tripwire, so a CVE waiver can never be slipped in silently.)

-----

Static security analysis (CodeQL)

ci-codeql.yml runs GitHub’s SAST for javascript-typescript, python, and rust with the security-extended query suite. It runs automatically on every pull request to master, on every push to master (to refresh the Security tab’s default-branch baseline), and on a weekly schedule (Monday 03:00 UTC) to catch CVEs newly disclosed against unchanged code. Manual workflow_dispatch is retained for on-demand re-scans, and a concurrency guard cancels a scan superseded by a newer commit.

All three languages use build-mode: none — CodeQL extracts from source without a compile. For javascript-typescript and python that is the natural mode; for rust (contracts-service) it means no Rust toolchain or mold-linker setup is needed, and the crate is found under server/contracts-service without a root Cargo.toml.

It runs as a standalone workflow rather than a leg of the gate, on purpose: CodeQL needs security-events: write to upload results, whereas the gate’s token is deliberately read-only, and a reusable workflow cannot hold more permission than its caller. Keeping CodeQL separate preserves the gate’s least-privilege token. Add CodeQL as a second required status check in branch protection (alongside CI Gate / CI Passed) to block merges on new high-severity findings.

-----

Maintenance

maint-cleanup runs every Sunday at 02:00 UTC (or on manual dispatch). It removes old package versions from GHCR, keeping a minimum of three versions per image and protecting the :latest tag, so the registry does not grow unbounded.

-----

Dependency updates

Dependency upgrades are made manually (there is no Dependabot). Bump the version in the relevant manifest, then verify and regenerate locks locally before opening a pull request:

	just deps-check   # every lockfile in sync (npm ci / uv sync --locked / cargo check)
	just audit        # no known vulnerabilities (npm / uv / cargo audit)

Both checks also run inside the per-service CI workflows on every pull request, so a drifted or vulnerable dependency blocks the merge regardless.

-----

Docker image registry

All images are hosted at ghcr.io/nickghignatti/ and tagged both :latest and :<git-sha>. The Kubernetes deployment manifests reference :latest.

auth-servicechat-servicetwin-servicesensor-servicenotification-servicesocket-servicecontracts-serviceagent-serviceclientsensor-simulatoraq-simulatorServiceghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-authghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-chatghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-twinghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-sensorghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-notificationghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-socketghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-contractsghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-agentghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-clientghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-simulatorghcr.io/nickghignatti/crowdvision-aq-simulatorGHCR image

-----

Reproducing the gates locally

Run the same checks CI runs before pushing, to avoid a slow round-trip through a failed pipeline:

	just test            # all unit tests (moon-cached); or just test-affected
	just lint-affected   # ESLint / Ruff on changed projects
	just deps-check      # lockfile sync
	just audit           # vulnerability audit

Per-service tests are available as just test-auth, just test-chat, just test-twin, just test-sensor, just test-notification, just test-socket, just test-client, and just test-agent. Full backend integration tests run via just test-integration (it composes the full stack — see Docker Compose Orchestration.

headings.text: Selective builds: only changed services are pushed

entries.title: Adding an Agent Tool
entries.content:
Adding an Agent Tool

A “tool” is a capability the language model can call — search docs, fetch a building, send a
notification. For how the agent uses tools at runtime, see Agent Service.

The agent loop (app/agent/loop.py) discovers tools through a REGISTRY, validates the
model’s JSON arguments through your Pydantic Args model, calls run, and feeds the result
back into the conversation. The whole contract is structural: a tool is any object that
exposes the right attributes, so you never inherit from a base class. This guide walks through
adding one end-to-end.

-----

1. Pick The Shape

Before writing code, answer four questions:

1. What does the tool do, in one sentence? This becomes the description the model reads.
2. What inputs does it need? Each input becomes a field on the Args Pydantic model.
3. What does it return? Keep payloads small — the result is fed back into the model’s
	context as JSON, where it costs tokens and can distract the model. Project away fields it
	does not need.
4. Read-only or write? The current agent has no confirmation flow for write actions. Keep
	tools read-only unless authorization, confirmation, audit logging, and retry behavior are
	explicitly designed.

Warning
-----
The current twin tools do not forward the caller JWT or enforce ToolContext
authorization. Do not copy that behavior for sensitive tools: authorize against ctx.user,
or call a downstream endpoint that enforces the caller’s identity.

-----

2. Where The File Lives

- Single tool, focused domain → a new file in app/agent/tools/<name>.py.
- Cluster of related tools sharing a backend → group them in one file (see
	app/agent/tools/twin.py — four tools sharing the same twin-service backend and client
	factory).

Use # ─── tool_name ──... divider comments inside multi-tool files to keep them scannable.

-----

3. Write The Args Model

	from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
	class GetRoomArgs(BaseModel):
	    building_id: str = Field(description="Building containing the room.")
	    room_id: str = Field(description="Id of the room within the building.")

The reason every field is a Pydantic field with a description is that the agent turns this
class into a JSON Schema (Args.model_json_schema()) and hands it to the model — so this class
is the model-facing contract, not just internal validation.

- Every field gets a Field(description=...). The model sees the complete generated JSON
	Schema — argument names, types, required fields, defaults, enums, constraints, and
	descriptions. Descriptions explain the semantics those structural fields cannot express, so
	vague descriptions still cause vague tool calls.
- Use the right primitive (str, int, bool, Literal[...], enums). Pydantic enforces
	it and the model sees it in the schema.
- Add constraints where they help: Field(default=5, ge=1, le=10) (see search_docs.py).
- Keep names plain. building_id, not bldgIdentifier — the model produces these names
	from the schema.
- Resist making everything optional. Required-by-default produces fewer “I forgot to pass
	that” tool errors.

-----

4. Write The Tool Class

	from app.agent.tools.base import ToolContext, ToolResult
	class GetRoomTool:
	    name = "get_room"
	    description = (
	        "Fetch a single room's full state: capacity, current occupancy, temperature, "
	        "dimensions, position. Use after locating the room id via list_rooms."
	    )
	    Args = GetRoomArgs
	    async def run(self, args: GetRoomArgs, ctx: ToolContext) -> ToolResult:
	        ...

namedescriptionArgsrunAttributeStable identifier the model emits in tool_calls. Snake_case, unique.What the tool does and when to use it versus alternatives. See below.The Pydantic class from step 3; the registry exposes it via model_json_schema().Async method, signature (self, args: <YourArgs>, ctx: ToolContext) -> ToolResult.Purpose

Writing A Good description

The model uses this to choose between tools, so state what it does and when to pick it
over neighbors. Compare:

- ❌ “Get a room.” — too thin; the model will guess.
- ✅ “Fetch a single room’s full state… Use after locating the room id via list_rooms.” —
	names the precondition and steers the model away from calling with a guessed id.

For a tool with a sibling that does something close (search_docs versus the twin tools),
explicitly say what it is not for — see SearchDocsTool in search_docs.py.

About The Generic Protocol

Tool in app/agent/tools/base.py is a Protocol[ArgsT] — generic over your Args type. You
do not inherit from it: Python’s structural typing means that as long as your class has
name, description, Args, and a matching run, it already satisfies Tool[YourArgs]. The
type variable is what lets the type checker see that run(args: GetRoomArgs, ...) is a valid
implementation rather than a signature violation.

Note
-----
Do not widen your run parameter to BaseModel to “match the protocol” — that defeats the
point and loses type safety. Keep it narrow.

-----

5. Implement run

	async def run(self, args: GetRoomArgs, ctx: ToolContext) -> ToolResult:
	    async with _client() as c:
	        r = await c.get(f"/building/{args.building_id}")
	    if r.status_code == 404:
	        return ToolResult(content=f"building {args.building_id} not found", is_error=True)
	    if r.status_code >= 400:
	        return ToolResult(content=f"twin-service error {r.status_code}: {r.text}", is_error=True)
	    ...
	    return ToolResult(content=room)

- Return errors as data, don’t raise. Use ToolResult(content=<message>, is_error=True).
	The loop catches uncaught exceptions anyway, but a clean is_error message is sent back to
	the model so it can recover (try a different id, ask the user). Raising is for genuine bugs.
- Use ctx: ctx.session is an async SQLAlchemy session; ctx.user is the authenticated
	AuthUser (use it to enforce per-tool access control before touching sensitive data);
	ctx.citations accumulates citation candidates — prefer returning new ones via
	ToolResult(citations=[...]), which the loop appends (see SearchDocsTool).
- Project the response. When wrapping an HTTP call, return only the fields the model needs.
- Treat tool results as untrusted data. Downstream responses and retrieved documents may
	contain instruction-like text. Return structured, minimized payloads, and never rely on the
	model to enforce authorization or decide whether sensitive data should be exposed.
- No prints, use structlog: from app.logging import get_logger; log = get_logger(__name__).
- No per-call global state. Module-level singletons (a shared httpx.AsyncClient, a
	retriever) are fine; per-call state belongs in ctx or local variables.

-----

6. Register The Tool

Open app/agent/tools/__init__.py and add two lines:

	from app.agent.tools.twin import GetRoomTool
	...
	REGISTRY.register(GetRoomTool())

Order does not matter. Duplicate names raise at startup — that is intentional, to catch
copy-paste collisions early.

-----

7. Test It

Three layers, lightest first:

- Args model — if you put non-trivial validation on the args (regex, ranges, custom
	validators), unit-test it under tests/unit/. Skip it for trivial fields.
- run with a fake backend — inject a fake client/session and assert on the ToolResult.
	Mock the boundary (httpx, the SQLAlchemy session), not the tool itself.
- Through the agent loop — the integration suite has no reusable scripted LLMClient yet,
	so for now test run at the backend boundary and smoke-test dispatch through /ask. When a
	reusable fake model is added, assert the full tool-call and recovery sequence through
	Agent.answer.

-----

8. Run The Checks

	npm run lint       # ruff + pyright, must be clean
	npm run test       # unit + integration

The checks catch many common mistakes, including invalid return annotations and ordinary type
errors inside run. The generic Tool[ArgsT] protocol documents the intended relationship
between Args and the run parameter, but registration currently stores tools as Tool[Any];
do not rely on Pyright alone to catch every mismatch between those two attributes. Exercise
schema generation and dispatch in tests for non-trivial tools.

-----

Quick Checklist

- File in app/agent/tools/<name>.py (or grouped sensibly with siblings).
- Args is a BaseModel; every field has a Field(description=...).
- Class has name (snake_case, unique), description (with when to use), Args, async run.
- run(args: <YourArgs>, ctx: ToolContext) -> ToolResult — narrow types, no widening to BaseModel.
- Errors returned as ToolResult(..., is_error=True), not raised.
- Sensitive operations explicitly authorize ctx.user; write tools have confirmation and audit behavior.
- Response projected to the minimum the model needs.
- Tool results treated as untrusted data; authorization is enforced before returning data.
- Registered in app/agent/tools/__init__.py.
- npm run lint and npm run test pass.
